key_results.evidence: Eqs.(14)-(21); text on p.5: 'for the limit case where g_AD=0, F_lm=delta_lm so T_00=T_11=H_AD recovers the standard ET; T_01=hbar*omega_c*t_AD, T_10=-hbar*omega_c*t_AD represents ET coupled to the cavity photon.'

key_results.claim: The collective interaction is built from the N-1 non-reacting molecules; the single reacting (active) molecule j=1 experiences coupling diluted as 1/sqrt(N), so the per-molecule effect decreases with N (resolves the 'polariton large-N problem').
key_results.evidence: Polariton states Eqs.(27),(28),(33),(34) use 1/sqrt(N-1) collective sum over j=2..N; Discussion: 'the reacting molecule is excluded from the formation of polariton', effect on single molecule decreases with N.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Schematic potential energy surfaces in the active-mode coordinate R_1 for absorption (I) and emission (II). Left panels: bare {D}m (black) and {A_1}m (blue) diabatic parabolas; for (I) {D}1 & {A_1}0 cross barrier-less-ish (ET coupled to photon absorption); for (II) {D}0 & {A_1}1. Right panels: corresponding N-molecule dressed picture showing upper/lower polariton PES P_pm (red) and dark-state manifold DS (grey dashed). Illustrates that Rabi splitting scales as sqrt(N-1) and the polariton PES shifts toward barrier-less.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: active-mode coordinate R_1 (arb. units); y: energy (eV). Parabolas V_D(R)=E_D+1/2 omega_v^2 R^2 and V_A(R)=E_A+lambda_v R + 1/2 omega_v^2 R^2, shifted by m*hbar*omega_c; polariton curves at Omega_pm.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Relative rate constants (ET and PR) vs N for (I) absorption and (II) emission. ET rates of path (b) (red solid: P_pm -> {A_1}0 abs / P_pm -> {A_1}1 emis), path (c) (blue solid: P_pm -> {A_1}1 abs / P_pm -> {A_1}0 emis), and PR rates (grey dashed) — all plotted relative to path (a) (k_{{D}0->{A_1}0} for I, k_{{D}1->{A_1}1} for II). Non-monotonic curves P_+ -> {A_1}1 (I) and P_- -> {A_1}0 (II) peak at black dotted vertical line = N_max. Beyond N_max ET and PR both decrease; PR dominates at large N.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: Number of Molecules N, log scale 10^0 to 10^5; y: Relative Rate Constant, log scale 10^{-8} to 10^8. Vertical dotted line at N_max (~order 10^2-10^3 for absorption, ~10^3-10^4 for emission).

figures_to_emulate.shows: Cavity-induced quantum yields vs N for (I) absorption and (II) emission. Total QY = QY_b + QY_c (black solid), QY_b (red dashed, path b), QY_c (blue dashed, path c). QY_b propto N at small N (Dicke linear scaling, matches dotted line), peaks ~1, then suppressed at large N. QY_c bounded < 0.5. Both suppressed for large N (PR dominance).
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: Number of Molecules N, log scale 10^0 to 10^5; y: Quantum Yield, log scale 10^{-2} to ~10^0 (I) / 10^{-4} to 10^0 (II). Dotted reference line shows linear QY propto N.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Heatmap of total quantum yield QY as a function of primary-mode frequency omega_v (y, log) and number of molecules N (x, log), for (I) absorption and (II) emission, with E_r fixed at 1.0 eV and lambda_v^2 = 2 omega_v^2. Red dashed contour encloses QY_b contribution (suppressed in fast-bath / large omega_v regime); blue dotted contour encloses QY_c contribution (robust against thermal fluctuation, vertical band near N_max independent of omega_v).
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: N, log scale ~10^1 to ~10^5; y: omega_v (relative units), log scale ~10^{-2} to ~10^0; color: QY from 0.0 to 1.0 (colorbar).

uncertainties: The damping coefficient is printed as 'gamma = 0.065 s^-1' on p.8. A friction coefficient in s^-1 of 0.065 is physically odd for a fast Markovian bath; it is likely in reduced/atomic units or relative to omega_v, but the paper states '0.065 s^-1' verbatim. I transcribed the printed value and units but flag the unit as suspect.

The Langevin equation on p.3 is printed as R-double-dot_j = -omega_v^2 R_j - gamma_v * P-dot_j + eta_j(t). The friction term written as gamma_v * P-dot (rather than the conventional gamma_v * R-dot = gamma_v * P) may be a typesetting artifact in the OCR/source; standard Langevin friction is proportional to velocity R-dot. I transcribed as printed.

Eq.(38) PR rate is written with sum k=1 to N-1, but the dark states are explicitly defined as |X_k> for k=1,...,N-2 (Appendix A, Eq.A6/A7 and text on p.8). The summation upper limit in Eq.(38) (N-1) appears inconsistent with the stated N-2 dark states; this may be an indexing typo in the paper. Appendix B Eq.(B1) uses sum over k generically.

Exact numeric value of N_max is not printed as a number in the paper; I computed N_max ~ 1 + (1.0 eV)/(8.55e-3 eV) ~ 118 from hbar*omega_c=1.0 eV and |T_01|=69 cm^-1=8.55e-3 eV. The figure dotted lines appear at somewhat larger N (~10^3 for absorption per Fig.2 text ranges 20<=N<=1300 for path-b enhancement), so the precise printed N_max line position in Fig.2 could not be read off exactly; the order of magnitude is consistent.

The exact numeric ranges/values of the omega_v axis in Fig.4 are in relative (dimensionless) units and the absolute scaling (relative to 80.6 cm^-1 baseline) is not explicitly tabulated; axis read as ~10^-2 to ~10^0 from the figure.

Whether E_DA in Eq.(25) equals E_D - E_A (= -E_AD) or +E_AD: I interpreted E_DA = E_D - E_A based on context (donor block diagonal) but the paper's sign convention for E_DA vs E_AD in the matrices (Eqs.25,31) is not 100% unambiguous from the text; the resonance condition |E_AD|=hbar*omega_c is unambiguous.

Eq.(2) writes the acceptor energy with bath coupling sum_alpha c_{alpha,j}(b^dag + b); the exact placement of factors (whether c is energy or coupling with hbar) follows standard spin-boson; transcribed as printed.

I could not independently verify the precise value 245 cm^-1 ~ 0.03 eV consistency (245 cm^-1 = 0.0304 eV, consistent) and 69 cm^-1 = 8.55e-3 eV vs stated |t_AD|=8.5e-3 (consistent to rounding, since 69/8065.5 ~ 8.55e-3); these are internally consistent within rounding.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Approximate analytical (blue/colored lines) and numerical (black lines) steady-state solutions of equation (5) for a single radially symmetric Gaussian-pumped condensate: density rho (solid), velocity u (dashed), and the Gaussian pump profile p(r)=p0 exp(-sigma r^2) (green shaded). Two parameter sets: (a) sigma=0.2, p0=5; (b) sigma=0.4, p0=10. Fixed b=1.5, gamma=0.2, g=0.5. Reported velocity parameters: (a) k_c=1.65885, l=0.541858; (b) k_c=1.99239, l=0.909976.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: Distance (micrometers), range -10 to +10. y-axis: dimensionless amplitude; panel (a) approx -2 to 5, panel (b) approx -2 to 10 (density rho, velocity u, and pump p all overlaid, dimensionless after non-dimensionalization).

figures_to_emulate.shows: Theoretical prediction of classical magnetic spin (phase) configurations: contour plots of normalized polariton density |Psi|^2 with arrows showing relative condensate phases. Panels: (a) ferromagnet all J_ij>0; (b) rhomb horizontal J_ij>0; (c) spiral 1 all J_ij<0; (d) spiral 2 horizontal J_ij<0; (e) defect (single ferromagnetic edge in antiferromagnetic graph); (f) star (central vertex ferromagnetic to all neighbours); (g) fully disordered graph. Solid lines = ferromagnetic coupling (J_ij>0), dashed lines = antiferromagnetic (J_ij<0). Density shown is |Psi|^2 with Psi = sum_i Psi_i, individual Psi_i are analytic approximations.
figures_to_emulate.axes: 2D real-space density contour plots (x,y in micrometers); scale bar = 10 micrometers shown in panel (g). Color = normalized density; arrows = phase orientation (XY spin direction).

figures_to_emulate.shows: (a) Schematic energy landscape of the simulated XY Hamiltonian showing classical thermal annealing, quantum annealing via tunnelling between metastable states, and bosonic stimulation leading to condensation at threshold. (b) Schematic comparison between photon laser and polariton graph operation above threshold.
figures_to_emulate.axes: Schematic only: horizontal axis = configuration/coordinate of energy landscape, vertical axis = energy. No quantitative scale.

key_results.claim: Maximizing the total number of polariton particles M is equivalent to minimizing the classical XY Hamiltonian H_XY = -sum_{i<j} J_ij cos(theta_i - theta_j). This is the central mapping: the polariton graph is an analogue XY-model simulator.
key_results.evidence: Total mass M = 2 pi N integral |Psi_0|^2 r dr + sum_{i<j} J_ij cos(theta_i - theta_j) (eq 18). Since the system maximizes M, it minimizes H_XY = -sum_{i<j} J_ij cos(theta_ij). Coupling J_ij defined by eq (19) as overlap integral of Bessel-transformed single-condensate wavefunctions.

key_results.claim: The sign and strength of the coupling J_ij between condensate sites can be controlled experimentally to be ferromagnetic (J_ij>0) or antiferromagnetic (J_ij<0) by tuning the inter-site distance |r_i - r_j| or the pumping parameters (intensity p0, inverse width sigma).
key_results.evidence: Main contribution to the integral defining the Bessel-transformed wavefunction is at k = k_c (outflow wavevector fully determined by pump profile). J_ij = (1/pi) integral |Psi_0_tilde(k)|^2 J_0(k|r_i-r_j|) k dk (eq 19) oscillates with separation. Figure 3 demonstrates ferromagnetic, rhombic, spiral, defect, star, and disordered configurations.

key_results.claim: The complex Ginzburg-Landau (cGLE) dynamics reduce to the Kuramoto model for the condensate phases, whose gradient flow minimizes the XY Hamiltonian.
key_results.evidence: Phase dynamics theta_dot_i = -rho - sum J_ij sin(theta_ij) (eq 22) is the Kuramoto model; its equilibria are stationary points of the potential V(theta) = rho sum theta_i - (1/2) sum J_ij cos(theta_ij) (eq 23).

key_results.claim: For two condensates, the relative phase locks to 0 (in-phase) when J_12>0 and to pi (anti-phase) when J_12<0, as pumping increases above threshold, with phase oscillations damped at a rate proportional to 2(1 - p^{-1} + J_12 cos theta_12).
key_results.evidence: Relative phase satisfies second-order ODE eq (25): theta_ddot_12 + 2(1 - 1/p + J_12 cos theta_12) theta_dot_12 = -4(1 - 1/p) J_12 sin theta_12. Agrees with H_XY = -J_12 cos theta_12 minimized at theta_12=0 for J_12>0 and pi for J_12<0.

key_results.claim: Relative strength of the saturable vs cubic nonlinearity: the dimensionless saturation parameter b is of order 2-4 / (gamma_R) ps for typical GaAs microcavity parameters, i.e. of order the real nonlinearity for gamma_R ~ 1 ps^-1.
key_results.evidence: Using hbar R_R = 0.1 meV um^2, U_0 = 0.02-0.04 meV um^2 yields b = 2-4 / gamma_R ps; with gamma_R of order 1 ps^-1, b is of order the real nonlinearity. Figure 2 uses b=1.5.

key_results.claim: Away from the pumping spot (p(r)=0) the condensate density decays and the outflow velocity is set by the outflow wavenumber k_c via r rho + rho' = -gamma rho r / k_c, giving rho ~ exp(-r gamma/k_c)/r, with chemical potential mu = k_c^2 - gamma^2/(4 k_c^2) at infinity.
key_results.evidence: From eq (7) at infinity mu = k_c^2 - gamma^2/(4 k_c^2). Density and velocity approximated by eqs (8) and (9): u(r) = k_c tanh(l r / k_c).

uncertainties: CRITICAL SCOPE MISMATCH: This paper does NOT contain a Hopfield/coupled-oscillator photon-exciton model. There is NO 2x2 photon-exciton coupling matrix, NO lower/upper polariton dispersion E_LP(k) or E_UP(k), NO vacuum Rabi splitting equation, NO cavity photon dispersion E_cav(k)=sqrt(...), and NO explicit Hopfield coefficients (excitonic/photonic fractions) anywhere in the text. The paper is a driven-dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii / complex Ginzburg-Landau (cGLE) treatment of polariton CONDENSATES mapped onto the classical XY and Kuramoto models. The requested Hopfield-model content is simply not present in this article; those concepts are only alluded to in the introduction ('strong exciton-photon coupling regime', ref [15] Weisbuch et al). To emulate Rabi-split polariton dispersions one would need a different/companion reference.

The paper uses a non-dimensionalized form throughout sections 2-4. Most quantities (rho, u, p0, k_c, l, b, g, gamma, eta) are DIMENSIONLESS after the rescaling psi -> sqrt(hbar^2/(2 m U_0 l0^2)) Psi, r -> l0 r, t -> 2 m t l0^2/hbar with l0 = 1 micrometer. Physical/SI values are only given for the un-rescaled reservoir parameters (hbar R_R = 0.1 meV um^2, U_0 = 0.02-0.04 meV um^2, gamma_R ~ 1 ps^-1). I could not extract independent SI values for m (polariton effective mass), gamma_C, g_R individually — they enter only through the dimensionless combinations.

In equation (3)/(4), I read the dimensionless cGLE as i dPsi/dt = -(1 - i eta n_R) grad^2 Psi + |Psi|^2 Psi + g n_R Psi + i(n_R - gamma) Psi with n_R = p(r)/(1 + b|Psi|^2). The sign/placement of the g n_R blueshift term and the eta relaxation factor are transcribed as printed but the small-eta limit is used to drop them in later equations.

Equation (11) for k_c^2 is a long polynomial expansion; I transcribed it but the exact grouping of terms (e.g. the coefficient 3 gamma^2(8 l^3 - 12 l^2(p0-gamma)+(p0-gamma)^2 gamma) ) should be double-checked against the PDF for sign/factor fidelity before use in simulation.

The definitions Q_+ and Q_- appearing in the two-spot density equations (eq 24) are written as Q_pm = (1 + b(R pm z))^{-1} based on the text; the subscript pairing (which of R+z vs R-z goes with Q_+ vs Q_-) follows the printed equation but should be verified.

Figure 2 y-axis is not explicitly labelled with a quantity name in the caption beyond 'density (solid lines) and velocity (dashed lines)'; the numeric axis range is read from the plot tick marks (a: -2 to 5; b: -2 to 10) and may be approximate.

The natural frequencies omega_i in the Kuramoto formulation are stated to be 'associated with the chemical potential of individual condensates' but an explicit omega_i term does not appear in the final reduced Kuramoto equation (22) because all vertices are pumped identically (degenerate frequencies); for heterogeneous pumping omega_i would re-enter.

paper.title: A polariton graph simulator
paper.authors: Pavlos G Lagoudakis, Natalia G Berloff

key_equations.name: Driven-dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii / complex Ginzburg-Landau equation (cGLE) for the condensate

variables.typical: ~10^-4 to 10^-5 electron masses (GaAs microcavity; not numerically given in paper)
not given numerically (enters via dimensionless g=2 g_R/R_R)

variables.meaning: energy relaxation coefficient (rate gain decreases with increasing energy)
rate reservoir excitons enter (stimulate into) the condensate
variables.units: um^2 / s (hbar R_R given as meV um^2)

variables.meaning: rate of condensate losses (photon decay through Bragg mirrors)

key_equations.context: Mean-field driven-dissipative model of a non-resonantly pumped polariton condensate coupled to a hot exciton reservoir. Valid in the strong-coupling polariton regime under incoherent (non-resonant) pumping.

key_equations.name: Reservoir rate equation

variables.meaning: redistribution rate of reservoir excitons between energy levels (reservoir decay)

variables.typical: spatial profile set by pump laser

variables.meaning: reservoir density (stationary R = P/(gamma_R + R_R|psi|^2) in limit gamma_R >> gamma_C)

key_equations.context: Coupled to eq (1). The sign convention as printed; in the limit gamma_R >> gamma_C the stationary reservoir R = P(r)/(gamma_R + R_R|psi|^2) is used.

key_equations.name: Dimensionless cGLE with saturable nonlinearity
key_equations.latex: i\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = -(1 - i\eta n_R)\nabla^2 \Psi + |\Psi|^2 \Psi + g n_R \Psi + i(n_R - \gamma)\Psi

variables.meaning: rescaled condensate wavefunction (psi -> sqrt(hbar^2/(2 m U_0 l0^2)) Psi)
dimensionless condensate loss gamma = m gamma_C l0^2/hbar
dimensionless energy relaxation eta = eta_d hbar/(m R_R l0^2)
dimensionless pump p = m l0^2 R_R P(r)/(hbar gamma_R)
dimensionless reservoir saturation b = R_R hbar^2/(2 m l0^2 gamma_R U_0)

key_equations.context: Non-dimensionalized form of eqs (1)-(2) with saturable (reservoir-clamped) nonlinearity n_R = p/(1+b|Psi|^2). Length scale l0 = 1 um, time -> 2 m t l0^2/hbar.

key_equations.name: Standard cGLE (Taylor-expanded reservoir, small |Psi|^2)
key_equations.latex: i\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = -(1 - i\eta p)\nabla^2 \Psi + (1 - g p b)|\Psi|^2 \Psi + g p \Psi + i(p - \gamma - p b|\Psi|^2)\Psi

variables.meaning: dimensionless pump profile p(r)=p0 exp(-sigma r^2)
dimensionless interaction, saturation, loss, relaxation parameters

key_equations.context: Obtained by Taylor expansion of n_R for small |Psi|^2; the g eta|Psi|^2 term dropped given smallness of eta. This is the equation actually solved numerically and analytically (Fig 2).

key_equations.name: Mass continuity equation (Madelung form, Psi = sqrt(rho) exp(iS), u = grad S)
key_equations.context: Real part of the radially symmetric steady-state cGLE (3)-(4). Madelung transformation Psi = sqrt(rho) exp(iS).

key_equations.name: Bernoulli equation (chemical potential)

variables.typical: mu = k_c^2 - gamma^2/(4 k_c^2) at infinity

key_equations.context: Imaginary part / phase equation of the radially symmetric steady-state cGLE. Away from pump (p=0): mu = k_c^2 - gamma^2/(4 k_c^2).

key_equations.name: Approximate condensate density profile
key_equations.latex: \rho(r) = \frac{a_0}{\gamma r \exp(\gamma r k_c^{-1}) k_c^{-1} + \xi - \gamma r/k_c + a_3 r^3}

variables.meaning: parametric-family fitting parameters determined by matched asymptotics (eqs 10-13)

key_equations.context: Approximate analytic density built from origin (r->0) and infinity (rho ~ exp(-r gamma/k_c)/r) asymptotics. Used for single-condensate wavefunction in Fig 2 and Fig 3.

key_equations.name: Approximate condensate velocity profile
key_equations.context: Outflow velocity grows linearly near origin and saturates to k_c at infinity. k_c, l found via matched asymptotics (eqs 10-13).

key_equations.name: Parameters xi and a_3 (leading order)
key_equations.context: Leading-order expansion of eq (6) and first-order of eq (7) about r=0.

key_equations.name: Outflow wavenumber k_c^2 (matched-asymptotics expansion of eq 6 to O(r^2))
key_equations.latex: k_c^2 = \frac{4 a_0 b l^3 p_0 (2l + \gamma) + 3\gamma^2(8 l^3 - 12 l^2(p_0 - \gamma) + (p_0 - \gamma)^2 \gamma) + 2l(2 p_0^2 - 5 p_0 \gamma + 3\gamma^2)}{3 a_0 b p_0 \sigma(2l + \gamma)^2}
key_equations.context: Determines k_c from system + pump parameters. NOTE: grouping of polynomial terms should be re-verified against PDF (see uncertainties).

key_equations.name: Closure relations for k_c^2 and l^2 (O(r^2) expansion of eq 7)
key_equations.latex: k_c^2 = \frac{a_0}{\xi} + \frac{\gamma^2(\xi + 8)}{4 k_c^2 \xi} + \frac{g p_0 \xi}{a_0 b + \xi}, \qquad l^2 = \frac{a_0 \gamma^2}{k_c^2 \xi^2} + \frac{5\gamma^4}{k_c^4 \xi} - \frac{4\gamma^4}{3 k_c^4 \xi} - \frac{a_0 b g p_0 \gamma^2}{k_c^2(a_0 b + \xi)^2} + \frac{g p_0 \sigma \xi}{a_0 b + \xi}
key_equations.context: Two coupled nonlinear equations that, with eqs (10)-(11), fully determine a_0, l, xi, a_3, k_c for given g, b, gamma, p0, sigma.

key_equations.name: Single-condensate wavefunction (phase from integrated velocity)
key_equations.context: Phase S(r) = (k_c^2/l) log(cosh(l r/k_c)) obtained by integrating u(r)=k_c tanh(l r/k_c). Building block for the N-condensate ansatz Psi_N approx sum_i Psi_0(|r-r_i|) exp(i theta_i).

key_equations.name: Multi-spot Gaussian pump profile

variables.meaning: pump intensity at centre of spot i
variables.typical: p_i = p0 for all i (p0=3 in Fig 3)

variables.meaning: inverse width (squared) of Gaussian spot i
position of pump spot / graph vertex i

key_equations.context: Spatial light modulator imprints condensates at graph vertices. All vertices typically pumped identically (p_i=p0, sigma_i=sigma).

key_equations.name: Total mass with XY coupling term (the analogue XY mapping)

variables.meaning: total number of polaritons (total mass); the system maximizes this
coupling between sites i and j (eq 19); sign tunable
phase of condensate i (XY spin angle)

key_equations.context: Maximizing M is equivalent to minimizing H_XY = -sum_{i<j} J_ij cos(theta_ij). Central result establishing the polariton graph as an XY simulator. Wavefunctions assumed well-separated.

key_equations.name: Coupling coefficient J_ij (Bessel overlap integral)

variables.meaning: Hankel transform of single-condensate wavefunction, = 2pi integral Psi_0(r) J_0(kr) r dr
zeroth-order Bessel function of the first kind

key_equations.context: Main contribution from k=k_c. The oscillatory J_0(k_c d) makes J_ij alternate sign with separation d -> tunable ferro/antiferromagnetic coupling. This is the microscopic origin of the XY couplings.

key_equations.name: Classical XY Hamiltonian (minimized by the polariton graph)
key_equations.context: The global ground state of the polariton graph (max M) corresponds to the minimum of H_XY. The simulator targets this NP-hard minimization for non-convex/dense J.

key_equations.name: Two-mode model (network of radiatively coupled condensates)

variables.meaning: complex amplitude of condensate i (no spatial variation)
radiative coupling between condensates i and j (from interference of outflowing polaritons)

key_equations.context: Spatially-averaged (two-mode) reduction representing each condensate as a single mode coupled radiatively. Basis for the Kuramoto derivation.

key_equations.name: Density and phase dynamics of coupled condensates
key_equations.context: Real/imaginary parts of eq (20) with blueshift g neglected and gamma=1. Density steady state rho=(p-1)/b.

key_equations.name: Kuramoto model for condensate phases
key_equations.context: Fast-density limit (rho_i = rho = (p-1)/b). Gradient flow that minimizes V(theta) and hence the XY Hamiltonian. Natural frequencies degenerate for identical pumping.

key_equations.name: Kuramoto potential energy landscape

variables.meaning: potential whose minima are the Kuramoto equilibria (XY ground states)

key_equations.context: theta_dot_i = -dV/dtheta_i; gradient flow to minima of V minimizes XY Hamiltonian.

key_equations.name: Two-condensate phase oscillation (damped) equation

variables.meaning: relative phase of the two condensates
radiative coupling between the two spots

key_equations.context: Two-spot reduction (R=(rho1+rho2)/2, z=(rho1-rho2)/2, R=(p-1)/b to leading order). Oscillations damp at rate 2(1 - 1/p + J12 cos theta12); phase locks to 0 for J12>0, pi for J12<0, matching H_XY = -J12 cos theta12.

parameters.meaning: Dimensionless reservoir saturation parameter, b = R_R hbar^2/(2 m l0^2 gamma_R U_0)
parameters.range: estimated 2-4 / (gamma_R in ps^-1); value 1.5 used in Figures 2 and 3

parameters.meaning: Dimensionless polariton-exciton (reservoir) interaction strength, g = 2 g_R/R_R
Dimensionless condensate loss rate, gamma = m gamma_C l0^2/hbar
Dimensionless energy relaxation coefficient, eta = eta_d hbar/(m R_R l0^2)
Peak (maximum) dimensionless pumping intensity at spot centre
Inverse-width-squared of the Gaussian pump spot, p(r)=p0 exp(-sigma r^2)
Outflow wavenumber (asymptotic radial velocity of polaritons leaving a condensate); determines coupling oscillation period
Velocity-profile scale parameter (slope of u(r) at origin in u = k_c tanh(l r/k_c))
Length scale for non-dimensionalization
Effective polariton-polariton interaction strength (physical, pre-rescaling)
Reservoir-to-condensate stimulated scattering rate coefficient (physical)
Reservoir exciton redistribution/decay rate (physical)
Number of condensate sites / graph vertices
XY coupling between sites i,j (sign tunable via separation and pump params)
Radiative (Kuramoto) coupling between condensates from outflow interference

pdfFindings.summary: This paper (Lagoudakis and Berloff, New J. Phys. 19, 125008, 2017) proposes non-resonantly pumped exciton-polariton condensates arranged on an arbitrary 2D graph (imprinted by a spatial light modulator) as an analogue simulator for the classical XY and Kuramoto models. The condensates are modelled by a driven-dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii / complex Ginzburg-Landau equation (cGLE) coupled to a saturable hot-exciton reservoir (eqs 1-5), which is non-dimensionalized with a length scale l0 = 1 um into parameters g, b, gamma, eta, p, sigma. For a single radially symmetric Gaussian pump spot the authors derive, via matched asymptotics, approximate analytic density rho(r) (eq 8) and outflow velocity u(r) = k_c tanh(l r/k_c) (eq 9), giving a single-condensate wavefunction Psi_0(r) = sqrt(rho0) exp[i (k_c^2/l) log(cosh(l r/k_c))] (eq 15). The central result is that the system, by maximizing the total polariton number M = 2 pi N integral|Psi_0|^2 r dr + sum_{i<j} J_ij cos(theta_i - theta_j) (eq 18), minimizes the classical XY Hamiltonian H_XY = -sum J_ij cos(theta_ij), where the couplings J_ij are Bessel overlap integrals (eq 19) whose sign oscillates with site separation (set by the outflow wavevector k_c), enabling experimentally tunable ferromagnetic/antiferromagnetic couplings. The cGLE further reduces to a Kuramoto phase model theta_dot_i = -rho - sum J_ij sin(theta_ij) (eq 22) whose gradient flow minimizes the same XY potential, and a two-condensate analysis (eq 25) shows the relative phase locks to 0 for J12>0 and pi for J12<0. IMPORTANT: this paper contains NO Hopfield model, NO 2x2 photon-exciton coupling matrix, NO upper/lower polariton dispersions, NO Rabi-splitting equation, and NO cavity-photon dispersion E_cav(k) — those FOCUS items are absent from this article.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Polariton energy-level diagrams and absorption spectra: (c) single-molecule JC polariton splitting 2g_c with UP/LP; (d) N-molecule collective splitting 2*sqrt(N)*g_c with UP, LP and dark states (DS); (e) bare molecular absorption (single Lorentzian); (f) cavity absorption showing two polariton peaks (LP, UP) with dark states sitting optically-dark in between.
figures_to_emulate.axes: Energy-level panels: vertical axis = energy (with Rabi splitting 2g_c or 2*sqrt(N)*g_c marked, and detuning Delta-E and hbar*omega_c indicated). Absorption panels: x-axis = Energy, y-axis = Absorption (arb. units).

figures_to_emulate.shows: Jaynes-Cummings polariton ladder E_pm(n) vs photon number n, showing upper/lower polariton branches split by Rabi frequency Omega_n = 2 g_c sqrt(n+1).
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis = photon number n (0,1,2,...); y-axis = Energy E (eV). Branches labeled |+,n>, |-,n>.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Polariton eigenspectrum of a two-level system + single cavity mode computed at different theory levels (full Pauli-Fierz, perturbation theory, Rabi, RWA, JC). (a) three lowest polariton energies vs eta = g_c/omega_c at zero detuning; (b) energies vs detuning Delta-E - hbar*omega_c at fixed hbar*g_c = 1 eV.
figures_to_emulate.axes: (a) x-axis = eta = g_c/omega_c (0 to ~0.5), y-axis = Energy (eV, 0 to 5). (b) x-axis = Delta-E - hbar*omega_c (eV, -0.8 to 0.8), y-axis = Energy (eV). Curves labeled |g,0>,|-,0>,|+,0> for PF/PT/Rabi/RWA/JC.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Fabry-Perot polariton dispersion: upper and lower polariton energy (purple solid) vs incident angle theta, with bare cavity-mode dispersion (red) and flat exciton dispersion (blue). Three panels for (b) zero detuning, (c) positive detuning, (d) negative detuning.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis = incident angle theta (degrees, -20 to +20); y-axis = Energy (eV, ~2.0 to 2.6). Curves: UP, LP (purple), Cavity Mode (red parabola), Exciton (blue flat line).

figures_to_emulate.shows: (a) Collective coupling level scheme: N identical emitters coupling to one cavity mode forming UP, LP and N-1 dark states; (d) population dynamics of lower polariton, upper polariton, all dark states, and ground state vs time showing transient transfer into dark-state manifold prolonging excited-state lifetime.
figures_to_emulate.axes: (d) x-axis = time (fs, 0 to 200); y-axis = population (0 to 1.0). Curves: lower polariton, upper polariton, all dark states, ground state.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Vibrational strong coupling Rabi splitting scaling: (a) IR transmission spectra at increasing molecular concentration (PTA %) showing splitting of vibrational band into two polariton peaks; (b) Rabi splitting energy Omega_R vs sqrt(concentration) showing linear (sqrt(N)) scaling.
figures_to_emulate.axes: (a) x-axis = wavenumber (cm^-1, 700-1100), y-axis = transmittance. (b) x-axis = [PTA]^(1/2) (M^1/2, 0-2.5), y-axis = Rabi splitting energy (cm^-1).

key_results.claim: The collective Rabi splitting scales as sqrt(N) with the number of molecules N, enhancing the effective coupling by sqrt(N) or equivalently with the concentration N/V.
key_results.evidence: Eq 16-17: Omega_R = sqrt((Delta-E - hbar*omega_c)^2 + 4 N g_c^2); at resonance Omega_R = 2 sqrt(N) g_c = 2 sqrt(N/V) sqrt(2 omega_c / hbar epsilon_0) e-hat . mu_eg. Verified experimentally in Figure 20b where Rabi splitting depends linearly on sqrt(concentration).

key_results.claim: N molecules collectively coupled to one cavity mode form 2 bright polariton states (UP, LP) and N-1 dark states; the dark states are optically dark from the ground state (zero net transition dipole) and carry no photonic character (in the disorder-free TC limit).
key_results.evidence: Eq 13-14, 18: bright state |B,0> = (1/sqrt(N)) Sum_J |E_J,0>; dark states |D_alpha,0> = Sum_J c_J^alpha |E_J,0> with Sum_J c_J^alpha = 0; net transition dipole D_alpha = mu_eg Sum_J c_J^alpha = 0.

key_results.claim: The number of molecules effectively coupled to a Fabry-Perot cavity mode is estimated as N ~ 10^6 to 10^11 organic molecules; for CdSe nanoplatelets N ~ 10^3 emitters per cavity mode.
key_results.evidence: Section 1.2: 'theoretically estimated... that there are N ~ 10^6 - 10^11 organic molecules effectively coupled to the cavity mode. For CdSe nanoplatelets... N ~ 10^3 emitters per cavity mode.'

key_results.claim: Coupling regimes are classified by g_c*sqrt(N)/gamma (loss rate) for weak vs strong, and by g_c*sqrt(N)/omega_c for ultrastrong vs deep-strong: weak (g_c sqrt(N)/gamma < 1), strong (>1), ultrastrong (0.1 < g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c < 1), deep-strong (g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c > 1).
key_results.evidence: Section 1.3: 'coupling considered weak for g_c sqrt(N)/gamma < 1 and strong for g_c sqrt(N)/gamma > 1... ultrastrong regime being 0.1 < g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c < 1, and the deep strong regime being g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c > 1.'

key_results.claim: The dipole self-energy (DSE) term, proportional to (mu . A_0)^2, is essential in the strong/ultrastrong coupling regime; dropping it makes the ground state unbounded and violates the Maxwell equations in matter. It arises only when the field is quantized (absent in classical/semiclassical d.E derivations).
key_results.evidence: Eq 52: E_DSE = (omega_c/hbar)(mu-hat . A_0)^2. 'the loss of the DSE term causes the ground state to be unbounded... it is essential to include the DSE term in the strong and ultrastrong coupling regimes.' Section 2.3.2.

key_results.claim: Gauge ambiguities arise from matter-state truncation: the p.A (Coulomb gauge) and d.E (dipole gauge) Hamiltonians give DIFFERENT polariton spectra under the same finite truncation, even though they should be identical in the complete basis. The dipole gauge converges faster because mu behaves locally in the energy representation.
key_results.evidence: Figure 2; Section 2.4: 'p.A usually requires a larger subset of the matter states to converge... mu behaves locally in the energy space and thus truncation is often a valid approximation.' TRK sum rule eq 68 explains the asymmetry.

key_results.claim: Within the simple 1D transition state theory (TST) for VSC, the free-energy barrier Delta-E-double-dagger is INDEPENDENT of cavity frequency omega_c and coupling A_0 (because the light-matter coupling term is a complete square in q_c), so TST predicts NO ground-state rate modification. This is why TST cannot explain VSC experiments.
key_results.evidence: Eq 197-199: V(R,q_c) = E_g(R) + (1/2) omega_c^2 (q_c + sqrt(2/hbar omega_c) A_0 mu_gg(R))^2; minimum-energy path barrier unchanged; 'k_TST is also independent of omega_c or A_0.'

key_results.claim: The cavity photon mode acts as a non-Markovian solvent-like degree of freedom that enhances recrossing of the reaction coordinate (dynamical caging effect), suppressing the rate; this is captured by Grote-Hynes / Pollak-Grabert-Hanggi rate theory through the transmission coefficient kappa, NOT through the barrier.
key_results.evidence: Section 5.4-5.5; Figure 21-22; eq 200 k = lim kappa(t) k_TST; eq 204 k = kappa_d kappa_GH k_TST. 'rate constant... in Figure 20c results in ~4 kJ/mol change in effective Delta(Delta-G-double-dagger)... in fact due to the change of kappa.'

key_results.claim: In the collective regime, polariton-mediated charge transfer reorganization energy of the polariton states scales as 1/N (polaron decoupling): the effective electronic coupling scales as 1/sqrt(N), driving the reorganization energy of polariton/dark states toward zero relative to the acceptor.
key_results.evidence: Eq 212-215: |+-> = (1/sqrt(2))[(1/sqrt(N)) Sum_J |D_J,0> +- |G,1>]; <+|H_pl|A_J,0> = (cos Theta_N / sqrt(N)) V_DA; reorganization energy proportional to donor reorganization energy times 1/N.

key_results.claim: Free-energy (entropic) reordering can place the lower polariton ABOVE the dark states in free energy: while LP lies below the dark states energetically, the dark-state manifold has much larger entropy (k_B log N), so F = E - TS reorders them. The ratio T(S_Dark - S_LP)/(E_Dark - E_LP) ~ log N / sqrt(N) -> 0 as N -> infinity.
key_results.evidence: Figure 26; eq 210-211; 'lim_{N->inf} T(S_Dark - S_LP)/(E_Dark - E_LP) proportional to lim log N / sqrt(N) = 0.' Dark-LP energy gap proportional to sqrt(N) g_c, max entropy ~ k_B log N.

key_results.claim: Collective coupling delays excited-state relaxation: after exciting a polariton, population transfers into the dark-state manifold (no photonic component), which suppresses cavity loss and gives cavity-molecule systems a much longer excited-state lifetime than the bare cavity.
key_results.evidence: Figure 24d; Section 6.1: 'nonadiabatic transitions to the dark-state manifold... does not have photonic contributions, thus suppressing cavity loss... cavity-molecule hybrid systems having a much longer lifetime than the bare cavity.'

key_results.claim: Ebbesen group achieved ultrastrong light-matter coupling in a Fabry-Perot cavity with effective g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c = 0.16; single molecules in plasmonic cavities (Baumberg group) reached nearly the ultrastrong regime.
key_results.evidence: Section 1.3: 'the Ebbesen group in ref 49 achieved ultrastrong light-matter coupling in a Fabry-Perot cavity with an effective g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c = 0.16.'

uncertainties: The exact numerical 'typical' values I provide for parameters (e.g., omega_c ~ 0.1-5 eV electronic, ~1000-2000 cm^-1 vibrational; g_c ~ tens to hundreds of cm^-1 for VSC; Omega_R ~ 0.1-0.3 eV electronic) are inferred from the figures and scattered text in the review, not from a single consolidated parameter table. The paper is a review and does not provide one master parameter table; ranges are synthesized across Sections 1, 5, and 6.

In eq 3 the coupling g_c = sqrt(hbar omega_c / 2 epsilon V) e-hat . mu_eg: I transcribed it as written, but the paper uses both 'epsilon' (permittivity) and later epsilon_0; for vacuum epsilon = epsilon_0. The script font for the cavity volume is 'V' (calligraphic) throughout - I rendered it as V (mathcal V / cavity quantization volume).

Several equations contain subtle font distinctions between calligraphic operators (truncated Hilbert space, e.g. H-mathcal-M) and regular operators (full Hilbert space H-M). I have preserved the physics but in LaTeX rendered most as standard symbols; the distinction matters for the truncation/gauge-ambiguity discussion (Section 2.4).

In eq 195/220 the single-vs-collective VSC Rabi splitting, the reduced mass M and mode frequency omega_0 (reactant well / vibrational frequency) appear; I am confident in the structure hbar*Omega_R = 2 sqrt(N) hbar omega_c eta but the exact identification of M (reduced mass of reaction coordinate) vs the vibrational frequency normalization should be double-checked against eqs 193-195 if used quantitatively.

Figure 2 panel details (square vs double-well potential, n-level truncation values n=2,3,10,20,200) were read from the caption and figure; the precise coupling-strength axis ranges (g_0/omega_c from 0.01 to ~10) are read from small log-scale axes and may have minor reading error.

The Holstein-Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian (eq 103) phonon coupling term g_nu^J (b_nu^dagger + b_nu) sigma_j^dagger sigma_j: I transcribed the operator ordering as printed; the exact placement of the J vs nu indices on the phonon coupling constant g and frequency omega_nu is as shown but the review presents it as a phenomenological extension of eq 12.

I did not exhaustively read every page (the PDF is ~94 pages / 9786-9879); I focused on the equation-dense fundamental theory (Sec 2), quantum-optics-models (Sec 2.5-2.6), ab initio (Sec 3 partial), VSC model (Sec 5.1-5.5), and collective regime (Sec 6). Sections 3.2 (self-consistent QED: QED-HF, QED-CC, QEDFT), 4 (photochemistry methods detail), and parts of 5.6 were skimmed, not fully transcribed.

paper.title: Theoretical Advances in Polariton Chemistry and Molecular Cavity Quantum Electrodynamics
paper.authors: Arkajit Mandal, Michael A. D. Taylor, Braden M. Weight, Eric R. Koessler, Xinyang Li, Pengfei Huo
paper.venue: Chemical Reviews 2023, 123, 9786-9879 (ACS); part of the virtual special issue 'Polaritonic Chemistry'. DOI: 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855

pdfFindings.summary: This is a comprehensive review of the theory of molecular cavity QED and polariton chemistry. It builds the canonical light-matter Hamiltonians from first principles: the minimal-coupling (p.A, Coulomb gauge) Hamiltonian, the dipole-gauge (d.E) Hamiltonian obtained via the Power-Zienau-Woolley transformation, and the widely used Pauli-Fierz (PF) Hamiltonian, emphasizing the essential role of the dipole self-energy (DSE) term in the strong/ultrastrong regimes and showing that all gauges are equivalent only in the complete basis. It connects these rigorous Hamiltonians to standard quantum-optics models, the quantum Rabi, Jaynes-Cummings (single molecule), and Tavis-Cummings / Holstein-Tavis-Cummings (many molecules) models, and discusses the gauge ambiguities that arise upon matter-state truncation. The central collective-coupling result is that N molecules coupled to one cavity mode produce two bright polaritons (upper/lower) split by a collective Rabi frequency Omega_R = 2 sqrt(N) g_c that scales as sqrt(N) (or sqrt(concentration)), plus N-1 optically and photonically dark states. It reviews ab initio QED methods (parameterized QED and self-consistent QED-HF/CC/DFT) for computing polariton potential energy surfaces, polariton photochemistry (modified nonadiabatic dynamics, charge transfer, conical intersections), and vibrational strong coupling (VSC), where simple 1D transition state theory predicts no rate change and the cavity instead acts as a non-Markovian solvent-like degree of freedom (dynamical caging, Grote-Hynes/PGH theory). Finally it covers the collective regime, including dark-state-mediated lifetime enhancement, entropic free-energy reordering of polaritons (k_B log N entropy), 1/N polaron decoupling in polariton-mediated electron transfer, and the still-unresolved mechanism of collective VSC-modified ground-state reactivity.

key_equations.name: Single-molecule coupling strength g_c

variables.units: rad/s (or eV when multiplied by hbar)
variables.typical: tens to hundreds of cm^-1; ~meV per molecule in FP cavities

variables.meaning: permittivity inside cavity (= epsilon_0 in vacuum)

key_equations.context: Single emitter coupled to one cavity mode; derived rigorously from minimal coupling in Section 2.5. Defines coupling in the Jaynes-Cummings model.

key_equations.name: Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian
key_equations.context: Single two-level molecule + single cavity mode under rotating-wave approximation (RWA), dipole approximation, two-level approximation, no permanent dipole, no DSE. Good qualitative picture only; breaks down for realistic molecules and ultrastrong coupling.

key_equations.name: JC polariton eigenstates and mixing angle
key_equations.context: Single-molecule JC model. At resonance (Delta-E = hbar omega_c) the mixing angle is pi/4 giving 50/50 light-matter hybrid states.

key_equations.name: JC polariton eigenenergies and Rabi splitting

variables.meaning: upper(+)/lower(-) polariton energy for n photons
Rabi splitting = energy gap between UP and LP

key_equations.context: Single-molecule JC. At resonance the Rabi splitting = hbar Omega_n = 2 hbar g_c sqrt(n+1), scaling with sqrt of photon number.

key_equations.name: Collective (Tavis-Cummings) Rabi splitting

variables.typical: 0.1-1 eV electronic; up to ~100s cm^-1 VSC

key_equations.context: N identical two-level molecules collectively coupled to one cavity mode (Tavis-Cummings). The sqrt(N) (or sqrt(concentration)) scaling is the hallmark collective effect, verified experimentally.

key_equations.name: Collective bright state, polaritons, and dark states

variables.meaning: all-ground matter state with 1 cavity photon

key_equations.context: Single-excitation manifold of the Tavis-Cummings model for identical, non-interacting, disorder-free molecules. Only the bright state couples to the photon; the N-1 dark states have zero net transition dipole (optically dark) and zero photonic character.

key_equations.name: Minimal coupling (p.A) Hamiltonian, Coulomb gauge

variables.meaning: canonical momentum of charged particle j
charge and mass of particle j (electrons and nuclei)
transverse vector potential = A_0(a-hat + a-hat^dagger), A_0 = sqrt(hbar/2 omega_c eps_0 V) e-hat

key_equations.context: Long-wavelength, single-mode approximation, Coulomb gauge. Light-matter coupling carried through matter momentum + vector potential. Requires more matter states to converge than dipole gauge under truncation.

key_equations.name: Dipole-gauge (d.E) Hamiltonian

variables.meaning: total molecular dipole operator = Sum_j z_j x_j

key_equations.context: Obtained from p.A via Power-Zienau-Woolley (PZW) transformation. Length/dipole gauge. The (mu.A_0)^2 DSE term arises only from field quantization and is required for a bounded ground state.

key_equations.name: Pauli-Fierz (PF) Hamiltonian (dipole gauge, q_c/p_c form)
key_equations.context: Purely real Hamiltonian (long-wavelength approx). Obtained from d.E via U_0 = exp(-i pi/2 a^dagger a). The photonic DOF behaves as an additional 'nuclear coordinate' displaced by sqrt(2/hbar omega_c) mu.A_0. Standard form used for ab initio polariton chemistry.

key_equations.name: PF Hamiltonian, ab initio / lambda form

variables.typical: set by mode volume; cavity-volume dependent

key_equations.context: Convention used in ab initio polariton literature. lambda = sqrt(hbar/eps_0 V) is the magnitude of the vector potential times sqrt(2 omega_c); A_0 = lambda/sqrt(2 omega_c).

key_equations.name: Quantum Rabi Hamiltonian (no DSE, no RWA)
key_equations.context: Two-level molecule + single mode, ignoring permanent dipole and DSE, keeping counter-rotating terms. JC obtained by dropping counter-rotating terms; Rabi overestimates negative energy corrections, incorrectly destabilizing the ground state without DSE.

key_equations.name: Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian (N molecules)
key_equations.context: Many identical two-level molecules + one cavity mode, RWA, dipole approximation, no permanent dipole, no DSE. Analytical solution in single-excitation subspace gives 2 polaritons + N-1 dark states.

key_equations.name: Holstein-Tavis-Cummings (HTC) Hamiltonian

variables.meaning: phonon (Holstein) coupling strength for molecule J, mode nu
molecular excitation projector for molecule J

key_equations.context: Tavis-Cummings extended with phenomenological phonon (Holstein) modes linearly coupled to molecular excitation. Standard model for collective polariton dynamics with vibronic coupling; used heavily in dynamics simulations.

key_equations.name: Generalized Tavis-Cummings (GTC) Hamiltonian (many modes, many molecules)

variables.typical: k_z = n_z pi / L_z quantized; k_x, k_y quasi-continuous

key_equations.context: Many molecules coupled to many cavity modes (Fabry-Perot dispersion). Reduces to standard TC when removing phase factors (long-wavelength) and single mode. Used for angle-resolved dispersion simulations.

key_equations.name: Fabry-Perot cavity photon dispersion

variables.meaning: incident angle of photon from mirror normal (tan theta = k_x/k_z)

key_equations.context: Planar Fabry-Perot microcavity, single k_z. At theta=0, E_ph = hbar c k_z/n_eff = hbar omega_c. Quadratic dispersion in angle gives the parabolic cavity branch observed in angle-resolved spectra.

key_equations.name: Angle/k-resolved polariton dispersion and Hopfield coefficients
key_equations.context: Tavis-Cummings model evaluated mode-by-mode for the FP dispersion; gives the band-bending (anticrossing) of polariton branches with incident angle. Hopfield coefficients quantify light/matter mixing.

key_equations.name: Dipole self-energy (DSE)
key_equations.context: Required term in d.E and PF Hamiltonians. Ensures bounded ground state and Maxwell equations in matter. Negligible for small coupling (g_c << omega_c) but essential in (ultra)strong coupling. Absent in classical/semiclassical treatments because it arises from field commutation relations.

key_equations.name: Unitless coupling parameter (regimes)
key_equations.context: Defines coupling regime. JC accurate for eta < 0.1; collective version g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c sets the ultrastrong/deep-strong boundary.

key_equations.name: VSC model Hamiltonian (single molecule, reaction coordinate + cavity)

variables.meaning: ground-state PES along R (e.g. double well)
ground-state permanent dipole as function of R

key_equations.context: Single molecule in vibrational strong coupling: ground electronic state, reaction coordinate R coupled to one cavity mode q_c through the permanent dipole, plus a dissipative bath. Cavity acts as a solvent-like DOF. Resonant when omega_c = omega_0 (reactant well frequency).

key_equations.name: VSC single-molecule Rabi splitting

variables.meaning: reduced mass of reaction coordinate R
slope of permanent dipole at reactant well

key_equations.context: Single-molecule VSC; coupling enters through the dipole derivative (IR-active vibration). Valid under linear dipole approximation; generalizes to collective form eq 220.

key_equations.name: Collective VSC Rabi splitting (N molecules)

variables.meaning: number of molecules coupled to q_c
collective (bright) molecular coordinate that couples to q_c

key_equations.context: N identical molecules collectively coupled to one cavity mode in VSC; the collective bright coordinate couples with sqrt(N). Only valid under linear dipole approximation; breaks for ultrastrong (eta > 0.1).

key_equations.name: Polariton-mediated charge transfer: effective electronic coupling (1/sqrt(N))

variables.meaning: state with molecule J in donor excited state, 0 photons

key_equations.context: Collective polariton-mediated electron transfer. The 1/sqrt(2N) prefactor causes the polariton reorganization energy to scale as 1/N (polaron decoupling) and the effective coupling to scale as 1/sqrt(N) per acceptor.

key_equations.name: Marcus rate for polariton-mediated charge transfer
key_equations.context: Marcus (Fermi golden rule) rate from polariton |+/-> to acceptor states. Cavity tunes both driving force and reorganization energy; the 1/N reorganization scaling is the key collective effect.

key_equations.name: Transition state theory rate for VSC (cavity-independent barrier)
key_equations.context: Simple 1D TST for VSC. Because the PF light-matter term is a complete square in q_c, the barrier is independent of cavity frequency and coupling, so TST predicts NO VSC rate modification, demonstrating TST cannot explain VSC experiments.

key_equations.name: Rate with dynamical transmission coefficient (Grote-Hynes / PGH)

variables.meaning: stable normal-mode frequencies at reactant well

key_equations.context: The cavity modifies the ground-state rate through the transmission coefficient (dynamical caging / recrossing), NOT the barrier. GH captures the rate suppression; PGH (eq 204) extends to both energy-diffusion (enhancement) and spatial-diffusion (suppression) regimes.

parameters.meaning: cavity (single) mode frequency
parameters.range: electronic strong coupling: ~0.1-5 eV; vibrational strong coupling: ~600-4000 cm^-1 (tuned to molecular vibration)
parameters.default: tuned to resonance with matter transition (Delta-E or omega_0)

parameters.meaning: single-molecule light-matter coupling strength
parameters.range: per molecule small (sub-meV to meV in FP); plasmonic single-molecule can reach near-ultrastrong
parameters.default: set by mode volume and transition dipole, eq 3

parameters.meaning: number of molecules collectively coupled to one cavity mode
parameters.range: CdSe nanoplatelets ~10^3; organic molecules ~10^6-10^11; VSC experiments often >10^10

parameters.meaning: (collective) Rabi splitting = UP-LP energy gap
parameters.range: electronic: ~0.1-1 eV (e.g. ultrastrong FP at hbar Omega_R giving g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c=0.16); VSC: tens to ~100+ cm^-1 (e.g. 97 cm^-1 in Figure 20)

parameters.meaning: unitless coupling parameter (single molecule); collective version g_c sqrt(N)/omega_c
parameters.range: weak/strong (JC valid): <0.1; ultrastrong: 0.1-1; deep-strong: >1; experimental values e.g. eta = 0.047, 0.094, 0.188 (Figure 21)

parameters.meaning: ratio of collective coupling to loss rate (weak vs strong classification)
parameters.range: weak: <1; strong: >1 (Rabi splitting resolvable in absorption)
parameters.default: must be >1 to observe strong coupling

parameters.meaning: effective cavity quantization (mode) volume
parameters.range: Fabry-Perot: diffraction-limited ~(lambda/2)^3 (mirror separation L_z ~ half-wavelength to microns); plasmonic: deeply sub-wavelength (<< lambda^3, enabling single-molecule strong coupling)
parameters.default: FP ~ S*L_z with S = effective area, L_z = mirror separation

parameters.meaning: molecular transition dipole moment
parameters.range: ~1-10 Debye for organic chromophores; from TD-DFT/B3LYP for the lowest ~20 electronic states (Figure 5)

parameters.meaning: ab initio light-matter coupling strength = sqrt(hbar/eps_0 V)
parameters.range: model values e.g. lambda = 0.0033, 0.005, 0.01 a.u. (Figure 27h); set by mode volume

parameters.meaning: quantized perpendicular photon wavevector (FP cavity)
parameters.range: n_z = 1, 2, 3, ... (positive integer); usually single k_z considered (k_z = pi/L_z)

parameters.meaning: incident/propagation angle of photon from mirror normal
parameters.range: -20 to +20 degrees in angle-resolved experiments (small-angle quadratic dispersion regime)

parameters.meaning: reactant-well / molecular vibrational frequency (VSC)
parameters.range: ~1000-2000 cm^-1 (e.g. C=O stretch, Si-C stretch); resonant condition omega_c = omega_0

parameters.meaning: effective refractive index inside cavity
matter electronic energy gap (detuning reference)
cavity photon loss rate (Lindblad/decay)
parameters.range: sets strong-coupling threshold with gamma; partially transparent mirrors increase kappa

parameters.meaning: matter excitation decay rate
parameters.range: molecular dephasing/decay; must satisfy Omega_n >> kappa, gamma for strong coupling

paper.title: Theoretical Challenges in Polaritonic Chemistry
paper.authors: Jacopo Fregoni, Francisco J. Garcia-Vidal, Johannes Feist

pdfFindings.summary: This Perspective frames polaritonic chemistry as an inherently multiscale problem in which molecules are strongly coupled to confined, lossy electromagnetic (EM) modes, producing hybrid light-matter states (polaritons). It distinguishes two experimental regimes: wavelength-scale optical (Fabry-Perot) cavities that reach strong coupling only through collective coupling of macroscopic numbers of molecules (10^6-10^10), and subwavelength plasmonic nanocavities (effective mode volumes down to ~1 nm^3) that can reach strong coupling with few or single emitters but at the cost of very short lifetimes (below ~10 fs) and a strongly inhomogeneous, atomistically-detailed EM field. The authors derive the collective Rabi splitting scaling (Omega_R proportional to sqrt(mu^2 N / V_eff), i.e. set by dipole density, not absolute molecule number), present the correct starting point as non-relativistic QED in Coulomb gauge, and show how repartitioning the Hamiltonian into H_mol + H_cav + H_mol-cav yields the polaritonic potential energy surface (PoPES/PoPES) picture. A central theme is the emergence of a dark-state manifold: for N coupled molecules, the first excited subspace contains N+1 states, of which 2 are bright polaritons (UP/LP) and N-1 are nominally dark; these dark states dominate relaxation, can be near-resonant with and overlap the polaritons (especially for broadband absorbers), and cause ultrafast dephasing (~100 fs) that can make polariton reactivity collapse back to bare-molecule behavior. The paper maps theoretical methods (Table 1) across electronic structure, field quantization (single-mode vs multimode/continuum/macroscopic QED), and nuclear dynamics (MCTDH/grid, surface hopping, Ehrenfest), with explicit warnings: surface hopping fails for many quasi-degenerate states; the dipole self-energy term must NOT be included for longitudinal-Coulomb-mediated plasmonic coupling; single-mode treatments miss multimode/dispersive effects; and treating a plasmonic nanocavity as a lossless photonic mode while neglecting its atomistic structure are both severe approximations. The authors stress that strong coupling is not a binary switch and that a credible simulation must respect losses, collective vs single-molecule scaling of effects, and the dark-state manifold.

key_equations.name: Collective vacuum Rabi splitting (single-mode, N identical two-level molecules)

variables.meaning: vacuum Rabi splitting (energy gap between upper and lower polariton on resonance)
number of identical molecules coupled to the single EM mode
molecular transition dipole moment (lowest two electronic states)
vacuum (single-photon) electric field amplitude of the confined mode
photon energy of the cavity mode (≈ molecular transition energy on resonance)
relative background permittivity of the molecular material
effective mode volume of the confined EM field
variables.typical: below 100 nm^3, down to ~1 nm^3 for picocavities; wavelength^3 scale for optical

key_equations.context: Simplified model: N identical two-level molecules, all coupled identically to a single EM mode, space-dependent field profile ignored, rovibrational motion ignored. Leads to Omega_R proportional to sqrt(mu^2 N / V_eff), i.e. proportional to sqrt(dipole density) — independent of absolute molecule number or cavity geometry at fixed density.

key_equations.name: Full non-relativistic QED coupled Hamiltonian (Coulomb gauge)

variables.meaning: kinetic energies and Coulomb interactions of the charged particles (electrons and nuclei); the standard quantum-chemistry Hamiltonian
radiative (transversal) EM field modes, treated as harmonic oscillators
interaction between charges and EM modes
variables.typical: weak in free space; strong in cavity

key_equations.context: The 'correct' theory. In free space H_ch-EM is weak (main effect = spontaneous emission); standard quantum chemistry treats only H_ch explicitly and emission perturbatively. In cavities H_EM and H_ch-EM must be treated explicitly.

key_equations.name: Repartitioned (cavity) Hamiltonian — starting point of polaritonic chemistry

variables.meaning: the molecule (or system treated in detail) Hamiltonian — electronic + nuclear
combined excitations of the coupled cavity material plus free-space EM modes; if cavity material treated by linear response, diagonalizing H_cav = solving macroscopic Maxwell equations
variables.typical: single or few effective modes with finite linewidth

key_equations.context: Obtained by regrouping parts of H_ch and H_ch-EM (the cavity material and its interaction with the EM field) together with H_EM into a new 'cavity' Hamiltonian. Caveat: a cavity is really a material system; for plasmonic (strongly subwavelength) nanocavities the coupling is mediated by longitudinal Coulomb interactions (an E·d interaction even in minimal coupling), so the dipole-self-energy term should NOT be included. Describes three DOF types: electronic (r), nuclear (R), photonic (q).

key_equations.name: Cavity-mode Hamiltonian (position-space and ladder-operator forms)

variables.meaning: photonic position-space coordinate of the cavity mode
conjugate momentum of the cavity mode

key_equations.context: Harmonic cavity mode. Position-space form natural for Born-Huang/polaritonic-PES treatment; ladder form natural in quantum optics. Quality factor relations: Q = omega_c/kappa, tau = 1/kappa.

key_equations.name: Born-Huang expansion of the polaritonic wavefunction

variables.typical: reaction coordinate in a.u. in figures

variables.meaning: photonic coordinate (fast DOF, grouped with electrons)
nuclear wavepacket amplitude on polaritonic surface k
variables.typical: propagated by MCTDH / grid / surface hopping

variables.meaning: mixed photonic-electronic (polaritonic) eigenstates of the Hamiltonian without nuclear kinetic energy; depend parametrically on R
variables.typical: give polaritonic PES when energies plotted vs R

key_equations.context: Electronic strong-coupling regime: cavity modes near-resonant with electronic transitions, electron+cavity dynamics fast, so grouped together (slow nuclei separated). Yields polaritonic potential energy surfaces (PoPES). In vibrational strong coupling (VSC) one instead groups photonic+nuclear coordinates {q,R} parametric to electrons -> cavity Born-Oppenheimer (CBO) approximation; CBO works well for VSC on the ground state but breaks down for electronic strong coupling where electron-photon hybridization is essential.

parameters.meaning: Number of molecules collectively coupled to one EM mode
parameters.range: 1 (single-emitter plasmonic) to 10^6-10^10 (optical microcavities); 'even more at IR/VSC frequencies'
parameters.default: N=50 used for the dark-state PoPES illustration in Figure 2b

parameters.meaning: Effective EM mode volume
parameters.range: below 100 nm^3 for plasmonic, down to ~1 nm^3 for picocavities; ~wavelength^3 for optical cavities

parameters.meaning: Vacuum Rabi splitting
parameters.range: ~100 meV up to >1 eV (ultrastrong coupling at the high end)

parameters.meaning: Molecular transition dipole moment
Cavity quality factor
parameters.range: Q ≈ 10 (metal mirrors), Q ≈ 1000 (DBR mirrors); plasmonic dominated by losses

parameters.meaning: Cavity mode photon lifetime
parameters.range: ~10 fs (metal Fabry-Perot, plasmonic <10 fs); picosecond scale for DBR (Q~1000)

parameters.meaning: Spontaneous emission lifetime of good molecular emitters at optical frequencies
parameters.range: few ns for mu~10 D; some J-aggregates reach tens of ps at cryogenic temperatures

parameters.meaning: Timescale of vibrational relaxation and thermalization

parameters.symbol: polariton dephasing time to dark manifold
parameters.meaning: Ultrafast decoherence of cavity excitation into dark states
Radiative decay from the lower polariton when decoupled from dark states
parameters.range: ~1 ps (Figure 3b); occurs at roughly half the bare-cavity decay rate

parameters.meaning: Cavity (and resonant molecular transition) frequency
parameters.range: ~1-3 eV optical/electronic; ~0.1-0.4 eV vibrational (IR-active transitions, VSC)

parameters.meaning: Organic dye excitation energy and room-temperature linewidth
parameters.range: excitation ~ few eV; linewidth ~ an order of magnitude smaller (i.e. ~0.1-0.x eV) at room temperature

parameters.meaning: Scalar factor measuring the fraction of the photonic mode filled with molecular material (Rabi splitting proportional to dipole density times this factor)

key_results.claim: Rabi splitting is set by dipole DENSITY, not absolute molecule number or cavity geometry: Omega_R proportional to sqrt(mu^2 N / V_eff). Large and small cavities give the same splitting at fixed density because per-molecule coupling drops as 1/sqrt(V_eff) but more molecules fit.
key_results.evidence: Derivation around eq (1); 'the Rabi splitting is proportional to the dipole density of the molecular material, but does not depend separately on the absolute number of molecules or volume'; can be rewritten via the Lorentz-oscillator dielectric function so it is computable from measurable macroscopic quantities; maximum splitting = bulk-polariton splitting, independent of cavity geometry.

key_results.claim: Strong coupling is NOT a binary switch. A smooth crossover from weak to strong coupling is expected; the crossover point is somewhat arbitrary (especially with disorder/nonidealities), and optimal effects can lie just at the onset of strong coupling rather than deep in it.
key_results.evidence: 'this should not be misunderstood as a binary switch ... a smooth behavior is to be expected as a function of the light-matter coupling strength, with some optimal value that is often clearly within the strong-coupling regime, but can also lie just at its onset.'

key_results.claim: A dark-state manifold necessarily emerges for molecular ensembles. In the first excited subspace there are N+1 single-excitation states: 2 bright polaritons (UP, LP) plus N-1 dark states. Only the collective bright state couples to the cavity, with coupling enhanced by sqrt(N) over the single-molecule value.
key_results.evidence: Figure 2b (N=50) shows a dark-state manifold between UP and LP; 'the molecular bright state and the cavity mode couple to form the typical upper and lower polariton states ... the other orthogonal superpositions ... constitute the N-1 dark states manifold.'

key_results.claim: Dark states are not perfectly dark in realistic systems and inherit polaritonic properties. The simple bright/dark picture is exact only for perfectly degenerate two-level emitters; with distinct nuclear configurations (even nominally identical molecules) the dark states absorb/emit residually and the manifold inherits delocalized polaritonic character; its energy distribution closely matches the bare-molecule absorption spectrum.
key_results.evidence: 'this simple picture is only true in the case of perfectly degenerate two-level emitters ... the dark states are not fully dark and provide residual light absorption and emission ... it has been shown that the dark state manifold inherits some of the delocalized polaritonic properties.'

key_results.claim: Dark-state dephasing can make polariton reactivity collapse to bare-molecule reactivity. When the dark manifold overlaps the polaritons (notably for broadband absorbers), polaritons dephase into cavity-free superpositions on ~100 fs timescales, which becomes the dominant decay channel and yields reactivity essentially equal to that of isolated molecules.
key_results.evidence: 'when the dark states manifold embeds (strongly overlaps with) polaritonic states ... the polaritons dephase into cavity-free superpositions ... This ultrafast loss of coherence to the dark states can become the dominant decay process for polaritons, resulting in reactivity essentially equal to that of isolated molecules.' Figure 3a shows ~100 fs decoherence.

key_results.claim: Design rule for retaining polariton reactivity: Rabi splitting should EXCEED the molecular absorption band so polaritons do not overlap the dark states. Polariton lifetime is then limited by the spread of molecular excitation energies, not by intrinsic excitation lifetime.
key_results.evidence: 'if we want to ensure that photochemical reactions can efficiently take place on the polaritonic potential energy surfaces, the Rabi splitting should be larger than the molecular absorption band ... the lifetime of the polaritons is not limited by the molecular absorption bandwidth since the latter is dominated by the spread of molecular excitation energies, not by the intrinsic lifetime of molecular excitations.'

key_results.claim: When polaritons do NOT overlap the dark manifold, the dominant decay is radiative from the lower polariton at ~half the bare-cavity rate (~1 ps), giving linewidths much smaller than the bare molecular one and lifetimes comparable to photochemical reaction times — opening cavity-modified chemistry.
key_results.evidence: Figure 3b; 'the dominant decay process becomes radiative decay from the lower polariton ... at roughly half the bare-cavity decay rate ... can give line widths much smaller than the bare molecular one. These decay times are comparable to those of several photochemical reactions.'

key_results.claim: The dipole self-energy term must NOT be included for strongly subwavelength (plasmonic) nanocavities. Plasmonic modes are material (collective electron) excitations coupled via longitudinal Coulomb interaction (E·d, unaffected by the Power-Zienau-Woolley transformation), which is the only route to single-molecule strong coupling. Single-molecule strong-coupling results that include the dipole self-energy should be treated with care.
key_results.evidence: 'the Coulomb interaction is not affected by the Power-Zienau-Woolley transformation and, in particular, gives an E·d interaction, even in minimal coupling, without any dipole-self-energy term. The dipole-self-energy term should thus not be included when treating a physical situation corresponding to a strongly subwavelength (e.g., plasmonic) nanocavity ... Results in the literature with single-molecule strong coupling, where the dipole self-energy term is included, should therefore be approached with care.'

key_results.claim: Single-molecule (per-molecule) changes generally depend on the single-molecule coupling strength and are NOT collectively enhanced in many-molecule setups. So few-state expansions suffice for collective optical-cavity problems; convergence of the polaritonic CI requires many excited states only when single-molecule coupling is large (e.g. nanogap/picocavities).
key_results.evidence: 'single-molecule changes usually depend on the single-molecule coupling strength and are not collectively enhanced in many-molecule setups. This effect is thus not expected to be present in such systems, and few-state expansions should work well.'

key_results.claim: Semiclassical surface hopping fails for polaritonic ensembles because of inaccurate transition probabilities among many quasi-degenerate states — precisely the situation when many molecules couple to one mode. Ehrenfest dynamics is more robust in the presence of a large manifold of closely spaced PoPES.
key_results.evidence: 'they are best exploited to describe one to a small number of molecules, as the algorithm fails at grasping collective effects ... The failure is due to the inaccurate evaluation of transition probabilities in the presence of many quasi-degenerate states.' Table 1: surface hopping 'Fails for many degenerate states'; Ehrenfest 'Many degenerate states.'

key_results.claim: Multimode/dispersive cavity treatments are only recently implemented; most methods assume a single cavity mode. Ignoring the material response of the cavity (treating it as boundary conditions / abstract mode) can lead to misleading conclusions.
key_results.evidence: 'A common approximation in the methods discussed above is to rely on the case of a single cavity mode. An extension to the case of multimodal cavities has been implemented only recently.' Also: 'ignoring the simple fact that H_cav also includes a material response can have serious consequences and lead to misleading conclusions.' Table 1 contrasts Single Mode (no geometric profile) vs Dispersive Cavities (many modes, realistic materials, high cost) vs Continuum/Macroscopic QED.

key_results.claim: The Stokes shift / reorganization energy is a critical parameter: if it is large enough that the relaxed excited-state minimum lies below the lower polariton (or other electronic states lie below the polariton), a NEW nonradiative relaxation channel opens, degrading polariton reactivity.
key_results.evidence: 'A further important parameter is the Stokes shift or reorganization energy of the molecules ... When this is large enough for the excited-state energy minimum to lie below the lower polariton energy, it opens a new nonradiative relaxation channel. The same effect is also present when other electronic states lie below the polariton energy.'

key_results.claim: PoPES of an N-molecule ensemble are N x N_m dimensional (N_m = nuclear DOF per molecule), so the dark-state landscape of each state resembles the collective ground state — making it intractable to infer ensemble photochemistry from studying a small subset of molecules; the Holstein + Tavis-Cummings model is the main route to include thousands of molecules but lacks atomistic structural rearrangement (charge transfer).
key_results.evidence: 'the PoPESs in such a setup are N x N_m-dimensional ... The exciton-based approaches present several drawbacks. The most evident is that the nonatomistic description does not allow to grasp structural rearrangements of molecules upon, for example, charge transfers.'

figures_to_emulate.shows: Polaritonic potential energy surfaces (PoPES) for a SINGLE molecule strongly coupled to light. The cavity (blue dotted) and molecule (orange dotted) diabatic states couple to form polaritons (upper and lower polariton curves). A black ground-state curve sits below. Color coding shows photon vs exciton character via the photon number expectation <a-dagger a> (0 = orange/molecule-like, 1 = blue/cavity-like).
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: nuclear reaction coordinate R (a.u.), range approx -1.5 to 1.5. y-axis: Energy (eV). Color scale: <a-dagger a> from 0 (orange, molecular) to 1 (blue, photonic).

figures_to_emulate.shows: PoPES for a molecular ENSEMBLE (N=50) showing the emergence of a DARK-STATE MANIFOLD (dotted curves) between the upper and lower polariton. Demonstrates how N-1 dark states fill the gap and acquire residual polaritonic character; the dark-state landscape resembles the collective molecular ground state. Key figure for visualizing the dark-state problem.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: nuclear reaction coordinate R (a.u.), range approx -1.5 to 1.5. y-axis: Energy (eV). Color scale: <a-dagger a> from 0 (orange) to 1 (blue); dark states drawn as dotted lines.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Dominant process schematic: ULTRAFAST decoherence (~100 fs) of the cavity excitation. A wavepacket launched on the polariton (UP/LP) funnels into the dark-state manifold via overlap between bright and dark states, then undergoes nonradiative processes of the individual molecules. Illustrates the loss-of-coherence pathway that makes reactivity collapse to bare-molecule behavior.
figures_to_emulate.axes: Energy-level schematic (not quantitative axes): vertical = energy, showing UP and LP levels, a dense dark-state ladder to the right, with a ~100 fs timescale arrow and a wavepacket/Gaussian density on the right.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Dominant process schematic for the favorable regime: when the dark manifold is DECOUPLED (energetically separated) from the polaritons, the dominant process is radiative decay from the lower polariton on a ~1 ps timescale. This long-lived delocalized excitation can enable cavity-modified chemistry. Shows the Rabi splitting 'g' separating UP and LP clearly from the dark states.
figures_to_emulate.axes: Energy-level schematic: vertical = energy; UP and LP levels separated by the Rabi splitting (labeled g), dark-state manifold drawn separately, ~1 ps decay arrow, Gaussian densities at right.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Conceptual map of polaritonic chemistry as a multiscale problem. (a) Optical cavities: N molecules + solvent in multimodal Fabry-Perot cavities (collective coupling, solvent effects, multimodal cavities). (b) Plasmonic nanocavities: progression from model molecule + model cavity to realistic molecule + realistic plasmonic cavity. Useful as an orienting schematic for a simulation's regime selector (optical/collective vs plasmonic/single-molecule).
figures_to_emulate.axes: No quantitative axes; conceptual flow diagrams.

uncertainties: This is a Perspective/review article, NOT a primary modeling paper: it contains essentially no original numerical results, no fitted parameter tables, and no benchmark numbers. All 'parameters' are order-of-magnitude figures quoted in prose (e.g. V_eff ~1 nm^3, Q~10/~1000, ~100 fs, ~1 ps). A simulation built from this paper must source concrete potential-energy-surface parameters, Holstein/Tavis-Cummings constants, etc. from the cited primary works (refs 96, 27, 42, etc.), not from this Perspective.

Equation (1)'s field amplitude |E| = sqrt(hbar*omega / (2*eps0*eps_r*V_eff)): I read eps_r as the relative background permittivity of the molecular material as stated in the text; the exact symbol rendering (epsilon_r vs epsilon_b) and whether the factor is exactly 2 (vs including a 1/2 zero-point convention) is as printed but small-factor conventions vary across the literature.

Figures 2 and 3 are schematic/illustrative. Figure 2 energy and R ranges (R in a.u. roughly -1.5 to 1.5; y in eV) are read from the rendered axes but no numeric tick values for the y-axis (Energy) are legible, so absolute energy scales are not recoverable from the figure alone.

The color-bar quantity in Figure 2 is the photon-number expectation <a-dagger a> ranging 0 to 1; I am confident of the 0/1 endpoints but the exact mapping (linear vs other) is not specified in the caption.

In Figure 3b a coupling/splitting is labeled 'g' (lowercase) in the schematic while the main text uses Omega_R for the Rabi splitting; I interpret the figure's 'g' as denoting the polariton splitting (Rabi splitting) but the paper does not separately define a single-molecule coupling constant g in an equation, so this identification is inferred.

N=50 for Figure 2b is taken from the caption; the per-molecule coupling, detuning, and Holstein displacement parameters used to generate that specific figure are not given in this paper (they come from the authors' cited methods papers).

The paper states the dipole-self-energy term should be excluded for plasmonic (longitudinal-Coulomb) coupling but INCLUDED implicitly for transversal free-space modes; the precise boundary of when each treatment applies (hybrid metallodielectric cavities, intermediate confinement) is flagged by the authors as an open problem and is not quantitatively resolved here.

Several timescale numbers ('below 10 fs' plasmonic lifetime, 'tens of ps' J-aggregate cryogenic emission, 'few nanoseconds' emission) are quoted approximately in prose with citations; I transcribed them as given but they are representative, not measured-in-this-work values.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Reaction rate constant k (number of products formed after 2 ps, per ps) as a function of cavity frequency, for the unidirectional reaction PTAF- -> PtMeSi + PA- at 400 K with g0/omega_c = 1.132, overlaid with the 400 K IR transmission spectrum (negative of absorption). Shows the strongly nonmonotonic frequency dependence: inhibition near 200 cm-1 and near 770/856 cm-1, ~2-fold enhancement near 290 and 460 cm-1. Horizontal dotted line is the cavity-free reference g0=0. Black dots with vertical error bars (standard error); vertical magenta dashed lines mark the kinetic-estimate frequencies (198, 461, 856 cm-1).
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: cavity frequency omega_c (cm-1), range ~0 to 1300; left y: rate k (ps-1), range ~25 to 260; right y: IR transmission (arb. units)

figures_to_emulate.shows: Eyring plot: ln(k/T) vs 1/T for four cavity conditions (g0=0 free space black; and resonant cavity frequencies 198, 461, 856 cm-1), with linear fits whose slope/intercept give Delta-H and Delta-S. k is number of products N_p per ps. Used to extract the changes in enthalpy and entropy in Table 1.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: T^-1 (1000/K), range ~1.8 to 3.7; y: ln(k/T) (ln(N_p/ps/K)), range ~ -4.0 to -1.0

figures_to_emulate.shows: Difference in power spectrum of the Si-C bond for PTAF- coupled to the cavity (g0/omega_c=1.132) at 400 K minus the same system outside the cavity, as a 2D map vs cavity frequency. Reveals avoided crossings (polariton hybridizations) along the diagonal omega=omega_c, with substantial Si-C contribution. Gray-dotted guide lines at hbar omega = 290, 520, 770, 856 cm-1.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: cavity frequency omega_c (cm-1), 0 to 1300; y: frequency (cm-1), 0 to ~1300; color: Delta Si-C power spectrum (symlog, +/-10^4)

figures_to_emulate.shows: Temperature-dependent infrared spectrum of the molecule outside the cavity (IR intensity vs vibrational frequency) for T = 10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 K, projected on the cavity polarization. Shows vibrational spectrum sensitivity to temperature; reaction-relevant Si-C modes primarily below 1300 cm-1.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: IR intensity (arb. units, log scale); y: frequency (cm-1), 0 to ~1300

figures_to_emulate.shows: Impact of dynamic electronic polarization: change in rate constant Delta-k vs cavity frequency (black dots, ML+MD, propagated from the 30 QEDFT initial configs of ref 13, g0/omega_c=1.132) contrasted with change in average Si-C distance from QEDFT (blue stars, x50), plus IR transmission spectra from DFT 0 K (blue dashed) and ML+MD 400 K NVE (red solid). Demonstrates ML+MD and QEDFT give similar profile in intermediate-frequency domain but ML+MD is elevated into catalysis near 461 cm-1.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: cavity frequency omega_c (cm-1), 0 to ~1300; left y: Delta k (ps-1), ~ -5 to 15; right-left y: Delta(R_SiC)/g0 (Angstrom) for QEDFT stars; far right y: IR transmission (arb. units)

figures_to_emulate.shows: ML+MD change in rate Delta-k vs cavity frequency repeated for five fundamental light-matter coupling strengths g0/omega_c = 2.264, 1.698, 1.132, 0.556, 0.283. Shows how the frequency-resolved catalysis/inhibition profile scales with coupling strength (roughly g0^2).
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: cavity frequency omega_c (cm-1), 0 to ~1300; y: ML+MD Delta k (ps-1), ~ -2 to 20

figures_to_emulate.shows: Frequency dependence of reaction bursts: 2D map of the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function of changes in number of products |FT{ dN_p(t) dN_p(0) }| at 400 K, g0/omega_c=1.132, vs cavity frequency. Linear dispersion at low frequency (omega=omega_c line) shows reaction bursts correlate with cavity frequency; bending mode at omega=117.7 cm-1 marked. Inset: time-dependent accumulation of products N_p(t) for omega_c = 44, 461, 1207 cm-1 over time in ps.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x: cavity frequency omega_c (cm-1), 0 to ~1300; y: frequency (cm-1), 0 to ~700; color: F<dN_p(t) dN_p(0)> (log, 10^3 to 10^4). Inset x: time (ps); inset y: product N_p

key_results.claim: In free space (no cavity, omega_c=0), NVT ensemble calculations recover a transition-state enthalpy Delta-H-double-dagger = 0.345 eV = 33.3 kJ/mol, in excellent agreement with the experimental estimate Delta-H-double-dagger = 35 +/- 4 kJ/mol; a 0 K DFT NEB barrier instead gives a higher 0.43 eV, illustrating the importance of vibrational/thermal contributions.
