key_results.evidence: Stated in Results: 'transition-state enthalpy of Delta-H-double-dagger = 0.345 eV = 33.3 kJ mol-1 ... in excellent agreement with experimental estimates of Delta-H-double-dagger = 35 +/- 4 kJ mol-1. DFT calculations using the nudged elastic band approach ... provide a higher barrier of 0.43 eV.'

key_results.claim: The cavity-modified reaction rate constant is strongly NONmonotonic in cavity frequency: inhibition regions especially around 200 cm-1 and around 770 and 856 cm-1, and a near 2-fold enhancement (catalysis) around 290 and 460 cm-1. Experiments only probed near 856 cm-1 and confirm the inhibiting trend there.
key_results.evidence: Figure 2C and text: 'pronounced regions of inhibited reactivity, especially around 200 cm-1 and the domain including the 770 and 856 cm-1 vibrations ... a near 2-fold enhancement of the rate constant at around 290 and 460 cm-1 is visible. Experimental investigations are available only near 856 cm-1 and support the general inhibiting trend.'

key_results.claim: Three distinct kinetic regimes are identified via the Eyring analysis: (i) strong inhibition without clear vibrational contribution (198 cm-1) -> large rise in Delta-H, small rise in Delta-S; (ii) vibrationally supported catalysis (461 cm-1) -> increases Delta-S, slightly lowers Delta-H; (iii) vibrationally supported inhibition (856 cm-1) -> raises Delta-H and gives the strongest increase in Delta-S. Quantified in Table 1.
key_results.evidence: Table 1: omega_c=198 cm-1: Delta-Delta-H=+0.052 eV, Delta-Delta-S=+0.22 k_B; 461 cm-1: Delta-Delta-H=-0.003 eV, Delta-Delta-S=+0.32 k_B; 856 cm-1: Delta-Delta-H=+0.030 eV, Delta-Delta-S=+0.56 k_B. Conclusions section enumerates the three regimes.

key_results.claim: The most pronounced inhibition feature at 200 cm-1 is NOT related to any optical excitation and is not connected to the transition-state curvature (estimated ~73 cm-1 from both NEP and DFT), making it a non-resonant kinetic effect.
key_results.evidence: Text: 'the most pronounced feature at 200 cm-1 seems to be not related to any optical excitation and is also not connected to the curvature at the transition state, which we estimate with both our NEP model and DFT calculations to be approximately 73 cm-1.'

key_results.claim: Changes in normal-mode occupation correlate strongly with infrared activity; optically active cavity frequencies (461 and 856 cm-1) produce larger cross-correlation changes than the non-optical 198 cm-1, supporting a mechanism of cavity-mediated redistribution of vibrational energy among optically active modes. The C=C stretch near 1200 cm-1 is strongly affected at 856 cm-1.
key_results.evidence: Figure 3 and Table 2 relative cross-correlation differences: omega_c=198 cm-1 ddC=+0.24% (acc) / +1.09% (abs); 461 cm-1: +66.3% / +11.3%; 856 cm-1: +45.3% / +3.11%. 'This effect is especially apparent for omega_c=856 cm-1 which involves the C=C stretching mode around 1200 cm-1.'

key_results.claim: The ML+MD approach predicts catalysis (enhancement) near 461 cm-1, in CONFLICT with the earlier ab initio QEDFT/Ehrenfest simulations of ref 13, which suggested inhibition there. The discrepancy is attributed to the absence of DYNAMIC electronic polarization in the ML+MD (Born-Oppenheimer ground-state) approach.
key_results.evidence: Text: 'ML+MD calculations show enhanced rate constants at 461 cm-1, an effect that has not been observed in the QEDFT calculations ... our ML+MD calculations are lacking the possibility to describe this dynamic electronic polarization and will thus tend toward a higher reactivity.' Figure 4 contrasts the two.

key_results.claim: Reaction events occur in BURSTS that are correlated with the cavity frequency (linear dispersion in the FT of the product-number autocorrelation), an effect absent in the QEDFT calculations of ref 13. A bending mode at 117.7 cm-1 contributes to the methyl-group rearrangement needed for the reaction.
key_results.evidence: Figure 5 and text: 'the bursts in reaction events are correlated with the cavity frequency ... This trend continues up to a bending mode at 117 cm-1, which contributes to the necessary rearrangement of the methyl groups.' Note: text says ~117 cm-1, figure labels omega = 117.7 cm-1.

key_results.claim: The chosen single-molecule coupling g0 (with g0/omega_c = 1.132) is much larger than experimentally achievable values in Fabry-Perot cavities; results are argued to be partially transferable to plasmonic cavities with large single-molecule couplings. Coupling strength tuned via cavity length L_c with g0 proportional to sqrt(omega_c)/sqrt(L_c) and omega_c proportional to 1/L_c.
key_results.evidence: Text: 'we choose large coupling values g0 = e a0 sqrt(omega_c/2 hbar epsilon0 V_c) ... considerably larger than experimentally achievable values in Fabry-Perot cavities, but the qualitative agreement ... suggests similar microscopic mechanisms.' Strong Coupling section: 'g0 proportional sqrt(omega_c)/sqrt(L_c) and omega_c proportional 1/L_c.'

key_results.claim: Two separate NEP (neuroevolution potential) models were trained on DFT data: one for the PES energy V_PES(r) (and its force F_PES) and one for the dipole moment vector mu(r). Validation parity plots (Figure 1) show good agreement of NEP-predicted forces and dipoles vs DFT. A second NEP trained on a smaller 6-31G* basis reproduces the same qualitative trend, confirming robustness.
key_results.evidence: Figure 1 and text: 'One for predicting the PES V_PES(r), as well as the associated force F_PES(r), and another for predicting dipole moments mu(r).' Limitations: 'the same investigation ... using a smaller NEP model based on DFT data calculated with a smaller electronic basis-set. The overall trend up to 700 fs ... is consistent with the here-presented ML+MD.'

key_equations.name: Effective nuclear-photonic (single-mode cavity) Hamiltonian

variables.meaning: photon creation / annihilation operators of the single cavity mode

variables.typical: set by L_c; V_c = A_c L_c

key_equations.context: Single-mode, single-molecule cavity QED in the long-wavelength (dipole) approximation, Coulomb gauge, after projecting onto the electronic ground state (Born-Oppenheimer); nuclei treated classically. The last two terms are the bilinear light-matter coupling and the dipole self-energy term.

key_equations.name: Minimal-coupling QED Hamiltonian (Coulomb gauge)

variables.meaning: mass and charge of particle i (electrons and nuclei)
transverse electric and magnetic field operators

key_equations.context: Nonrelativistic QED starting point in Coulomb gauge; all charged particles couple via longitudinal Coulomb interaction and to the transverse vector potential. Reduced to the effective Hamiltonian above by single-mode + dipole + Born-Oppenheimer approximations.

key_equations.name: Electronic (PES) force on nucleus j

variables.meaning: Born-Oppenheimer electronic force on nucleus j
gradient with respect to position of nucleus j
potential energy surface, predicted by the NEP PES model

key_equations.context: Always; the cavity-free electronic force, supplied by the NEP PES model in the MD propagation.

key_equations.name: Optical (cavity) force on nucleus j

variables.meaning: optical force on nucleus j from light-matter coupling

variables.units: depends on normalization (sqrt(J)·s units of mode coordinate)

variables.meaning: gradient of the projected dipole moment, from the NEP dipole model
as above (cavity frequency, volume, permittivity, polarization)

key_equations.context: Single-mode classical-nuclei cavity MD. The total force on nucleus j is F^j = F^j_PES + F^j_c (Eq 1 + Eq 2). The term containing q_c is the bilinear coupling; the (eps_c·mu)^2 term is the dipole self-energy contribution. This is the only light-matter interaction in the model.

key_equations.name: Cavity mode displacement (history integral over dipole)

variables.meaning: initial cavity displacement; chosen so cavity force is zero at t=0
molecular dipole moment at earlier time t'
initial cavity momentum, explicitly set to zero

key_equations.context: Self-consistent driven cavity mode; the photon coordinate depends on the full history of the molecular dipole. Initial momentum q_c-dot(0)=0 is enforced; initial state chosen so no electric field (cavity force) exists at t=0.

key_equations.name: Effective single-molecule coupling strength

variables.meaning: cavity frequency, reduced Planck constant, permittivity, cavity volume

key_equations.context: Defines the coupling scale used throughout; the dimensionless ratio g0/omega_c is held constant (=1.132 for main results) while scanning omega_c. Note g0 proportional sqrt(omega_c)/sqrt(L_c) since V_c = A_c L_c and omega_c proportional 1/L_c (Fabry-Perot tuning).

key_equations.name: NEP predicted atomic site energy (neuroevolution potential, single hidden layer)

variables.meaning: atomic site energy of atom i; total energy U = sum_i U_i
descriptor vector component nu for atom i (function of pairwise distances r_ij = r_i - r_j; basis expansion over N-body interactions within cutoff r_c, truncated at 4-body)
descriptor vector length (number of descriptor components)
descriptor cutoff radius for the local atomic environment

key_equations.context: NEP machine-learning model architecture: a single-hidden-layer MLP with tanh activation, mapping a per-atom descriptor (with trainable descriptor parameters) to atomic energy. Used for both the PES model and (extended form) the dipole model.

key_equations.name: NEP PES force on atom i

variables.meaning: force on atom i from the NEP PES model (= F_PES)
site energies of atoms i and j

key_equations.context: Analytic gradient of NEP site energies; gives the PES force used to propagate the MD. Forces and virials obtained by differentiating predicted site energies.

key_equations.name: NEP predicted dipole moment

variables.meaning: molecular dipole moment vector (tensorial NEP output)
gradient of NEP site energy (tensorial extension)

key_equations.context: Tensorial extension of the NEP formalism to predict dipole moments; this mu and its gradient feed the optical force (Eq 2) and the cavity displacement (q_c history integral). Note: equation as printed uses outer product (x) notation; the second form collapses it.

key_equations.name: Eyring rate equation (used for enthalpy/entropy extraction)

variables.meaning: reaction rate constant (here: number of products N_p per ps)
transmission coefficient (here used only to compare with experiment)

variables.typical: 0.345 eV free space; Delta-Delta-H = -0.003 to +0.052 eV (Table 1)

key_equations.context: Transition-state theory; used only to enable comparison with experimentally extracted enthalpy/entropy. Extracted from linear fits in the Eyring plot ln(k/T) vs 1/T (Figure 2D). Authors caution the time-scale-separation assumptions become questionable in the catalyzing domain.

key_equations.name: Relative difference of the cross-correlation function (occupation/IR descriptor)

variables.meaning: relative change of cross-correlation at cavity freq omega_c vs the high-frequency reference at 1251 cm-1
infrared spectrum (absorption) at normal-mode frequency omega_i
accumulated (absolute) difference in normal-mode occupation at omega_i for cavity frequency omega_c
high-frequency reference cavity value omega_c = 1251 cm-1

key_equations.context: Diagnostic measure correlating cavity-induced changes in normal-mode occupation (A=accumulated difference, AAD=accumulated absolute difference) with infrared activity; computed only over the frequency domain shown in Figure 3. Large values for optically active cavity frequencies (461, 856 cm-1) indicate the mechanism is mediated by optically active modes.

key_equations.name: Perturbation-theory cavity-induced ground-state energy shift

variables.meaning: electronic excitation energy for the dominant dipole transition

key_equations.context: Estimates the dynamic electronic polarization contribution missing in the ML+MD (Born-Oppenheimer ground-state) approach. The expansion parameter scales as 1/sqrt(V_c); only g0/omega_c takes appreciable values. Explains why ML+MD lacks an additional barrier increase (extra suppression) present in QEDFT.

parameters.meaning: propagation / analysis time window for the rate constant (number of products)
parameters.range: rate k = products after 2 ps; Fig 5 averages over 2 ps (ref 13 used 0.7 ps); SI consistency to 700 fs

parameters.meaning: reaction-defining Si-C bond length; reaction event = Si-C stretched beyond threshold
parameters.range: reaction event when stretched beyond 3.5 Angstrom; transition-state Si-C distance ~3 Angstrom

parameters.meaning: transition-state (activation) enthalpy in free space
parameters.range: 0.345 eV = 33.3 kJ/mol (NVT); 0.43 eV (0 K DFT NEB); experiment 35 +/- 4 kJ/mol

parameters.meaning: initial-state energy difference above the minimum PTAF- configuration (nonequilibrium F- + PTA start)
parameters.range: 1.34 eV (chosen to shorten reaction time; limits transferability of absolute rates)

parameters.meaning: vibrational curvature (imaginary frequency) at the transition state
bending mode contributing to methyl-group rearrangement, seen in reaction-burst dispersion
electronic excitation energy (gap) for the dominant dipole transition
parameters.range: ~3 eV (separation between ground and excited electronic state at minimum and TS)

parameters.meaning: cavity length and effective mode volume (Fabry-Perot tuning knob: V_c = A_c L_c)
parameters.range: not given numerically; g0 proportional sqrt(omega_c)/sqrt(L_c), omega_c proportional 1/L_c

uncertainties: Exact NEP hyperparameters are NOT in the main text: N_neu (number of hidden neurons), N_des (descriptor length), cutoff radius r_c, training-set size, and the train/test errors are all deferred to the Supporting Information (not provided to me), so I could not transcribe their numeric values.

The units/normalization of the cavity mode coordinate q_c and the precise prefactor conventions in Eqs (2) and the q_c history integral are not fully pinned down dimensionally in the main text; I report them structurally but could not verify SI units of q_c.

Table 1 values are read from the rendered table: omega_c=198 -> Delta-Delta-H=+0.052 eV, Delta-Delta-S=+0.22 k_B; 461 -> -0.003 eV, +0.32 k_B; 856 -> +0.030 eV, +0.56 k_B. The 461 cm-1 enthalpy is essentially zero (-0.003 eV); sign/magnitude is small and could be sensitive to rendering, though I am fairly confident.

Figure axis numeric limits (e.g., exact min/max of rate, Delta-k, Eyring axes, color-scale bounds) are estimated from the rendered figures, not from printed tick values; treat the stated ranges as approximate.

Eq (5) for the dipole is printed with an outer-product/dot-product structure; I transcribed both the printed tensor form and the collapsed r_ij^2 form. The exact index/contraction convention (whether it is a true outer product giving a tensor vs the vector dipole) is slightly ambiguous as rendered.
The bending-mode frequency is quoted as ~117 cm-1 in the text but labeled 117.7 cm-1 in Figure 5; I report both.

The perturbation-theory expression for the dynamic-polarization energy shift on p5408 is written somewhat schematically (proportionality, subscript r, bracket); I transcribed it as printed but the exact bracket/limit notation may be approximate.

All quantitative training/validation metrics, the NVT vs NVE protocol details, Arrhenius plots, and the second (6-31G* basis) NEP results live in the Supporting Information PDF, which was not available to me; my extraction is from the main article only.

The product reaction is written as PTAF- -> PtMeSi + PA- in some figure captions and as the S_N2 deprotection of 1-phenyl-2-trimethylsilylacetylene (PTA) elsewhere; 'FtMeSi + PA-' vs 'PtMeSi' labeling in Fig 2 caption may reflect OCR of the chemical formula and the exact product-species notation should be double-checked against the figure.
pdfFindings.summary: This JACS paper (Schafer, Fojt, Lindgren, Erhart, 2024) builds a machine-learning + molecular-dynamics framework to access CHEMICAL KINETICS of a polaritonic (vibrational strong coupling) reaction: the S_N2 deprotection of 1-phenyl-2-trimethylsilylacetylene (PTA), with the reaction event defined by stretching the Si-C bond beyond 3.5 Angstrom. Two neuroevolution-potential (NEP) neural-network surrogates are trained on DFT data: one predicts the ground-state PES energy/force F_PES (Eqs 3-4) and one predicts the molecular dipole moment mu (Eq 5); these are combined with a custom single-mode cavity propagator (ASE) in which the photon coordinate q_c(t) is driven self-consistently by the history of the dipole, and an optical force (Eq 2) couples nuclei to the cavity via the dipole gradient. The key observable is the reaction rate constant k (number of products formed after 2 ps), mapped vs cavity frequency omega_c at fixed coupling g0/omega_c=1.132, which shows strongly NONmonotonic resonance dependence: inhibition near 200 and 770-856 cm-1, ~2x catalysis near 290-460 cm-1. Eyring analysis (Fig 2D, Table 1) decomposes the rate changes into activation enthalpy and entropy shifts, identifying three distinct kinetic regimes. The free-space barrier Delta-H = 0.345 eV (33.3 kJ/mol) matches experiment (35 +/- 4 kJ/mol). The mechanism is attributed to cavity-mediated redistribution of vibrational energy among optically active modes (correlated with IR activity, Fig 3/Table 2), and reaction events occur in bursts correlated with omega_c (Fig 5). Notably, ML+MD predicts catalysis at 461 cm-1 where prior ab initio QEDFT (ref 13) predicted inhibition; the authors attribute the discrepancy to the absence of DYNAMIC electronic polarization in the Born-Oppenheimer ML+MD approach."

paper.title: Machine Learning for Polaritonic Chemistry: Accessing Chemical Kinetics
paper.authors: Christian Schafer, Jakub Fojt, Eric Lindgren, Paul Erhart
paper.venue: Journal of the American Chemical Society (JACS), 146, 5402-5413; DOI 10.1021/jacs.3c12829

paper.title: Pilot Plan: Physics-Informed Bayesian Optimization for Inverse Design of Polariton-Enhanced Electron Transfer
paper.venue: Working Draft (University of Notre Dame), May 2026 — unpublished pilot plan; built on Sharma & Chen, J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104102 (2024)

pdfFindings.summary: This is a two-week pilot plan to establish physics-informed Bayesian optimization (BO) as an inverse-design methodology for the collective strong-coupling regime of polariton chemistry, governed by the Holstein-Tavis-Cummings (HTC) Hamiltonian. The HTC model describes N identical donor-acceptor molecular pairs (each with electronic donor/acceptor states and a vibrational mode of frequency omega_v with vibronic coupling lambda_v) coupled to a single cavity mode, giving collective coupling G that scales as sqrt(N-1) times the diabatic coupling |T_01| (absorption) or |T_10| (emission). The work uses the closed-form analytical HTC rate expressions of Sharma & Chen (2024) as a millisecond-scale ground-truth 'oracle' (htc_oracle.py): polariton energies Omega_+/-, Hopfield mixing angles, five Marcus-type electron-transfer (ET) rate channels, Fermi-golden-rule polariton relaxation rates Gamma_+/-, cavity-induced quantum yields QY_b and QY_c, and the optimal ensemble size N_max. The central scientific test compares two Gaussian-process surrogates: Surrogate A using four raw parameters (omega_c, |T_01|, g_AD, omega_v) versus Surrogate B using four dimensionless groups (eta_1 = g_AD/omega_c, eta_2 = Delta/Omega_Rabi, eta_3 = lambda_v/omega_v, eta_4 = N|T_01|^2/(hbar omega_c)). BO uses Expected Improvement (with UCB beta=2 robustness check), n_0=10 Latin Hypercube initial points, and n_BO=90 sequential evaluations (100 total), via GPyTorch + BoTorch. The objective is to maximize cavity-induced quantum yield enhancement QY and the width of the enhancement window over N. The headline physics the surrogate must reproduce without training on it is the non-monotonic N-turnover in ET rates (rate proportional to N at small N in the Dicke regime, dropping as e^{-N} at large N), with the peak at N_max.

key_equations.name: Collective coupling (Rabi/light-matter)

variables.meaning: collective light-matter coupling strength (effective Rabi-type coupling)
variables.typical: set by |T_01| and N; grows with sqrt(N-1)

variables.meaning: effective diabatic coupling for absorption channel (vibronic, 0->1)
effective diabatic coupling for emission channel (1->0)
ensemble size (number of identical donor-acceptor molecular pairs)

key_equations.context: After polaron transformation and rotating-wave approximation (RWA), within the HTC model of Sharma & Chen. Each molecule has donor/acceptor states |D_j>,|A_j> with vibrational mode omega_v and vibronic coupling lambda_v.

key_equations.name: Polariton energies (upper/lower)
key_equations.context: Produces upper/lower polariton states |P_+/->. Delta = hbar*omega_c - |E_AD| is the detuning.

key_equations.name: Hopfield mixing coefficients

variables.meaning: Hopfield mixing angle between photonic and matter (excitonic) components
diabatic coupling (T_01 or T_10 depending on channel; written generically as T here)

key_equations.context: Note: (N-1)|T|^2 = G^2 by Eq.(1), so the radicand equals (Delta/2)^2 + G^2. The generic symbol T is ambiguous in the equation (absorption vs emission); paper writes |T|.

key_equations.name: Marcus-type ET rate (oracle kernel)

variables.units: rate (s^-1 or eV/hbar-equivalent; paper does not state explicitly)

variables.meaning: electronic/diabatic coupling matrix element for the given channel
activation energy of the channel (set by polariton-modulated energy surfaces)

key_equations.context: General Marcus-type kernel. Activation energies determined by the polariton-modulated energy surfaces. NOTE: E_r is written as lambda_v^2/(2 omega_v^2), which gives units of eV^-1 if lambda_v and omega_v are both in eV — possible dimensional inconsistency or implicit unit convention; flagged in uncertainties.

key_equations.name: Five ET rate channels
key_equations.latex: k_{\{D\}0\to\{A_1\}0} = \mathcal{K}(\mathcal{T}_{00}, E_{AD}); \quad k_{P_+\to\{A_1\}0} = \mathcal{K}(\mathcal{T}_{01}\cos\theta, \Omega_+); \quad k_{P_-\to\{A_1\}0} = \mathcal{K}(\mathcal{T}_{01}\sin\theta, \Omega_-); \quad k_{P_+\to\{A_1\}1} = \mathcal{K}(\mathcal{T}_{11}\cos\theta, \Omega_+ - \hbar\omega_c); \quad k_{P_-\to\{A_1\}1} = \mathcal{K}(\mathcal{T}_{11}\sin\theta, \Omega_- - \hbar\omega_c)

variables.meaning: rate of each of the five electron-transfer channels (path a = direct donor->acceptor; paths b/c = via upper/lower polariton to vibrational ground/excited acceptor)
diabatic couplings for 0->0 and 1->1 vibronic channels

key_equations.where: Page 3, Eqs. (5),(6),(7); labeled path a, path b/c
key_equations.context: The activation-energy argument of K is the relevant polariton energy (Omega_+, Omega_-, or shifted by hbar*omega_c). Coupling argument is the diabatic coupling weighted by Hopfield cos/sin theta.

key_equations.name: Polariton relaxation rates (Fermi golden rule)

variables.meaning: polariton (upper/lower) relaxation rate via vibrational bath
variables.typical: scales as N^-2 at large N (from (N-1)/N factor combined with theta dependence — see large-N regime)

variables.meaning: bath spectral density (Lorentzian) evaluated at polariton frequency
damping/decay rate of the vibrational mode

key_equations.context: Derived via Fermi's golden rule from the fluctuation Hamiltonian. The sin^2/cos^2 theta selects the matter character; (N-1)/N factor encodes collective dilution. Spectral density J is a Lorentzian peaked at omega_v with width gamma.

key_equations.name: Cavity-induced quantum yields
key_equations.latex: QY_b = \frac{k_{P_+\to A_0}}{K_+} + \frac{k_{P_-\to A_0}}{K_-}, \qquad QY_c = \frac{k_{P_+\to A_1}}{K_+} + \frac{k_{P_-\to A_1}}{K_-}, \qquad K_{\pm} = k_{P_\pm\to A_0} + k_{P_\pm\to A_1} + \Gamma_\pm

variables.meaning: quantum yield into acceptor vibrational ground state (channel b)
quantum yield into acceptor vibrational excited state (channel c)
total decay rate out of upper/lower polariton (ET channels + relaxation)
total cavity-induced quantum yield, QY = QY_b + QY_c
variables.typical: objective; enhancement window defined by QY > QY_threshold (e.g. 0.5)

key_equations.context: Branching ratios of polariton-mediated ET vs total decay. Total cavity-induced yield QY = QY_b + QY_c. The enhancement window is the range of N over which QY > QY_threshold.

key_equations.name: Optimal ensemble size N_max
key_equations.context: Location of the non-monotonic N-turnover. NOTE: dimensionally hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2 has units of 1/eV unless an implicit normalization makes it dimensionless; this directly defines the eta_4 dimensionless group (eta_4 ~ 1 at turnover). Flagged in uncertainties.

key_equations.name: Activation energy at the turnover (barrier-less condition)
key_equations.latex: E_a = \frac{(\Omega_+ - \hbar\omega_c)^2}{4 E_r} \to 0 \quad \text{exactly when the polariton PES crossing becomes barrier-less}

variables.meaning: ET activation energy (Marcus barrier) for the polariton channel

key_equations.where: Page 4, Section 2.3 (mechanistic understanding, N = N_max bullet)
key_equations.context: At N = N_max the Marcus barrier vanishes -> barrier-less ET -> rate is at its peak. This is the headline non-monotonic-turnover mechanism.

key_equations.name: Dimensionless descriptors (physics-informed features, Surrogate B)

variables.meaning: detuning relative to collective Rabi splitting (controls polariton character)
N_max-encoding group; approximately 1 at the turnover
variables.typical: ≈ 1 at turnover (directly encodes N_max via Eq. 44/10)

variables.meaning: collective Rabi splitting; either full sqrt((Delta/2)^2 + G^2) or resonance limit 2*g*sqrt(N) (open question 1)

key_equations.context: The four dimensionless groups that govern the HTC Hamiltonian, chosen because (i) they are the natural variables the Hamiltonian respects, (ii) eta_4 directly encodes the turnover location via Eq. 44, (iii) they absorb rescaling symmetries raw-parameter surrogates must learn from data. NOTE: definition of Omega_Rabi in eta_2 is an explicit open question (full expression vs resonance limit 2*g*sqrt(N)).

parameters.meaning: cavity (photon) frequency
parameters.range: varied (one of the 4 pilot dimensions); full HTC-admissible vs ETSC-experimental range is an open question

parameters.meaning: single-molecule light-matter coupling
Franck-Condon shift / donor-acceptor coupling parameter (raw feature for Surrogate A; appears in eta_1)
ensemble size (number of donor-acceptor molecular pairs)
parameters.range: swept to capture non-monotonic turnover; small-N Dicke regime to large-N suppression

parameters.meaning: reorganization energy / vibronic coupling
donor-acceptor energy gap
vibrational frequency
parameters.range: varied (one of 4 pilot dimensions; enters eta_3)

parameters.meaning: damping rate (of vibrational mode / bath width)
temperature (via thermal energy k_B T)
inverse temperature 1/(k_B T)
detuning, Delta = hbar*omega_c - |E_AD|
diabatic coupling, absorption channel (one of 4 pilot dimensions, as |T_01|)
number of initial Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) design points
Bayesian optimization budget (sequential evaluations after initial design)
Upper Confidence Bound exploration coefficient (robustness-check acquisition)
quantum-yield threshold defining the enhancement window width W (range of N with QY > threshold)
parameters.range: experimentally relevant figure of merit; example 0.5

parameters.meaning: grid-search resolution per dimension (5^4 = 625 total points over the 4D subspace)
number of LHS-sampled training points for dataset generation
held-out contiguous slab test set (extrapolation testing)
scattered held-out points (interpolation testing)
number of random seeds for regret-curve statistics

key_results.claim: The HTC ET rate is non-monotonic in N: rate proportional to N at small N (Dicke regime, G ~ sqrt(N) small, near-degenerate polariton/dark states, barrier-less ET), peaks at N_max where the activation energy E_a = (Omega_+ - hbar*omega_c)^2/(4 E_r) -> 0, then drops as e^{-N} at large N as Rabi splitting pushes polaritons off-crossing.
key_results.evidence: Section 2.3 mechanistic bullets (small N / N=N_max / large N regimes), Eq. (10) for N_max, activation-energy expression on p.4.

key_results.claim: At large N, polariton relaxation Gamma_+/- scales as N^-2 (slower decay) so relaxation dominates over ET, producing suppression — the competition of three regimes (Dicke linear growth, barrier-less peak, exponential suppression) is what makes inverse design non-trivial.
key_results.evidence: Section 2.3 large-N bullet, combined with Eq. (8) (N-1)/N factor.

key_results.claim: Physics-informed dimensionless features (Surrogate B: eta_1..eta_4) are hypothesized to outperform raw-parameter features (Surrogate A: omega_c, |T_01|, g_AD, omega_v) by >= 30% in extrapolation RMSE on a held-out slab, demonstrating qualitative (not merely quantitative) improvement in what the ML model learns.
key_results.evidence: Section 3.2 hypothesis + Success Criterion 2 (p.7).

key_results.claim: Target sample-efficiency result: BO with physics-informed features should reach >= 95% of the grid-search optimum (5^4 = 625 points) using <= 30 oracle evaluations, while random sampling needs >= 300 to reach the same threshold (a 10-30x advantage).
key_results.evidence: Abstract + Success Criterion 1 (p.7); benchmark table (p.6).

key_results.claim: Four-way benchmark: Random sampling (100 evals, no features, non-adaptive), Grid search (625 evals = 5^4, non-adaptive), BO+raw features (100 evals, adaptive), BO+physics features (100 evals, adaptive). Primary metric is regret curves (median +/- IQR over 10 seeds); secondary is extrapolation RMSE on a held-out slab.
key_results.evidence: Section 3.4 benchmark table, Section 3.5.

key_results.claim: Physical-fidelity success criterion: Surrogate B posterior mean must reproduce the non-monotonic N-dependence to within 10% of the analytical curve on held-out points, including correct N_max within a factor of 2 — without being trained on these results.
key_results.evidence: Success Criterion 3 (p.7); validation list Section 3.5.

key_results.claim: Oracle (htc_oracle.py) input/output contract: inputs {omega_c, T_01, T_10, T_00, T_11, g_AD, omega_v, lambda_v, gamma, beta, E_AD, N}; outputs {K_0, K_21, K_31, K_24, K_34 (ET rates), Gamma_plus, Gamma_minus (relaxation), QY_b, QY_c, QY_total, N_max, Omega_plus, Omega_minus, cos_theta, sin_theta}. Evaluation cost O(ms) per point.
key_results.evidence: Section 3.1 code block (p.4) and p.5 evaluation-cost statement.

figures_to_emulate.shows: ET rate (or QY) vs ensemble size N, exhibiting linear growth (k proportional to N) at small N, a peak at N_max, and exponential suppression (k proportional to e^{-N}) at large N. Key validation figure; overlay surrogate posterior mean against analytical ground truth (referred to as 'Fig. 2 in the paper').
figures_to_emulate.axes: x = N (ensemble size, count, ideally log or linear); y = ET rate k (s^-1) or total quantum yield QY (dimensionless). Mark N_max.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Optimal ensemble size N_max as a function of cavity frequency omega_c at fixed other parameters (N_max = 1 + hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2). Correctness-validation item 2.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x = omega_c (cavity frequency / hbar*omega_c in eV); y = N_max (count)

figures_to_emulate.figure: QY enhancement window heatmap in (omega_v, N) plane
figures_to_emulate.shows: Quantum-yield enhancement window across vibrational frequency omega_v and ensemble size N; the region where QY > QY_threshold. Compare surrogate-generated heatmap against analytical ('Fig. 4 in the paper').
figures_to_emulate.axes: x = omega_v (vibrational frequency, eV); y = N (ensemble size, count); color = QY (total cavity-induced quantum yield, dimensionless)

figures_to_emulate.shows: Best objective (QY) found so far vs number of oracle evaluations, comparing 4 methods (random, grid, BO+raw, BO+physics), median +/- IQR over 10 seeds. Demonstrates sample-efficiency advantage of physics-informed BO.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x = number of evaluations (1 to ~100); y = best objective value found so far (QY or regret = optimum - best-found). Shaded interquartile bands.

figures_to_emulate.figure: Polariton energy diagram Omega_+/- vs detuning/coupling
figures_to_emulate.shows: Upper/lower polariton energies Omega_+/- = Delta/2 +/- sqrt((Delta/2)^2 + G^2) as function of detuning Delta or collective coupling G, showing the avoided crossing (anti-crossing) and Rabi splitting. Useful for interactive simulation of Eq.(2).
figures_to_emulate.axes: x = Delta (detuning, eV) or G (collective coupling, eV); y = Omega_+/- (polariton energy, eV)
figures_to_emulate.page: 3 (Eq. 2) — not an explicit paper figure but directly simulable

uncertainties: This is a working-draft pilot PLAN, not a results paper. Most quantitative claims (10-30x sample efficiency, >=30% RMSE improvement, 95% of optimum in <=30 evals) are TARGETS / success criteria, not measured results. No actual experimental/numerical results are reported.

Dimensional consistency concerns: E_r = lambda_v^2/(2 omega_v^2) (Eq. 4) and N_max = 1 + hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2 (Eq. 10) appear dimensionally non-standard if lambda_v, omega_v, hbar*omega_c, and T_01 are all in eV (would give eV^-1 and eV^-1 respectively rather than energy/dimensionless). There is likely an implicit unit convention (e.g., hbar=1, energies in units of omega_v) or a transcription simplification in the draft. I transcribed exactly as printed.

The generic coupling symbol in the Hopfield coefficients (Eq. 3) is written |T| without a 0/1 subscript, so it is ambiguous whether T_01 (absorption) or T_10 (emission) is meant; (N-1)|T|^2 = G^2 by Eq.(1).

Omega_Rabi in the eta_2 dimensionless group is explicitly UNRESOLVED in the paper (Open Question 1, p.8): either the full expression sqrt((Delta/2)^2 + G^2) or the resonance limit 2*g*sqrt(N). I cannot give its definitive form.

Units of several parameters (gamma = 0.065, beta, omega_c) are not all explicitly stated; I inferred eV from context (E_AD = 1.0 eV, 1/beta = 0.025 eV stated explicitly on p.6). gamma=0.065 has no units printed.
The exact rate units (s^-1 vs natural units) for K and the k channels are not stated in the paper; the oracle is described only as O(ms) evaluation cost, not as producing rates in specific units.
The mapping between oracle outputs (K_0, K_21, K_31, K_24, K_34) and the five labeled ET channels in Eqs. (5)-(7) is implied but not explicitly given index-by-index in the text.

The 'paper' being reproduced (Sharma & Chen, J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104102, 2024) is the source of Eqs. 21, 35-37, 40-41, 44, 45-46 and Figs. 2-4; those original equations/figures were NOT in this PDF, so I could only extract the versions quoted in this pilot plan.

The PDF is 9 pages total (confirmed by reading); no parameter values beyond E_AD=1.0 eV, gamma=0.065, 1/beta=0.025 eV, and the BO hyperparameters were given numerically — the 4D pilot parameter RANGES (min/max for omega_c, |T_01|, g_AD, omega_v) are not specified in the document (flagged as Open Question 4).

figures_to_emulate.shows: Schematic of the coupled-oscillator / strong-coupling mechanism. Bright-mode spectrum S_b (single Lorentzian peak, FWHM gamma_b, blue) on the left; coupled-mode spectrum S_c on the right showing a two-peak split (red) when coupling occurs; dark-mode spectrum S_d (gray dashed, FWHM gamma_d). The anticrossing-style energy-level cartoon shows the bright mode and dark mode splitting by 2g into upper/lower polariton branches; coupling coefficient annotated c = 4g/(gamma_b + gamma_d). This is the conceptual figure to reproduce as an interactive: sliders for g, gamma_b, gamma_d that show the single peak splitting into two and c crossing 1.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: Frequency; y-axis: spectral amplitude (absorption / intensity, arbitrary units). Peak separation in S_c equals 2g; FWHM of S_b is gamma_b, of S_d is gamma_d.

figures_to_emulate.shows: False-color (heat) map of the absorption spectrum showing the anticrossing: as the dark-mode central frequency omega_d sweeps, the coupled-mode spectrum exhibits an avoided crossing (two branches that repel near resonance). Directly reproducible by computing coupled-oscillator absorption vs omega_d.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: omega_d (dark-mode central frequency), approx 43-49 THz; y-axis: Frequency, approx 42-50 THz; color: absorption amplitude.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Predicted vs simulated absorption spectra for three hBN structures in distinct regimes: strong coupling (two well-separated peaks, red), weak coupling (partially split, orange), uncoupling (single peak, light blue). Good interactive target: show absorption spectrum shape changing across the three regimes.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: Frequency (THz), approx 41-42.5 THz; y-axis: Absorption (arb., 0 to ~0.6).

figures_to_emulate.shows: False-color map of predicted absorption spectrum vs hBN thickness t_z (with t_x=930 nm, t_y fixed). Shows transition from weak coupling to strong coupling; strong coupling onset annotated at t_z > 60 nm. Direct map of a STRUCTURAL geometry variable (layer thickness) to the coupling spectrum.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: t_z (nm), approx 60-150 nm; y-axis: Frequency (THz), approx 41-42 THz; color: absorption amplitude; vertical line marks weak/strong boundary at t_z ~ 60 nm.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Strong-weak coupling transition boundaries in the (t_y, t_g) structural-parameter plane at fixed t_x=930 nm, t_z=110 nm. E shows lower-bound (LB) boundary, F shows upper-bound (UB) boundary, G overlays both with a 99.6% confidence band and real FEM-verified transition points (red dots) inside the band. Color bar = coupling coefficient c (0-3). This is the core design-space map: geometry (width t_y, gap t_g) -> coupling coefficient c, with c=1 contour as the strong/weak boundary.
figures_to_emulate.axes: x-axis: t_y (nm), approx 205-250 nm; y-axis: t_g (nm), approx 40-220 nm (log-spaced ticks 40,130,220); color: coupling coefficient c (0-3); contour at c=1 is the transition boundary.

figures_to_emulate.shows: Experimental near-field (nanoIR / AFM-IR) spectra at six points (Points 1-6) along the gate-shaped hBN structure (4D, stacked spectra showing two-peak coupled-mode response), and the extracted coupling coefficient c at each point (4E) all lying in the strong-coupling region (c between ~1.6 and ~2.4, above the c=1 threshold). Reproducible as a coupled-oscillator fit to measured two-peak spectra returning c per spatial point.
figures_to_emulate.axes: 4D x-axis: Frequency (THz) approx 40-42 THz, y-axis: NanoIR signal (a.u., stacked/offset). 4E x-axis: Points (1-6), y-axis: c (~0.8 to 2.4) with strong/weak threshold line at c=1.

key_equations.name: Coupling coefficient (FWHM form)

variables.meaning: Coupling coefficient; the dimensionless figure of merit. c>1 strong coupling, 0<c<=1 weak coupling, c=0 uncoupling
variables.typical: 1.6-2.4 (measured strong-coupling hBN points); up to ~3 in design maps

variables.meaning: Interaction potential (coupling strength) between bright mode and dark mode; half the peak splitting
variables.units: frequency (THz) or energy (eV) depending on spectral axis
variables.typical: set so 2g equals the two-peak separation in S_c

variables.meaning: Full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the bright-mode spectrum S_b (bright-mode decay/linewidth)
variables.typical: comparable to gamma_d, sub-THz to ~THz scale

variables.meaning: FWHM of the dark-mode spectrum S_d (dark-mode decay/linewidth)

key_equations.context: Coupled-oscillator model. Equivalent to c = 2g/gamma_avg using gamma_avg = (gamma_b+gamma_d)/2. Defines the three coupling regimes. 2g is read directly as the separation between the two peaks in the coupled-mode spectrum S_c when the central frequencies of S_b and S_d are identical (on resonance).

key_equations.name: Coupling coefficient (average-FWHM form)

variables.meaning: Bright-dark interaction potential (coupling strength); 2g = two-peak separation in S_c on resonance
Average FWHM (average decay factor) of bright and dark modes

key_equations.where: page 2 (text) and Fig. 2A physics-loss box; page 7 (Materials and Methods, L_phy)
key_equations.context: Primary physical law enforced by the PINN as a physics-informed constraint. gamma_avg = (gamma_b + gamma_d)/2. This is the relation the PINN learns/extrapolates so it can predict c directly from a coupled-mode spectrum without measuring decay factors separately.

key_equations.name: Average decay factor (average FWHM)

variables.meaning: Average decay factor used in the coupling coefficient definition

key_equations.context: Definition of the average FWHM (referred to as the decay factor) of the bright- and dark-mode spectra.

key_equations.name: PINN total training loss (Eq. 1)
key_equations.latex: L_1(\theta_1) = L_{cross}(\theta_1) + L_c(\theta_1) + L_g(\theta_1) + L_{\gamma_{avg}}(\theta_1) + L_{phy}(\theta_1)

variables.meaning: PINN model parameters to be learned
Cross-entropy classification loss for coupling type z in {1,2}
MSE regression losses for the quantities c, g, and gamma_avg respectively

key_equations.context: Total objective for the first-stage physics-informed neural network. Input is the coupled-mode spectrum; outputs are c, g, gamma_avg plus a classification z.

key_equations.name: Cross-entropy classification loss (Eq. 2)

variables.meaning: Number of training samples in PINN
variables.typical: 400 initial, augmented to 3200; 572 total coupled-mode spectra split 7:1:2

variables.meaning: True one-hot label: 1 if coupling type of sample i is z, else 0
variables.typical: z in {1,2}; z=1 uncoupling (c=0), z=2 coupling (c>0)

variables.meaning: Predicted probability that sample i belongs to coupling type z

key_equations.context: Classifies each spectrum as uncoupling (z=1) vs coupling (z=2). If z=1, c is forced to 0; otherwise the regression head predicts c, g, gamma_avg.

key_equations.name: MSE regression loss for u in {c,g,gamma_avg}

variables.meaning: True value of quantity u (one of c, g, gamma_avg) for sample i
Predicted value of quantity u for sample i
Quantity index, u in {c, g, gamma_avg}

key_equations.context: Per-quantity mean squared error losses L_c, L_g, L_{gamma_avg}.

key_equations.name: Physics-informed loss (PINN constraint)

variables.meaning: Predicted coupling coefficient for sample i
Predicted coupling strength for sample i
Predicted average decay factor for sample i

key_equations.context: Soft-enforces the physical law c = 2g/gamma_avg on the network's own predictions, ensuring physical consistency and enabling extrapolation to the hBN system where true c labels are inaccessible.

key_equations.name: Cauchy-Lorentz PDF (single peak)

variables.meaning: Scale parameter related to the FWHM of the peak (half-width at half-maximum)

key_equations.context: Lorentzian lineshape used to fit the spectral curves and reduce a high-dimensional spectrum to low-dimensional shape parameters fed to the NN-MTGP.

key_equations.name: Single-peak (uncoupling) spectrum model (Eq. 3)

variables.meaning: Physical response (absorption) spectrum, single peak, uncoupling regime z=1
Amplitude/scaling parameter of the single Lorentzian
Central frequency and scale (FWHM-related) of the single peak

key_equations.context: Used when the spectrum has a single peak (uncoupling, z=1). The Cauchy-Lorentz parameters y_CL1 = (omega_0, gamma_0, a_0, b_0)^T are estimated via Non-Linear Least Squares.

key_equations.name: Two-peak (coupling) spectrum model (Eq. 4)
key_equations.latex: f(\omega) = a_l\, p(\omega; \omega_l, \gamma_l) + a_r\, p(\omega; \omega_r, \gamma_r) + b_c

variables.meaning: Coupled-mode spectrum with two peaks (coupling regime z=2)
Central frequencies of the left (lower polariton) and right (upper polariton) peaks
Scale (FWHM-related) parameters of the left and right peaks
Amplitudes of the left and right peaks

key_equations.context: Weighted sum of two Cauchy-Lorentz distributions; the coupled-mode shape parameters y_CL2 = (omega_l, gamma_l, a_l, omega_r, gamma_r, a_r, b_c)^T are the multitask GP regression response. The peak separation omega_r - omega_l corresponds to 2g, directly linking lineshape to coupling strength.

key_equations.name: NN-MTGP total loss (Eq. 5)

variables.meaning: Full NN-MTGP parameter set, theta_2 = (theta_NN, theta_CL1, theta_CL2,c)
Cross-entropy loss of the neural network classifier for coupling type z
Negative log-likelihood losses of the two multitask Gaussian processes (MTGP-1 predicts y_CL1; MTGP-2 jointly predicts y_CL2 and c)

key_equations.context: Second-stage model mapping structural parameters x = (t_x, t_y, t_z, t_g) to coupling coefficient c and coupled-mode shape parameters, with uncertainty quantification.

key_equations.name: MTGP multitask GP model

variables.meaning: Response for task j, modeled as a zero-mean GP
Covariance (kernel) function between inputs x and x' (structural-parameter space)
Covariance between task j and task l (intertask covariance)
d-dimensional structural input vector (here d=4: t_x,t_y,t_z,t_g)

key_equations.context: Multitask Gaussian process; full covariance K = K_T (x) K_X (Kronecker product), trained by minimizing negative log marginal likelihood. Provides predictive mean mu(x) and variance sigma^2(x) for c.

key_equations.name: MTGP predictive distribution

variables.meaning: Predicted response vector at new input x^*
Posterior predictive mean = prediction of y^*

key_equations.context: Bayesian predictive equations giving both mu(x) and sigma(x) used to build confidence bands on the coupling coefficient and the strong-weak transition boundary.

key_equations.name: Coupling-coefficient predictive distribution

variables.meaning: Coupling coefficient as a function of structure x
Predictive mean of the coupling coefficient

key_equations.context: NN-MTGP assumes a normal predictive distribution for c at each structure x; the c=1 contour of mu(x) defines the strong-weak transition boundary.

key_equations.name: Strong-weak transition boundary set

variables.meaning: Set of structures forming the strong-weak coupling transition boundary
Design space, Omega subset of R^4 (the four structural parameters)

key_equations.context: Defines the target the NN-MTGP identifies: the locus in geometry space where coupling transitions between weak (c<=1) and strong (c>1).

key_equations.name: Confidence interval / bounds on c (3-sigma)

variables.meaning: Lower bound of the 99.7% CI for c(x) -> lower-bound transition boundary (LB) where l(x)=1
Upper bound of the 99.7% CI for c(x) -> upper-bound transition boundary (UB) where u(x)=1
Predictive mean and SD of c at structure x

key_equations.context: 99.7% (3-sigma) confidence interval. The region between the LB boundary (l(x)=1) and UB boundary (u(x)=1) is the confidence band B_c of the true boundary B (shown in Fig. 3 E-G).

parameters.meaning: Length of the hBN gate-shaped polariton structure (horizontal rod, x-direction); the horizontal rod hosts the bright mode (electric dipole mode)
parameters.range: Examples used: 740, 807, 900, 930, 1220 nm. Held fixed at 930 nm for the t_y-t_g boundary maps; fabricated device = 930 nm

parameters.meaning: Width of the structure (vertical rod, y-direction); the vertical rod hosts the dark mode (electric quadrupole mode)
parameters.range: Map range ~205-250 nm; example values 154, 166, 219, 240 nm; fabricated device = 240 nm

parameters.meaning: hBN layer thickness (out-of-plane). Controls strength of coupling: strong coupling (c>1) may occur when t_z > 60 nm
parameters.range: Map range ~60-150 nm; example values 75, 85, 110, 145 nm; fabricated device = 110 nm; strong-coupling onset at t_z > 60 nm

parameters.meaning: Nanogap between the horizontal and vertical rods (gap where coupling/field confinement is maximized; bright-to-dark energy transfer occurs here)
parameters.range: Map range ~40-220 nm; example values 40, 100, 115 nm; fabricated device = 40 nm

parameters.meaning: Coupling coefficient (output figure of merit). c>1 strong, 0<c<=1 weak, c=0 uncoupling
parameters.range: 0 to ~3 across design maps; measured strong-coupling device: 1.6-2.4

parameters.meaning: Coupling-type classification label. z=1 uncoupling (c=0); z=2 coupling (c>0, includes both weak 0<c<=1 and strong c>1)
Dark-mode central (resonance) frequency; sweeping it produces the anticrossing in the coupled-mode spectrum
parameters.range: ~43-49 THz (Fig. 2B axis); spectra centered near 46 THz

parameters.meaning: Spectral window over which COMSOL absorption/coupled-mode spectra are computed for the hBN system
Tunable pulsed laser spectral range used in the nanoIR experiment
Periodic-boundary unit cell in FEM simulation; three chosen dimensions in both length and width directions
parameters.range: not numerically specified beyond 'three chosen dimensions'

parameters.meaning: Spatial resolution of the near-field measurement, set by AFM tip radius of curvature
Approximate degrees of freedom per COMSOL field calculation

key_results.claim: Coupling coefficient is defined purely from spectral observables via the coupled-oscillator model: c = 4g/(gamma_b + gamma_d) = 2g/gamma_avg, eliminating the need to separately measure decay factors. Three regimes: strong (c>1), weak (0<c<=1), uncoupling (c=0).
key_results.evidence: Defined in Fig. 1A inset and Results text; 2g equals the separation of the two peaks in the coupled-mode spectrum S_c when bright and dark central frequencies coincide.

key_results.claim: hBN layer thickness t_z is the dominant geometric knob for entering strong coupling: strong coupling (predicted c>1) occurs when t_z > 60 nm (at t_x=930 nm).
key_results.evidence: Fig. 3D false-color map and Results text: 'Strong coupling (predicted c>1) may occur when t_z > 60 nm.'

key_results.claim: The strong-weak transition boundary in geometry space is the c=1 contour, B = {x in Omega | c(x)=1}, with a 99.7% (3-sigma) confidence band between lower (mu-3sigma=1) and upper (mu+3sigma=1) bounds; FEM-verified true boundary points fall within the band.
key_results.evidence: Fig. 3 E-G; Results text defining B and confidence band B_c; real transition points (red dots) nearly all lie within B_c.

key_results.claim: NN-MTGP achieves 98.4% accuracy in identifying strong-weak transition boundaries, 97.5% overall classification accuracy (near-perfect recall for coupling z=2), R^2 = 0.995 and MAPE = 0.016 (accuracy 0.984) for coupling-coefficient regression.
key_results.evidence: Abstract/Results and Fig. 3C (R^2=0.995, MAPE=0.016); PINN stage gives R^2 ~ 1, MAPE=0.004 (Fig. 2D), 100% classification accuracy.

key_results.claim: ~10^4-fold computational speedup vs FEM: NN-MTGP predicts coupling coefficient and spectrum in 0.03 s vs ~1,200 s for a FEM (COMSOL) simulation.
key_results.evidence: Results text: 'Compared to FEM simulations, which take ~1,200 s per structure, NN-MTGP predicts ... in just 0.03 s, achieving a 10^4-fold improvement.'

key_results.claim: Physical coupling mechanism: a bright mode (electric dipole) in the horizontal rod (x-direction) couples to a dark mode (electric quadrupole) in the vertical rod (y-direction) across the nanogap; the gap t_g is where field intensity and bright-to-dark energy transfer are maximized.
key_results.evidence: Results text (page 6) and Fig. 4A,B field maps (Abs(E), Real(E_z)).

key_results.claim: Experimentally fabricated gate-shaped hBN-on-SiO2 structure with parameters (t_x, t_y, t_z, t_g) = (930, 240, 110, 40) nm operates in strong coupling: near-field AFM-IR spectra at six points yield c in the range 1.6-2.4, all exceeding the c=1 strong-coupling threshold.
key_results.evidence: Results (page 6) and Fig. 4C-E; nanoIR spectra (Fig. 4D) two-peak coupled-mode responses, extracted c values (Fig. 4E) between ~1.6 and 2.4.

key_results.claim: PINN trained on labeled dataset A (PM coupling system, complete spectra) extrapolates to generate synthetic coupling coefficients for the hBN dataset B where true c is inaccessible (only coupled-mode spectra available); synthetic c_syn closely matches reference c_ref.
key_results.evidence: Results and Fig. 1C; SI Appendix Fig. S2D shows minimal discrepancy between c_syn and c_ref; 572 PM coupled-mode spectra (400->3200 augmented) and 2,100 hBN coupled-mode spectra.

key_results.claim: hBN permittivity obtained by fitting experiment with a Lorentzian model; simulations use COMSOL with periodic boundary conditions, transverse magnetic excitation, ~60,000 DOF, ~1,200 s solve time on a 2-core CPU.
key_results.evidence: Materials and Methods, Electromagnetic Simulations and Theory.

paper.title: Designing strongly coupled polaritonic structures via statistical machine learning
paper.authors: Yang Yang, Xiangdong Guo, Shu Zhang, Qing Dai, Ke Deng, Jun S. Liu
paper.venue: PNAS (Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences), Vol. 122, No. 51, e2526690122

pdfFindings.summary: The paper presents a two-stage hybrid machine-learning framework to design strongly coupled polaritonic structures (hexagonal boron nitride phonon-polariton 'gate-shaped' resonators) without having to measure decay factors directly. The central physics is the coupled-oscillator model: the coupling coefficient c = 4g/(gamma_b + gamma_d) = 2g/gamma_avg classifies systems into strong (c>1), weak (0<c<=1), and uncoupling (c=0) regimes, where 2g is the splitting between the two peaks of the coupled-mode spectrum and gamma_b, gamma_d are the bright/dark-mode FWHMs. Stage 1 is a physics-informed neural network (PINN) trained on a polariton-molecule dataset with full spectra; it learns and physics-constrains (via L_phy) the law c = 2g/gamma_avg from coupled-mode spectra and extrapolates synthetic coupling labels to the hBN system where true c is inaccessible. Stage 2 is a neural-network-enhanced multitask Gaussian process (NN-MTGP) that maps four STRUCTURAL geometry variables x = (t_x length, t_y width, t_z hBN thickness, t_g nanogap) to the coupling coefficient c and the Cauchy-Lorentz lineshape parameters, with uncertainty quantification. The model identifies the strong-weak transition boundary B = {x | c(x)=1} with 98.4% accuracy and a 99.7% confidence band, runs ~10^4 times faster than FEM (0.03 s vs ~1,200 s). Key geometry-to-physics links: strong coupling onsets at hBN thickness t_z > 60 nm; the nanogap t_g is where bright-dipole (horizontal rod) to dark-quadrupole (vertical rod) energy transfer and field confinement are maximized. A fabricated hBN device with (t_x,t_y,t_z,t_g)=(930,240,110,40) nm experimentally shows strong coupling with measured c between 1.6 and 2.4 via AFM-IR near-field spectroscopy.
uncertainties: The symbol g is described both as the 'interaction potential' and effectively as half the two-peak separation (2g = peak split). The paper states 2g equals the peak difference in S_c when bright and dark central frequencies are identical (on resonance); off-resonance the relation between observed split and g is not given explicitly in the main text.

Exact numeric values of gamma_b, gamma_d, g, and gamma_avg for specific structures are not tabulated in the main text — only their defining relations. Typical magnitudes are inferred from the ~40.5-43.5 THz spectral window but not stated as numbers.

Fig. 2B, 3B, 3D, 3E-G, 4D, 4E axis ranges were read from the rendered figures, not from numeric tables; my stated ranges (e.g., t_y ~205-250 nm, t_g ~40-220 nm, t_z ~60-150 nm) are approximate axis estimates and may be off by the figure tick spacing.

The unit-cell dimensions used in the FEM periodic-boundary simulation are described as 'three chosen dimensions in both length and width directions' without explicit numeric values in the main text (likely in SI Appendix, which was not provided).

The functional/closed-form mapping from geometry (t_x, t_y, t_z, t_g) to g and gamma_avg is NOT given analytically — it is learned by the NN-MTGP. The only explicit geometry->physics statements are qualitative thresholds (e.g., strong coupling for t_z > 60 nm) and the mechanism (bright dipole in horizontal rod couples to dark quadrupole in vertical rod across gap t_g). There is no analytic DBR/mirror-reflectivity or cavity-length formula in this paper; the cavity is the hBN gate-shaped phonon-polariton resonator, not a classical DBR/Fabry-Perot stack, so 'mirror thickness / DBR parameters' map onto hBN layer thickness t_z and rod geometry rather than distributed-Bragg-reflector layer counts.
The hBN Lorentzian permittivity fit parameters (oscillator strength, phonon frequency, damping) are referenced (refs 14, 38) but not numerically given in the main text.

The fabricated device structural values appear twice with slight context: text on page 6 gives (t_x,t_y,t_z,t_g)=(930,240,110,40) nm; Fig. 3G design region and Fig. 4 confirm these, with t_z~110 nm also shown in the AFM height profile. I treated 930/240/110/40 nm as the device parameters.

Three example test structures in Fig. 3 are listed as (t_x,t_y,t_z,t_g) = (740,219,85,75), (1220,153,79,100), and (807,154,145,100) nm in the text; the pairing/order of which is 'bottom to top' in Fig. 3B is as stated but I could not independently verify each spectrum-to-structure assignment from the figure.

webFindings.facet: History & timeline of polariton and cavity-QED physics (verified history tree)
webFindings.summary: This is a verified, primary-source timeline of the polariton / cavity-QED lineage from its theoretical origins to modern polaritonic chemistry. ORIGIN OF THE POLARITON CONCEPT (phonon-polaritons): the coupled photon-phonon dispersion in ionic crystals was first obtained by Kirill B. Tolpygo (1950, quantum approach) and independently by Huang Kun (1951, classical approach). EXCITON-POLARITON THEORY: John J. Hopfield, Phys. Rev. 112, 1555 (1958), gave the quantum theory of exciton-photon mixed modes and coined the term "polariton" (S. I. Pekar's earlier "light-exciton" name was supplanted). CAVITY-QED FOUNDATIONS: R. H. Dicke, Phys. Rev. 93, 99 (1954) introduced superradiance / collective spontaneous emission; E. T. Jaynes & F. W. Cummings, Proc. IEEE 51, 89 (1963) gave the single-atom single-mode model; M. Tavis & F. W. Cummings, Phys. Rev. 170, 379 (1968) extended it to N emitters. MICROCAVITY STRONG COUPLING: C. Weisbuch, M. Nishioka, A. Ishikawa & Y. Arakawa, PRL 69, 3314 (1992) observed vacuum-Rabi (normal-mode) splitting of quantum-well excitons in a semiconductor microcavity — the birth of microcavity polaritons. POLARITON LASING / CONDENSATION: A. Imamoglu et al., Phys. Rev. A 53, 4250 (1996) proposed the polariton laser; H. Deng et al., PNAS 100, 15318 (2003) and Christopoulos et al., PRL 98, 126405 (2007, room temperature) advanced lasing; J. Kasprzak et al., Nature 443, 409 (2006) reported polariton Bose-Einstein condensation. POLARITONIC CHEMISTRY: J. A. Hutchison, T. Schwartz, C. Genet, E. Devaux & T. W. Ebbesen, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51, 1592 (2012) — birth of the field, modifying chemical landscapes by coupling to vacuum fields (electronic strong coupling). VIBRATIONAL STRONG COUPLING CHEMISTRY: A. Thomas et al. (Ebbesen group), Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 55, 11462 (2016) showed ground-state reaction-rate modification under VSC; A. Thomas et al., Science 363, 615 (2019) showed VSC tilting/site-selectivity. THEORY MACHINERY: Galego, Garcia-Vidal & Feist, PRX 5, 041022 (2015); Flick, Ruggenthaler, Appel & Rubio, PNAS 114, 3026 (2017, QEDFT); Mandal, ..., Huo, Chem. Rev. 123, 9786 (2023). CAVITY-MODIFIED ELECTRON TRANSFER / HOLSTEIN-TAVIS-CUMMINGS: culminating in S. K. Sharma & H.-T. Chen (Notre Dame), J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104109 (2024). All nodes carry author, year, journal, and one-line contribution; uncertain dates are flagged below.

key_equations.name: Hopfield polariton dispersion (exciton-photon coupling)
key_equations.context: Polariton (mixed exciton-photon) dispersion near an excitonic resonance; the avoided crossing of the photon line and exciton level is the defining polariton feature. Exact functional form transcribed schematically; the paper derives the dielectric-constant contribution of excitons from which this dispersion follows.

key_equations.name: Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian
key_equations.latex: H = \hbar\omega_c\, a^\dagger a + \frac{1}{2}\hbar\omega_0\, \sigma_z + \hbar g\,(\sigma_+ a + \sigma_- a^\dagger)
key_equations.context: Rotating-wave approximation, single two-level atom coupled to a single quantized cavity mode. Foundational cavity-QED model. The standard modern operator form is given here; the 1963 paper formulates the equivalent beam-maser problem.

key_equations.name: Vacuum Rabi (normal-mode) splitting at resonance

variables.meaning: Rabi splitting between upper and lower polariton branches

key_equations.context: On-resonance splitting observed in a semiconductor quantum-well microcavity; strong-coupling regime where Omega_R exceeds cavity and exciton linewidths. The vacuum (n=0) limit is the defining vacuum-Rabi splitting.

key_equations.name: Tavis-Cummings collective coupling (sqrt-N enhancement)
key_equations.latex: H = \hbar\omega_c a^\dagger a + \sum_{j=1}^{N}\frac{\hbar\omega_0}{2}\sigma_z^{(j)} + \hbar g\sum_{j=1}^{N}\left(\sigma_+^{(j)} a + \sigma_-^{(j)} a^\dagger\right), \qquad \Omega_R^{coll} = 2g\sqrt{N}
key_equations.context: N identical two-level systems coupled to one quantized mode (Dicke/Tavis-Cummings). Gives the collective sqrt(N) enhancement of the Rabi splitting underlying all collective strong-coupling chemistry.

facts.claim: ORIGIN (phonon-polaritons): Kirill B. Tolpygo (1950, quantum approach) and independently Huang Kun (1951, classical approach) first obtained the coupled photon-phonon (later 'phonon-polariton') dispersion in ionic crystals.
facts.source: Wikipedia 'Polariton' (citing Tolpygo 1950, Huang 1951), https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polariton

facts.claim: The term 'polariton' was proposed by John Hopfield; S. I. Pekar's earlier Soviet-literature name was 'light-exciton', but Hopfield's term was adopted.
facts.source: Wikipedia 'Polariton', https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polariton

facts.claim: EXCITON-POLARITON THEORY: John J. Hopfield, 'Theory of the Contribution of Excitons to the Complex Dielectric Constant of Crystals,' Phys. Rev. 112, 1555 (1958), gave the quantum theory in which crystal eigenstates are mixtures of photons and excitons (polaritons).
facts.source: Hopfield, Phys. Rev. 112, 1555 (1958), https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRev.112.1555

facts.claim: SUPERRADIANCE: R. H. Dicke (Princeton), 'Coherence in Spontaneous Radiation Processes,' Phys. Rev. 93, 99-110 (1954), introduced collective (super-radiant) spontaneous emission by treating a radiating gas as a single quantum system.
facts.source: Dicke, Phys. Rev. 93, 99 (1954), https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRev.93.99

facts.claim: JAYNES-CUMMINGS MODEL: E. T. Jaynes & F. W. Cummings, 'Comparison of Quantum and Semiclassical Radiation Theories with Application to the Beam Maser,' Proc. IEEE 51, 89-109 (1963), established the single-atom single-mode quantized light-matter model.
facts.source: Jaynes & Cummings, Proc. IEEE 51, 89 (1963), https://doi.org/10.1109/PROC.1963.1664

facts.claim: TAVIS-CUMMINGS MODEL: Michael Tavis & Frederick W. Cummings, 'Exact Solution for an N-Molecule-Radiation-Field Hamiltonian,' Phys. Rev. 170, 379-384 (1968), generalized Jaynes-Cummings to N emitters, giving the sqrt(N) collective coupling.
facts.source: Tavis & Cummings, Phys. Rev. 170, 379 (1968), https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRev.170.379

facts.claim: MICROCAVITY STRONG COUPLING: C. Weisbuch, M. Nishioka, A. Ishikawa & Y. Arakawa, 'Observation of the coupled exciton-photon mode splitting in a semiconductor quantum microcavity,' PRL 69, 3314-3317 (1992, Dec 7), first observed vacuum-Rabi/normal-mode splitting of quantum-well excitons — birth of microcavity polaritons.
facts.source: Weisbuch et al., PRL 69, 3314 (1992), https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.69.3314

facts.claim: POLARITON LASER PROPOSAL: A. Imamoglu, R. J. Ram, S. Pau & Y. Yamamoto, 'Nonequilibrium condensates and lasers without inversion: Exciton-polariton lasers,' Phys. Rev. A 53, 4250-4253 (1996), proposed coherent light from polariton condensation (inversionless lasing).
facts.source: Imamoglu et al., Phys. Rev. A 53, 4250 (1996), referenced via Wikipedia 'Polariton laser', https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polariton_laser

facts.claim: POLARITON vs PHOTON LASING: H. Deng, G. Weihs, D. Snoke, J. Bloch & Y. Yamamoto, 'Polariton lasing vs. photon lasing in a semiconductor microcavity,' PNAS 100, 15318-15323 (2003), distinguished the two regimes experimentally.
facts.source: Deng et al., PNAS 100, 15318 (2003), referenced via search results

facts.claim: POLARITON BEC: J. Kasprzak, M. Richard, S. Kundermann, A. Baas et al., 'Bose-Einstein condensation of exciton polaritons,' Nature 443, 409-414 (28 Sep 2006), reported spontaneous macroscopic quantum coherence (BEC) of polaritons in a CdTe microcavity.
facts.source: Kasprzak et al., Nature 443, 409 (2006), https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17006506/

facts.claim: ROOM-TEMPERATURE POLARITON LASING: Christopoulos et al., 'Room-Temperature Polariton Lasing in Semiconductor Microcavities,' PRL 98, 126405 (2007), demonstrated polariton lasing at room temperature (GaN microcavity).
facts.source: Christopoulos et al., PRL 98, 126405 (2007), referenced via search results

facts.claim: BIRTH OF POLARITONIC CHEMISTRY: J. A. Hutchison, T. Schwartz, C. Genet, E. Devaux & T. W. Ebbesen (Strasbourg), 'Modifying Chemical Landscapes by Coupling to Vacuum Fields,' Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51, 1592-1596 (2012), showed an electronic (photochemical) reaction altered by strong coupling to the cavity vacuum field.
facts.source: Hutchison et al., Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51, 1592 (2012), https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201107033

facts.claim: VIBRATIONAL STRONG COUPLING CHEMISTRY: A. Thomas et al. (Ebbesen group), 'Ground-State Chemical Reactivity under Vibrational Coupling to the Vacuum Electromagnetic Field,' Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 55, 11462-11466 (2016), showed a ground-state deprotection rate slowed up to 5.5x when the Si-C stretch was vibrationally strongly coupled.
facts.source: Thomas et al., Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 55, 11462 (2016), https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201605504

facts.claim: VSC SITE-SELECTIVITY: A. Thomas et al. (Ebbesen group), 'Tilting a ground-state reactivity landscape by vibrational strong coupling,' Science 363, 615-619 (2019), showed VSC of distinct vibrational modes can steer product selectivity (Si-C vs Si-O cleavage).
facts.source: Thomas et al., Science 363, 615 (2019), https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aau7742

facts.claim: THEORY (molecular structure under strong coupling): J. Galego, F. J. Garcia-Vidal & J. Feist, 'Cavity-Induced Modifications of Molecular Structure in the Strong-Coupling Regime,' Phys. Rev. X 5, 041022 (2015), built a first-principles model with full electronic+nuclear degrees of freedom beyond the two-level picture.
facts.source: Galego, Garcia-Vidal & Feist, PRX 5, 041022 (2015), https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevX.5.041022

facts.claim: THEORY (QEDFT): J. Flick, M. Ruggenthaler, H. Appel & A. Rubio, 'Atoms and molecules in cavities, from weak to strong coupling in quantum-electrodynamics (QED) chemistry,' PNAS 114, 3026-3034 (2017), established ab initio QED density-functional methods for cavity chemistry.
facts.source: Flick, Ruggenthaler, Appel & Rubio, PNAS 114, 3026 (2017), https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1615509114

facts.claim: THEORY (review): A. Mandal, M. Taylor, B. Weight, E. Koessler, X. Li & P. Huo, 'Theoretical Advances in Polariton Chemistry and Molecular Cavity Quantum Electrodynamics,' Chem. Rev. 123, 9786-9879 (2023), is the comprehensive theory review of the field.
facts.source: Mandal et al., Chem. Rev. 123, 9786 (2023), https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450711/

facts.claim: CAVITY-MODIFIED ELECTRON TRANSFER / HTC: S. K. Sharma & H.-T. Chen (Univ. of Notre Dame), 'Unraveling Abnormal Collective Effects via the Non-Monotonic Number Dependence of Electron Transfer in Confined Electromagnetic Fields,' J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104109 (2024), DOI 10.1063/5.0225434, studies collective effects in nonadiabatic electron transfer in a cavity (Holstein-Tavis-Cummings-type framework).

The non-equilibrium Fermi-golden-rule rate theory for the Holstein-Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian (a universal model for many molecules collectively coupled to a cavity) underlies modern cavity electron/energy-transfer rate theory.
facts.source: AIP J. Chem. Phys. polariton rate-theory articles, https://pubs.aip.org/aip/jcp/article/161/10/104109/3312358/
