Wikipedia, Polariton (history: Tolpygo 1950, Huang 1951, Pekar, Hopfield term)

uncertainties: Phonon-polariton origin dates (Tolpygo 1950, Huang 1951) are sourced from Wikipedia and review articles, not the original Russian/Chinese primary papers; the exact 1950 vs 1951 attribution and whether Tolpygo's was 'quantum' vs Huang's 'classical' should be verified against the primary literature (Tolpygo, Zh. Eksp. Teor. Fiz. 1950; Huang, Proc. R. Soc. A 208, 352, 1951) before being treated as definitive.

Whether Huang Kun literally used the word 'polariton' in 1951 is disputed; one source explicitly states Huang did NOT use the term in the original publication, and Wikipedia attributes coining the term to Hopfield. Treat 'Huang coined polariton' as FALSE; the term is Hopfield's.

Imamoglu et al. 1996 Phys. Rev. A 53, 4250 details (exact page, co-authors Ram/Pau/Yamamoto) come via secondary (Wikipedia) routing, not the APS page directly; volume/page should be double-checked on the APS site.

Deng et al. 2003 PNAS and Christopoulos et al. 2007 PRL volume/page numbers were obtained from search-result summaries rather than fetched primary pages; recommend confirming PNAS 100, 15318 and PRL 98, 126405 directly.

The Sharma & Chen 2024 J. Chem. Phys. volume/issue (161, 104109) and DOI (10.1063/5.0225434) are corroborated by arXiv:2406.17101 and AIP listing, but the AIP article page returned HTTP 403 so the exact published page number was not directly verified on the publisher site; the arXiv preprint title and Notre Dame authorship (Shravan Kumar Sharma, Hsing-Ta Chen) are confirmed.

Hopfield's dielectric-function/dispersion equation transcribed in key_equations is the standard textbook form following from his 1958 derivation, not a verbatim numbered equation lifted from the paper; the avoided-crossing polariton dispersion is the load-bearing physics, but the exact algebraic form should be checked against the original PR 112, 1555 text.

The precise first EXPERIMENTAL polariton-laser claim is contested in the literature (quasi-resonant 2000 coherence vs non-resonant lasing); the timeline lists the proposal (1996), the polariton-vs-photon-lasing distinction (2003), and room-temperature lasing (2007) but does not assert a single unambiguous 'first polariton laser' experiment.

S. I. Pekar and U. Fano (1956) and V. M. Agranovich (1959) are mentioned in secondary sources as contributors to early exciton-polariton theory but were not independently verified against primary sources here.

webFindings.facet: Standard physics of microcavity exciton-polaritons: cavity/exciton dispersions, the 2x2 coupled-oscillator Hamiltonian, lower/upper polariton eigenvalues, detuning, Hopfield coefficients, and canonical numerical values for accurate visualization.
webFindings.summary: Microcavity exciton-polaritons arise from strong coupling between a planar Fabry-Perot cavity photon mode and a quantum-well exciton. The cavity photon has an in-plane dispersion E_cav(k_parallel) = (hbar c / n) sqrt(k_z^2 + k_parallel^2), which for small k_parallel is parabolic with a very light effective mass m_cav = hbar n k_z / c ~ 1e-5 to 1e-4 m_e (e.g., 3.6e-5 m_0 measured in a CdTe cavity). The exciton dispersion is nearly flat on this scale because the exciton mass (m_exc ~ 0.1-1 m_e, hundreds of ps lifetime) is four orders of magnitude heavier than the photon. Coupling is described by a 2x2 coupled-oscillator (Hopfield) Hamiltonian with diagonal cavity/exciton energies and off-diagonal coupling V, where V is half the vacuum-field Rabi splitting (Rabi splitting hbar*Omega_R = 2V at resonance). Diagonalization gives the lower/upper polariton eigenvalues E_LP/UP(k) = (1/2)[E_cav+E_exc +/- sqrt((E_cav-E_exc)^2 + (2V)^2)] = (1/2)[E_cav+E_exc +/- sqrt(delta^2 + (hbar Omega_R)^2)] with detuning delta = E_cav - E_exc. The two branches anticross (never touch) with a minimum gap equal to the Rabi splitting at delta = 0. The eigenvectors define the Hopfield coefficients: the photon fraction |C_k|^2 and exciton fraction |X_k|^2 (with |X|^2 + |C|^2 = 1), which depend on detuning as |X|^2 = (1/2)[1 + delta/sqrt(delta^2 + (2V)^2)]. At resonance (delta=0) the polariton is exactly 50% light / 50% matter. Typical Rabi splittings: ~4-16 meV for GaAs/III-V, ~10-26 meV for II-VI (CdTe), and tens to ~100+ meV for wide-gap/organic/perovskite systems. Sources are dominated by the Carusotto-Ciuti RMP 2013, Deng-Haug-Yamamoto RMP 2010, Deng-Yamamoto PNAS 2003, the Deveaud "Fifteen years of microcavity polaritons" review, and Kavokin-Baumberg "Microcavities".

key_equations.name: Cavity photon in-plane dispersion (full)

variables.meaning: cavity photon energy at in-plane wavevector k_parallel
background refractive index of the cavity medium
quantized perpendicular (cavity-axis) wavevector of the confined photon mode, k_z = pi M / L_z
variables.typical: set by cavity length L_z, integer mode index M

variables.meaning: in-plane wavevector (set experimentally by emission/incidence angle, k_parallel = (E/hbar c) sin theta)
variables.typical: 0 to a few um^-1 over the angular range probed

key_equations.where: Carusotto & Ciuti, Rev. Mod. Phys. 85, 299 (2013), Eq. (1); same form in Deng, Haug & Yamamoto Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 1489 (2010)
key_equations.context: Planar (1D-confined) Fabry-Perot microcavity, single transverse photon mode with fixed k_z; valid for the lossless cavity photon dispersion.

key_equations.name: Cavity photon parabolic approximation and effective mass
key_equations.latex: E_{\mathrm{cav}}(k_\parallel) \simeq E_{\mathrm{cav}}^{0} + \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2 m_{\mathrm{cav}}}, \qquad m_{\mathrm{cav}} = \frac{\hbar n k_z}{c} = \frac{n^2 E_{\mathrm{cav}}^{0}}{c^2}

variables.units: kg (or in units of free-electron mass m_e/m_0)

variables.meaning: cavity photon cutoff energy at k_parallel = 0 (= hbar c k_z / n)

key_equations.where: Carusotto & Ciuti, Rev. Mod. Phys. 85, 299 (2013), Eqs. (1)-(2); Deng, Sebald, et al. / Deng & Yamamoto PNAS 100, 15318 (2003) define m = hbar n_r k_z / c
key_equations.context: Small in-plane wavevector expansion (k_parallel << k_z) of the full square-root dispersion; the photon behaves as a 2D massive particle. m_cav is ~4 orders of magnitude lighter than the exciton mass.

key_equations.name: Exciton dispersion (nearly flat)

variables.meaning: exciton translational (center-of-mass) effective mass = m_e* + m_h*
variables.typical: ~0.1-1 m_e (e.g. ~0.5 m_0 in GaAs QWs); ~1e4 x m_cav

key_equations.where: Carusotto & Ciuti, Rev. Mod. Phys. 85, 299 (2013) (text following Eq. 20); Deng, Haug & Yamamoto Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 1489 (2010)
key_equations.context: Because m_exc >> m_cav, over the small k_parallel range relevant to the cavity (a few um^-1) the exciton dispersion is essentially flat compared to the photon parabola; this asymmetry produces the characteristic polariton anticrossing geometry.

key_equations.name: Coupled-oscillator (Hopfield) 2x2 Hamiltonian

variables.meaning: light-matter coupling strength (off-diagonal element); equal to half the vacuum-field Rabi splitting
vacuum-field Rabi splitting (full normal-mode splitting at resonance) = 2V

key_equations.where: Deveaud (ed.) / 'Fifteen years of microcavity polaritons' arXiv:0811.2502, Sec. on two-coupled-oscillator model, Eq. (1); Carusotto & Ciuti RMP 85, 299 (2013) Eq. (21); standard form e.g. Kavokin & Baumberg, 'Microcavities' (OUP)
key_equations.context: Linear strong-coupling regime, single exciton and single photon mode, rotating-wave approximation, neglecting damping. Diagonal terms carry the k_parallel-dependence; V is taken k-independent near k_parallel ~ 0. Convention: Rabi splitting = 2V at zero detuning. (Note: some papers, e.g. Deng-Yamamoto PNAS 2003 Eq. 1, write the off-diagonal coupling so that the radicand contains 4*Omega with Omega the coupling; numerically the resonant splitting equals 2 x off-diagonal element in all conventions.)

key_equations.name: Lower / upper polariton eigenvalues

variables.typical: below both bare modes; minimum at k_parallel = 0 for delta<=0

variables.meaning: Rabi splitting; minimum gap between branches at resonance

key_equations.where: Deng & Yamamoto, PNAS 100, 15318 (2003), Eq. (1) [E_LP form]; Deveaud review arXiv:0811.2502 Eq. (1) [both branches, lossless limit]; Carusotto & Ciuti RMP 85, 299 (2013) Eq. (24)
key_equations.context: Eigenvalues of the 2x2 coupled-oscillator Hamiltonian. At resonance (E_cav = E_exc) the splitting E_UP - E_LP = 2V = hbar*Omega_R; the branches anticross and never cross (level repulsion). Equivalent compact form: E = (1/2)[E_cav + E_exc +/- sqrt(delta^2 + (hbar Omega_R)^2)].

key_equations.name: Detuning

variables.typical: -10 to +10 meV typical experimental tuning range

key_equations.where: Deng, Haug & Yamamoto Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 1489 (2010) (Delta = E_C - E_X); same convention in Carusotto & Ciuti RMP 85, 299 (2013)
key_equations.context: delta < 0 => photon-like (lower-energy) LP at k=0 with light effective mass; delta > 0 => exciton-like LP; delta = 0 is resonance (50/50 mixing at k=0). Sign convention E_cav - E_exc is the most common; some authors use E_exc - E_cav, flipping the sign in the Hopfield formula.

key_equations.name: Hopfield coefficients (exciton and photon fractions)

variables.meaning: excitonic (matter) fraction of the lower polariton
variables.typical: 0.5 at delta=0; ->1 for large positive delta

variables.meaning: photonic (light) fraction of the lower polariton
variables.typical: 0.5 at delta=0; ->1 for large negative delta

variables.meaning: detuning E_cav - E_exc at the given k_parallel

key_equations.where: Hopfield, Phys. Rev. 112, 1555 (1958) (original definition); explicit detuning form as in Deng, Haug & Yamamoto RMP 82, 1489 (2010) and Kavokin & Baumberg 'Microcavities'; equivalent |X|^2 = (1/2)(1 + Delta/sqrt(Delta^2 + 4|V|^2)) widely quoted
key_equations.context: Eigenvector weights of the 2x2 Hamiltonian; give the squared amplitudes of the exciton and photon components in the lower polariton (upper polariton has X and C swapped). With this sign convention (delta = E_cav - E_exc), positive detuning makes the LP more excitonic. At resonance delta=0 the LP is exactly 50% exciton / 50% photon. These coefficients set the effective mass, interaction strength, lifetime, and emission of each branch as functions of k_parallel and detuning.

facts.claim: Microcavity exciton-polaritons are the eigenmodes of strong (reversible) coupling between a confined cavity photon and a quantum-well exciton; the regime is signaled by an anticrossing of the photon and exciton modes whose minimum splitting equals the vacuum-field Rabi splitting.
facts.source: Deveaud (ed.) review, 'Fifteen years of microcavity polaritons', arXiv:0811.2502 (intro and two-coupled-oscillator section)

facts.claim: The cavity photon in-plane dispersion is E_cav(k_parallel) = (hbar c / n) sqrt(k_z^2 + k_parallel^2), which for k_parallel << k_z reduces to a parabola E_cav^0 + hbar^2 k_parallel^2 / (2 m_cav).
facts.source: Carusotto & Ciuti, Rev. Mod. Phys. 85, 299 (2013), Eq. (1)

facts.claim: The effective cavity photon mass is m_cav = hbar n k_z / c (equivalently n^2 E_cav^0 / c^2), giving a value on the order of 1e-5 to 1e-4 free-electron masses, about four orders of magnitude lighter than the exciton mass.
facts.source: Carusotto & Ciuti, Rev. Mod. Phys. 85, 299 (2013), Eq. (2); Deng & Yamamoto, PNAS 100, 15318 (2003)

facts.claim: A specific measured cavity photon effective mass of 3.6e-5 m_0 and a Rabi splitting of hbar*Omega = 15.9 +/- 0.1 meV are reported for a representative II-VI (CdTe) microcavity used in polariton condensation studies.
facts.source: Deng, Haug & Yamamoto, Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 1489 (2010) (via PMC-hosted polariton condensate study citing these parameters)

facts.claim: The exciton dispersion is essentially flat on the scale of the photon dispersion because the exciton translational mass (~0.1-1 m_e) is roughly 1e4 times the photon mass.
facts.source: Deng & Yamamoto, PNAS 100, 15318 (2003) ('cavity photons have an effective mass ... four orders of magnitude lighter than the exciton effective mass')

facts.claim: The strong-coupling system is described by a 2x2 coupled-oscillator Hamiltonian with cavity and exciton energies on the diagonal and an off-diagonal coupling V that is half the Rabi splitting (Rabi splitting = 2V at resonance).
facts.source: Deveaud review, arXiv:0811.2502, two-coupled-oscillator model (Eq. 1); Kavokin & Baumberg, 'Microcavities' (OUP, 2nd ed. 2017)

facts.claim: Lower/upper polariton eigenvalues are E_LP/UP = (1/2)[E_cav + E_exc +/- sqrt((E_cav - E_exc)^2 + (2V)^2)]; the lossless eigenfrequency form omega = (1/2)(omega_x + omega_c) +/- (1/2) sqrt(Omega_R^2 + (omega_x - omega_c)^2) is given explicitly in the Deveaud review.
facts.source: Deveaud review arXiv:0811.2502, Eq. (1); Deng & Yamamoto PNAS 100, 15318 (2003), Eq. (1)

facts.claim: The detuning is defined as delta (Delta) = E_cav - E_exc (E_C - E_X); the two polariton branches anticross as detuning is varied and never cross.
facts.source: Deng, Haug & Yamamoto, Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 1489 (2010) (Delta = E_C - E_X)

facts.claim: The Hopfield coefficients give the excitonic fraction |X|^2 = (1/2)(1 + delta/sqrt(delta^2 + (2V)^2)) and photonic fraction |C|^2 = 1 - |X|^2; at zero detuning the polariton is exactly 50% exciton and 50% photon.
facts.source: Hopfield, Phys. Rev. 112, 1555 (1958); Deng & Yamamoto PNAS 100, 15318 (2003) ('exciton fraction of the k_parallel=0 polariton x(0) = 1/2'); Kavokin & Baumberg 'Microcavities'

facts.claim: Typical vacuum-field Rabi splittings are about 4-16 meV in GaAs/III-V microcavities (up to ~16 meV anticrossing quoted for GaAs), about 10-26 meV in II-VI (CdTe), and tens to over 100 meV in wide-gap, organic, and perovskite systems.
facts.source: Deveaud review arXiv:0811.2502 ('as large as 16 meV in bulk GaAs'); Carusotto & Ciuti RMP 85, 299 (2013) ('Omega_R ~ 10 meV for III-V, ~20 meV for II-VI')

facts.claim: Because the lower polariton at small k_parallel is strongly photon-like, its effective mass near k=0 is about twice the photon mass (~1e-4 m_e), four orders of magnitude lighter than the exciton mass, which is the origin of the very steep LP dispersion bottom.
facts.source: Deng & Yamamoto, PNAS 100, 15318 (2003) ('effective mass m_pol twice the photon mass, four orders of magnitude lighter than the exciton mass')

facts.claim: Including phenomenological damping, the complex eigenfrequencies are omega = (1/2)(omega_x + omega_c) - (i/2)(gamma_x + gamma_c) +/- (1/2) sqrt(Omega_R^2 + (omega_x - omega_c - i(gamma_x - gamma_c))^2), reducing to equal LP/UP linewidths (average of gamma_x, gamma_c) at zero detuning.
facts.source: Deveaud review arXiv:0811.2502, Eq. (1)

sources.title: Carusotto & Ciuti, 'Quantum fluids of light', Reviews of Modern Physics 85, 299 (2013)
Carusotto & Ciuti, 'Quantum fluids of light' (arXiv preprint, full text)
Deng, Haug & Yamamoto, 'Exciton-polariton Bose-Einstein condensation', Reviews of Modern Physics 82, 1489 (2010)
Deng, Weihs, Snoke, Bloch & Yamamoto, 'Polariton lasing vs. photon lasing in a semiconductor microcavity', PNAS 100, 15318 (2003)
Deveaud (ed.) et al., 'Fifteen years of microcavity polaritons' (review, arXiv:0811.2502)
Kavokin, Baumberg, Malpuech & Laussy, 'Microcavities', 2nd ed., Oxford University Press (2017)
Hopfield, 'Theory of the contribution of excitons to the complex dielectric constant of crystals', Phys. Rev. 112, 1555 (1958)
'Microscopic description of exciton-polaritons in microcavities', Phys. Rev. Research 1, 033120 (2019)

uncertainties: Notation/sign conventions vary across sources. Some authors define the off-diagonal coupling as g or Omega such that the radicand is sqrt(delta^2 + 4g^2) (so Rabi splitting = 2g), while others (e.g. Deng-Yamamoto PNAS 2003 Eq. 1) write sqrt((E_x-E_c)^2 + 4*Omega) where Omega already denotes the splitting-related quantity. I standardized to V = half the Rabi splitting so that Rabi splitting = 2V; verify the exact symbol when copying a specific paper's equation.

The detuning sign convention (delta = E_cav - E_exc vs E_exc - E_cav) flips the sign inside the Hopfield coefficient formula. I used delta = E_cav - E_exc (Deng-Haug-Yamamoto convention), under which positive detuning makes the lower polariton more excitonic; confirm against the target source if matching a specific figure.

The exact numeric photon mass 3.6e-5 m_0 and Rabi splitting 15.9 meV were extracted from a PMC-hosted condensate paper attributing them to a CdTe cavity / Deng-Haug-Yamamoto context; I could not open the APS full text directly (403). The order of magnitude (~1e-5 m_e, splitting ~10-16 meV) is corroborated by multiple sources, but the precise per-sample values should be checked against the primary RMP 2010 article if exact reproduction is required.

Typical exciton translational mass values (~0.1-1 m_e) are material- and well-width-dependent; I gave representative GaAs/CdTe ranges rather than a single authoritative number, since the reviews quote it as 'four orders of magnitude heavier than the photon' rather than a fixed value.

I was unable to fetch the Kavokin-Baumberg 'Microcavities' textbook text directly (no open full text); its equation forms are inferred from the consistent standard treatment in the accessible reviews and are widely reproduced, but exact equation numbers from that book are not verified.

The 'as large as 16 meV in bulk GaAs' figure from the Deveaud review refers to a bulk-GaAs anticrossing context; quantum-well GaAs microcavity Rabi splittings are more commonly in the ~4-15 meV range. The distinction between bulk-polariton and QW-microcavity-polariton splitting magnitudes should be kept in mind.

webFindings.facet: Jaynes-Cummings, Tavis-Cummings, and Dicke models for collective light-matter coupling (cavity QED): exact Hamiltonians, vacuum Rabi splitting, sqrt(N) collective enhancement, single-molecule coupling vs mode volume, bright polaritons vs dark states, and the dipole-gauge Pauli-Fierz Hamiltonian of molecular cavity QED.
webFindings.summary: The Jaynes-Cummings model (JCM) describes one two-level emitter coupled to a single quantized cavity mode under the rotating-wave approximation (RWA), H_JC = hbar omega_c a-dagger a + (hbar omega_eg/2) sigma_z + hbar Omega_R (a sigma_+ + a-dagger sigma_-). It conserves total excitation number (U(1) symmetry), so it block-diagonalizes into 2x2 dressed-state doublets; at resonance the n-th doublet splits by 2 Omega_R sqrt(n), giving the single-photon vacuum Rabi splitting of 2 Omega_R = 2g (Rev/tutorial: Le Boite/De Liberato, arXiv:2403.02402). Dropping the RWA gives the quantum Rabi model with counter-rotating terms (a-dagger sigma_+ + a sigma_-), relevant in ultrastrong coupling. The single-emitter coupling is set by the emitter dipole and the cavity vacuum field: hbar g = mu . E_vac with E_vac = sqrt(hbar omega_0 / (2 V eps0 eps_host)), i.e. g = (mu/hbar) sqrt(hbar omega_c / (2 eps0 V)); g scales as 1/sqrt(V), so nanoplasmonic cavities with tiny mode volumes reach single-molecule strong coupling (g approximately 380 meV for a single methylene-blue molecule in a nanoparticle-on-mirror cavity, Chikkaraddy et al., Nature 2016). The Tavis-Cummings model (TCM) puts N identical emitters in one mode, H_TC = omega_r a-dagger a + sum_j [ (omega_j/2) sigma_z^j + Gamma_j (a-dagger sigma_-^j + sigma_+^j a) ]. In the single-excitation sector only the symmetric ('bright'/superradiant) combination |B> = (1/sqrt N) sum_J |e_J> couples to the photon, with collective coupling g_N = g sqrt(N); it hybridizes with the photon into upper and lower polaritons split by the collective vacuum Rabi splitting Omega_R = 2 g sqrt(N). The remaining N-1 orthogonal combinations are 'dark' states with zero net transition dipole that do not couple to light (uncoupled at the bare molecular energy). The Dicke model is the TCM/Rabi generalization keeping counter-rotating terms; in the dilute (bosonized) limit it again gives sqrt(N) enhancement of the vacuum Rabi frequency. For molecular cavity QED the rigorous starting point is the dipole-gauge Pauli-Fierz (PF) Hamiltonian: H_PF = H_M + hbar omega_c (a-dagger a + 1/2) - mu . E_0 q_c + (1/(2 hbar omega_c)) (mu . E_0)^2, where the last term is the dipole self-energy required for gauge invariance and a bounded spectrum (Mandal, Taylor, Weight, Koessler, Li, Huo, Chem. Rev. 2023). Typical molecular/excitonic collective Rabi splittings are tens of meV to about 1 eV (e.g. g_N approximately 0.25-0.31 eV for J-aggregate dye films, Rider et al., arXiv:2402.09885); vibrational strong coupling is in the tens-of-cm^-1 to few-meV range.

key_equations.name: Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian (single emitter, RWA)

variables.meaning: vacuum Rabi frequency / single-emitter light-matter coupling
Pauli inversion and raising/lowering operators of the two-level emitter; sigma_- = sigma_+^dagger = sigma_x - i sigma_y

key_equations.context: Single two-level system coupled to one cavity mode under the rotating-wave approximation (RWA); valid in weak-to-strong coupling where counter-rotating terms are negligible (g << omega_c, omega_eg). Conserves excitation number.

key_equations.name: Vacuum Rabi splitting (resonant single-photon doublet)

variables.meaning: excitation-number quantum number of the JC doublet
variables.typical: 1 for the single-photon vacuum Rabi splitting

key_equations.context: At zero cavity-emitter detuning (Delta = omega_c - omega_eg = 0). The n=1 doublet gives the experimentally measured vacuum Rabi splitting 2g. The sqrt(n) scaling (Jaynes-Cummings ladder anharmonicity) underlies photon blockade. Off resonance, omega_{n,+}-omega_{n,-} = sqrt(Delta^2 + 4 Omega_R^2 n).

key_equations.name: Quantum Rabi model (no RWA; counter-rotating terms)

variables.meaning: Pauli-x of the emitter; sigma_x = sigma_+ + sigma_-, generates flip-flop AND counter-rotating (a-dagger sigma_+ , a sigma_-) terms

variables.typical: becomes comparable to omega_c in ultrastrong coupling (USC), Omega_R/omega_c >~ 0.1

key_equations.context: Recovered by removing the RWA from the JCM. Breaks excitation-number conservation (only Z2 parity remains), so the ground state contains virtual photons. Needed in the ultrastrong/deep-strong coupling regimes.

key_equations.name: Single-emitter coupling and vacuum field vs mode volume

variables.meaning: transition (electric) dipole moment of the emitter (aligned with field)
variables.units: C*m (often quoted in Debye; 1 D = 3.336x10^-30 C*m)
variables.typical: few D to ~10 D for strong dye transitions
~(lambda/2)^3 for Fabry-Perot; down to <~10^-6 lambda^3 (nm^3) for plasmonic nanogaps

variables.meaning: background relative permittivity of the medium filling the mode

key_equations.context: Single electric dipole coupled to the cavity vacuum field. g scales as 1/sqrt(V): shrinking the mode volume (plasmonic picocavities) is the route to single-molecule strong coupling. Assumes dipole aligned with field and emitter inside the mode volume.

key_equations.name: Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian (N emitters, RWA)

variables.meaning: transition frequency of the j-th two-level system
single-emitter coupling of TLS j to the mode
raising/lowering and inversion operators of emitter j
number of emitters collectively coupled to the mode

key_equations.where: Dong, Zhang, Wu, Wu, arXiv:2110.14174, Eq. (5) (hbar=1)
key_equations.context: N non-interacting identical two-level systems coupled to one common cavity mode, RWA. Reduces to JCM for N=1. The collective coupling of the symmetric mode scales as sqrt(N).

key_equations.name: Collective bright (superradiant) state and collective coupling

variables.meaning: symmetric single-excitation (bright/superradiant) collective state, zero photons
collective vacuum Rabi splitting (upper-lower polariton splitting at resonance)
variables.typical: tens of meV to ~1 eV (excitonic); few cm^-1 to meV (vibrational)

key_equations.where: Mandal, Taylor, Weight, Koessler, Li, Huo, Chem. Rev. 2023, 123, 9786 (DOI 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855), Eq. (13); sqrt(N) per arXiv:2110.14174 / arXiv:2402.09885 Eq. (4)
key_equations.context: Single-excitation manifold of N identical emitters. Only the totally symmetric combination couples to the photon, with enhanced coupling g_N = g sqrt(N); the collective Rabi splitting therefore scales as sqrt(N) (equivalently sqrt(molecular density)).

key_equations.name: Upper / lower polariton states (collective)

variables.meaning: all-ground-state molecules with one cavity photon
mixing (Hopfield) angle; tan(2 theta_N) = 2 g_N / Delta, theta_N = pi/4 at resonance (50:50 light-matter)

key_equations.where: Mandal et al., Chem. Rev. 2023 (DOI 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855), Eqs. (14a)-(14b)
key_equations.context: Hybridization of the bright collective matter state with the single-photon state yields two polaritons split by the collective vacuum Rabi splitting 2 g_N (at resonance). These are the only optically bright (absorbing) states.

key_equations.name: Dark states (N-1 uncoupled collective states)

variables.meaning: expansion coefficients of dark state alpha over single-molecule excitations, constrained to sum to zero
number of degenerate dark states (single-excitation manifold)

key_equations.where: Mandal et al., Chem. Rev. 2023 (DOI 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855), Eq. (18)
key_equations.context: The N-1 combinations orthogonal to the bright state have zero net transition dipole and do not couple to the cavity field; they remain (in the bare TC model) at the original uncoupled molecular energy. Disorder/dephasing weakly couples them to the polaritons (a central theme in polariton chemistry).

key_equations.name: Dipole-gauge Pauli-Fierz (length-gauge) Hamiltonian of molecular cavity QED

variables.meaning: total molecular electric-dipole operator (sum over all charges)
per-photon field amplitude vector, E_0 = e_hat sqrt(hbar omega_c / (2 eps0 V))

key_equations.where: Mandal, Taylor, Weight, Koessler, Li, Huo, Chem. Rev. 2023, 123, 9786 (DOI 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855), Eq. (56) and Eq. (3) for E_0/g_c
key_equations.context: Exact, gauge-invariant non-relativistic light-matter Hamiltonian (dipole/length gauge, long-wavelength dipole approximation, single mode shown; generalizes to many modes/molecules). The final term is the dipole self-energy (DSE), essential for gauge invariance, a bounded-below spectrum, and correct strong/ultrastrong-coupling results. Expanding mu in a two-level basis and applying the RWA recovers the JC/Tavis-Cummings models.

facts.claim: The Jaynes-Cummings model describes one two-level system coupled to a single quantized cavity mode in the RWA; the interaction is flip-flop (a sigma_+ + a-dagger sigma_-), which conserves the total excitation number N_hat = a-dagger a + (sigma_z+1)/2 (U(1) symmetry).
facts.source: arXiv:2403.02402 (Le Boite, De Liberato), Eqs. (1)-(2)

facts.claim: Because excitation number is conserved, the JCM block-diagonalizes into 2x2 blocks spanned by {|n,g>, |n-1,e>}; the dressed-state eigenvalues are omega_{n,+/-} = -omega_eg/2 + omega_c n - Delta/2 +/- sqrt(Delta^2/4 + Omega_R^2 n), with detuning Delta = omega_c - omega_eg.

At resonance the intra-doublet splitting is 2 Omega_R sqrt(n); the n=1 case gives the single-photon vacuum Rabi splitting of 2 Omega_R = 2g. This sqrt(n) anharmonicity (Jaynes-Cummings ladder) enables photon blockade.

Removing the RWA gives the quantum Rabi model H_R = hbar omega_c a-dagger a + (hbar omega_eg/2) sigma_z + hbar Omega_R (a+a-dagger) sigma_x, whose counter-rotating terms (a-dagger sigma_+, a sigma_-) break excitation-number conservation (only Z2 parity survives) and put virtual photons in the ground state; they matter in the ultrastrong-coupling regime.
facts.source: arXiv:2403.02402, Eq. (11) and surrounding text

facts.claim: The single-emitter coupling energy is hbar g = mu . E_vac, with RMS vacuum field |E_vac| = sqrt(hbar omega_0 / (2 V_m eps0 eps_host)); hence g = (mu/hbar) sqrt(hbar omega_c / (2 eps0 V)), so g scales as 1/sqrt(V).
facts.source: Rider et al., arXiv:2402.09885, Eqs. (3)-(4)

facts.claim: For N dipoles the interaction scales as sqrt(N): g_N = (1/hbar) sqrt(N) mu sqrt(hbar omega_0 / (2 V_m eps0 eps_host)), and the (collective) vacuum Rabi splitting is Omega_R = omega_+ - omega_- = 2 g_N.
facts.source: Rider et al., arXiv:2402.09885, Eqs. (1) and (4); definition Omega_R = 2 g_N

facts.claim: The molecular strong-coupling condition is Omega_R = 2 g_N > (Gamma + K)/2, i.e. the Rabi splitting must exceed the mean of the molecular (Gamma) and cavity (K) linewidths.
facts.source: Rider et al., arXiv:2402.09885, Eq. (1)

facts.claim: The Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian for N identical emitters in one mode (RWA) is H_TC = omega_r a-dagger a + sum_j [ (omega_j/2) sigma_z,j + Gamma_j (a-dagger sigma_-,j + sigma_+,j a) ]; it reduces to the JCM for N=1 and exhibits sqrt(N) scaling of the collective coupling, observable as the vacuum Rabi splitting.
facts.source: Dong, Zhang, Wu, Wu, arXiv:2110.14174, Eq. (5) and Sec. III

facts.claim: In the single-excitation sector only the symmetric (bright/superradiant) state |B,0> = (1/sqrt N) sum_J |e_J> couples to the photon; hybridization yields upper and lower polaritons |+/-> = cos/sin theta_N |B,0> +/- sin/cos theta_N |G,1>, with 50:50 light-matter character (theta_N = pi/4) at resonance.
facts.source: Mandal et al., Chem. Rev. 2023 (DOI 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855), Eqs. (13)-(14)

facts.claim: The remaining N-1 collective combinations are degenerate 'dark' states with zero net transition dipole (coefficients sum to zero) that do not couple to the cavity field and remain at the bare molecular energy; line broadening/dephasing can weakly couple them to the polaritons.
facts.source: Mandal et al., Chem. Rev. 2023 (DOI 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855), Eq. (18); Antoniou et al., J. Chem. Phys. 159, 044306 (2023), DOI 10.1063/5.0155302

facts.claim: The Dicke model is the multi-emitter generalization keeping counter-rotating terms: H_I ∝ Omega_R (a+a-dagger) sum_i sigma_x^i; in the dilute (bosonized) limit, with collective operator b = (1/sqrt N) sum_i sigma_-^i, it gives H_I ≈ sqrt(N) Omega_R (a+a-dagger)(b+b-dagger), i.e. a sqrt(N)-enhanced vacuum Rabi frequency.
facts.source: arXiv:2403.02402, Eq. (18) and surrounding text

facts.claim: The rigorous molecular-cavity-QED Hamiltonian is the dipole-gauge Pauli-Fierz form H_PF = H_M + hbar omega_c (a-dagger a + 1/2) - mu . E_0 q_c + (1/(2 hbar omega_c))(mu . E_0)^2; the final dipole-self-energy term is required for gauge invariance and a bounded-below spectrum.
facts.source: Mandal et al., Chem. Rev. 2023 (DOI 10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00855), Eq. (56)

facts.claim: Single-molecule strong coupling has been demonstrated in plasmonic nanoparticle-on-mirror picocavities, with a measured vacuum Rabi splitting of g ≈ 380 meV for a single methylene-blue molecule (FDTD-confirmed), enabled by an extremely small mode volume.
facts.source: Chikkaraddy et al., Nature 535, 127 (2016), DOI 10.1038/nature17974 (quoted in arXiv:2403.02402)

facts.claim: Representative molecular collective couplings from routine optical data: g_N ≈ 0.31 eV for a Nile-Red-doped polymer brush (40 nm) and g_N ≈ 0.25 eV for a TDBC J-aggregate film (transition at 2.14 eV), placing excitonic Rabi splittings in the tenths-of-an-eV range.
facts.source: Rider et al., arXiv:2402.09885, worked examples (Sec. on Nile Red and TDBC)

facts.claim: In the single-excitation TC model the N-1 dark states are degenerate, antisymmetric under emitter permutation, and contain the single-excitation subradiant states as a subclass; bright states live in the controllable/observable subspace, dark states in the uncontrollable/unobservable subspace.
facts.source: Dong, Zhang, Wu, Wu, arXiv:2110.14174, Sec. I and Sec. IV

sources.title: Nonperturbative cavity quantum electrodynamics: is the Jaynes-Cummings model still relevant? (A. Le Boite, S. De Liberato, tutorial review)
Theoretical Advances in Polariton Chemistry and Molecular Cavity Quantum Electrodynamics (Mandal, Taylor, Weight, Koessler, Li, Huo)
Theoretical Advances in Polariton Chemistry and Molecular Cavity QED (open-access full text)
Strong coupling in molecular systems: a simple predictor employing routine optical measurements (Rider, Johnson, Bates, Wardley, Gordon, Oliver, Armes, Leggett, Barnes)
On the Dynamics of the Tavis-Cummings Model (Dong, Zhang, A.-G. Wu, R.-B. Wu)
The role of dephasing for dark state coupling in a molecular Tavis-Cummings model (Antoniou, Suchanek, Varner, Foley)
Single-molecule strong coupling at room temperature in plasmonic nanocavities (Chikkaraddy et al.)
A Cavity-Enhanced Spectroscopist's Lens on Molecular Polaritons (review of polariton/TC concepts and dark states)

uncertainties: Notation for the single-emitter coupling varies by source: some write g = (mu/hbar) sqrt(hbar omega_c / 2 eps0 V) (user's form, matches Rider et al. when eps_host=1), while the Mandal/Huo review writes a coupling constant g_c with omega_c in the numerator and sqrt(2 eps0 hbar V) in the denominator. These are algebraically related forms of mu.E_0/hbar with E_0 = e_hat sqrt(hbar omega_c/2 eps0 V); the WebFetch transcription of the review's g_c (its Eq. 3) was slightly garbled, so treat that exact prefactor placement as medium confidence. The user's stated form is well sourced via Rider et al.

The factor relating Omega_R (vacuum Rabi frequency) and g: many sources (including arXiv:2403.02402) use Omega_R as the coupling itself so that the resonant single-photon splitting is 2 Omega_R = 2g; others reserve Omega_R for the full splitting (Omega_R = 2g, as in Rider et al.). The physical splitting equals 2g either way, but symbol conventions differ between papers.

The dipole self-energy term is sometimes written as (1/2)(lambda.mu)^2 with a coupling vector lambda = e_hat sqrt(1/(eps0 V)), or absorbed into a shifted dipole operator; equivalent to the (1/(2 hbar omega_c))(mu.E_0)^2 form quoted here but with different bookkeeping of omega_c factors across gauge conventions. Cross-check the exact prefactor against the specific review when implementing.

Typical-magnitude numbers depend strongly on system: single-molecule plasmonic (g up to ~0.3-0.4 eV), bulk excitonic ensembles (collective g_N ~ tens of meV to ~1 eV), and vibrational strong coupling (g_N ~ few cm^-1 to ~10 meV). Exact values are system-specific; ranges given are representative, not universal.

The Mandal/Huo review equation numbers (Eqs. 2, 3, 13, 14, 18, 56) were read via automated PDF/HTML extraction; equation labels should be re-verified against the published PDF before being quoted as exact in a publication, though the equation content is corroborated across multiple sources.

webFindings.facet: Holstein-Tavis-Cummings model and cavity-modified electron transfer / Marcus theory (cross-check of Sharma & Chen 2024)
webFindings.summary: The Sharma & Chen 2024 paper [S. K. Sharma & H.-T. Chen, "Unraveling Abnormal Collective Effects via the Non-Monotonic Number Dependence of Electron Transfer in Confined Electromagnetic Fields," J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104102 (2024); arXiv:2406.17101] models N identical donor-acceptor pairs in an optical cavity using the Holstein-Tavis-Cummings (HTC) framework. The total Hamiltonian is H = H_C + H_I + sum_j H_{M,j} (their Eq. 1): a single quantized cavity mode H_C = hbar*omega_c a^dag a; per-molecule spin-boson (Holstein) terms with diabatic donor/acceptor states |D_j>, |A_j> at energies E_D, E_A, electronic coupling H_AD, and a vibrational primary mode R_j linearly coupled to the acceptor (their Eqs. 2-5); and PZW-gauge light-matter coupling H_I = H_DE + H_DS with dimensionless dipole couplings g_D, g_A, t_AD (their Eqs. 6-10). After a polaron transformation and reorganization-fluctuation timescale separation, ET rates between dressed (polariton) states are computed with the standard Marcus/FGR rate K(V, E_a) = sqrt(pi/(E_r k_B T)) (|V|^2/(2 hbar)) exp(-E_a^2/(4 k_B T E_r)) (their Eq. 35), with effective reorganization energy E_r = lambda_v^2/(2 omega_v^2) and activation energy E_a = E_{fi} + E_r. The cavity-photon-mediated ET channel uses effective couplings T_01 = hbar*omega_c t_AD, T_10 = -hbar*omega_c t_AD. The KEY non-monotonic (turnover) result: because the collective polariton energy at resonance is Omega_+^(I) = sqrt((N-1)|T_01|^2) (their Eq. 26, with detuning Delta = hbar*omega_c - E_AD), the photon-coupled ET path (Path c) becomes barrier-less when Omega_+ - hbar*omega_c = 0, giving the turnover at N_max^(I) = 1 + hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2 (their Eq. 44a) and N_max^(II) = 1 + hbar*omega_c/|T_10|^2 (Eq. 44b). Beyond N_max the rate falls off (asymptotically k ~ e^{-N}; polariton relaxation Gamma ~ N^{-2}). IMPORTANT CROSS-CHECK on the task's note: the published leading form is N_max = 1 + hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2, NOT hbar*omega_c*E_AD/|T_01|^2. The donor-acceptor gap E_AD enters because the resonance/turnover condition uses the cavity tuned to the gap (hbar*omega_c = E_AD = |E_A - E_D|), so at resonance hbar*omega_c in the N_max formula IS the gap E_AD. For |T_01|^2 to make N_max dimensionless, T_01 (=hbar*omega_c t_AD) carries units of energy, so |T_01|^2 is an energy and hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2 is dimensionless. The physical origin of the turnover is that collective coupling enhances the Rabi splitting as sqrt(N-1) (Tavis-Cummings/Dicke), which detunes the polariton PES away from the barrier-less single-molecule (Marcus inverted-region-eliminated, Semenov-Nitzan) condition for N>N_max, while the single reactive molecule competes against N-1 dark molecules (the 'large-N problem' of polariton chemistry). This is consistent with the collective-coupling PMET picture of Mandal/Krauss/Huo.

key_equations.context: Tavis-Cummings collective enhancement: effective Rabi splitting scales as sqrt(N-1)|T_01|. Active molecule j=1 excluded from polariton; N-2 dark states.

variables.meaning: molecule number maximizing the photon-absorption ET path (Path c)
variables.typical: 1636 and 10785 for the two example couplings in Fig. 2

square of photon-mediated ET coupling = (hbar*omega_c)^2 |t_AD|^2 (units of energy^2... see context)

Using Omega_+^{(I)}|_{Delta=0}=sqrt((N-1)|T_01|^2)=hbar*omega_c gives N-1=(hbar*omega_c)^2/|T_01|^2; the paper writes Eq.(44) as 1+hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2 (consistent only if |T_01|^2 is taken in units making the ratio dimensionless / a unit convention in the paper). CROSS-CHECK on the task's note: published form is hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2, and because resonance fixes hbar*omega_c = E_AD, the gap E_AD enters as the numerator; it is NOT hbar*omega_c*E_AD/|T_01|^2.

facts.claim: The paper is by Shravan Kumar Sharma and Hsing-Ta Chen (Univ. of Notre Dame), titled 'Unraveling Abnormal Collective Effects via the Non-Monotonic Number Dependence of Electron Transfer in Confined Electromagnetic Fields,' published in J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104102 (2024), DOI 10.1063/5.0225434; preprint arXiv:2406.17101.
facts.source: arXiv:2406.17101 abstract page (https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.17101); J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104102 (2024), https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0225434

facts.claim: The model is explicitly the Holstein-Tavis-Cummings (HTC) model: electronic two-level donor/acceptor states, a Holstein vibrational primary mode coupled to the acceptor, and a single Tavis-Cummings cavity mode collectively coupled to all N molecules.
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, Sec. II and Eqs. (1)-(10) (arXiv:2406.17101 full text)

facts.claim: The cavity-photon-mediated effective ET couplings are T_01 = hbar*omega_c*t_AD (photon absorption) and T_10 = -hbar*omega_c*t_AD (photon emission), while T_00=T_11=H_AD recover the bare diabatic coupling when the Franck-Condon dipole-self-energy parameter g_AD=0.
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, text following Eq. (23) (arXiv:2406.17101)

facts.claim: The collective Rabi splitting scales as 2*sqrt((N-1)|T_01|^2), i.e. sqrt(N-1) Tavis-Cummings/Dicke enhancement, with polariton energies Omega_pm^(I) = Delta/2 ± sqrt((Delta/2)^2+(N-1)|T_01|^2) and detuning Delta = hbar*omega_c - E_AD.
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, Eq. (26) (arXiv:2406.17101)

facts.claim: ET rates are computed with the classical Marcus rate K(V,E_a)=sqrt(pi/(E_r k_B T)) (|V|^2/(2 hbar)) exp(-E_a^2/(4 k_B T E_r)), activation energy E_a=E_fi+E_r, and effective reorganization energy E_r=lambda_v^2/(2 omega_v^2). This is algebraically identical to k_ET=(2pi/hbar)|V|^2 (1/sqrt(4 pi lambda k_B T)) exp(-(lambda+DeltaG)^2/(4 lambda k_B T)) with lambda=E_r and DeltaG=E_fi.
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, Eq. (35) and text (arXiv:2406.17101)

facts.claim: A non-monotonic dependence of the ET rate on N exists: the photon-coupled Path (c) rate has a maximum where the polariton PES becomes barrier-less (Omega_+ - hbar*omega_c = 0), giving turnover at N_max^(I)=1+hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2 and N_max^(II)=1+hbar*omega_c/|T_10|^2 (Eq. 44).
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, Eqs. (44a),(44b) and surrounding text (arXiv:2406.17101)

facts.claim: CROSS-CHECK / CORRECTION to the task hypothesis: the published N_max is proportional to hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2, NOT hbar*omega_c*E_AD/|T_01|^2. The donor-acceptor gap E_AD enters only through the resonance condition: the cavity is tuned to the gap (hbar*omega_c = E_AD = |E_A-E_D|), so at resonance the hbar*omega_c appearing in N_max equals E_AD. The turnover therefore does scale with the gap-at-resonance over the squared photon-coupling, but the literal extra factor of E_AD in the task's note is not present.
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, Eqs. (26),(44) and resonance condition hbar*omega_c=1.0 eV=|E_AD| in Sec. IV (arXiv:2406.17101)

facts.claim: For large N both the ET rates (Paths b and c) and polariton relaxation are suppressed: ET rate asymptotically scales as ~ e^{-N}, and the PR rate scales as ~ N^{-2}. The cavity-induced quantum yield rises linearly with N for small N (Dicke-like) then turns over and is suppressed for large N (the 'large-N problem' of polariton chemistry).
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, text after Eq. (44) and abstract (arXiv:2406.17101)

facts.claim: Numerical example: with |E_AD|=1.0 eV, H_AD=245 cm^-1 (~0.03 eV), E_r=1.0 eV, hbar*omega_v=80.6 cm^-1, k_B T=0.025 eV, hbar*omega_c=1.0 eV (resonant), hbar*omega_c|t_AD|=69 cm^-1 (|t_AD|=8.5e-3), |g_AD|=0.5, the two demonstrated turnover values are N_max=1636 and N_max=10785.
facts.source: Sharma & Chen 2024, Sec. IV (parameters) and Fig. 2 caption (arXiv:2406.17101)

facts.claim: The light-matter interaction follows Semenov & Nitzan's confined-field ET treatment (PZW gauge, long-wavelength), which for a single molecule shows strong coupling to a cavity mode can enhance the nonadiabatic ET rate and eliminate the Marcus inverted region (make the path barrier-less). Sharma & Chen extend this to N molecules.
facts.source: A. Semenov & A. Nitzan, 'Electron transfer in confined electromagnetic fields,' J. Chem. Phys. 150, 174122 (2019), https://doi.org/10.1063/1.5095940; cited as Ref. 36 in Sharma & Chen 2024

facts.claim: The collective-coupling PMET picture (rate enhancement from many molecules collectively coupled at small per-molecule coupling, with competition from dark states and cavity loss, yielding non-monotonic N dependence) is corroborated by Koessler, Mandal, Musser, Krauss & Huo, 'Polariton mediated electron transfer under the collective molecule-cavity coupling regime,' Chem. Sci. 16, 11644-11658 (2025), and the foundational Mandal, Krauss & Huo, J. Phys. Chem. B 124, 6321 (2020).
facts.source: Chem. Sci. 2025, DOI 10.1039/D5SC01911G (https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2025/sc/d5sc01911g); J. Phys. Chem. B 2020, DOI 10.1021/acs.jpcb.0c03227

facts.claim: A follow-up unified Fermi-golden-rule rate theory by Chen and co-workers (extension to lossy cavities, reproducing Marcus and Marcus-Jortner limits) appears as arXiv:2511.04017 / J. Chem. Phys. 164, 024113 (2026).
facts.source: arXiv:2511.04017 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.04017); J. Chem. Phys. 164, 024113 listing (https://pubs.aip.org/aip/jcp/article-abstract/164/2/024113)

sources.title: Sharma & Chen, 'Unraveling Abnormal Collective Effects via the Non-Monotonic Number Dependence of Electron Transfer in Confined Electromagnetic Fields,' J. Chem. Phys. 161, 104102 (2024)
Sharma & Chen 2024 preprint (full text used for equation transcription)
Semenov & Nitzan, 'Electron transfer in confined electromagnetic fields,' J. Chem. Phys. 150, 174122 (2019)
Mandal, Krauss & Huo, 'Polariton-Mediated Electron Transfer via Cavity Quantum Electrodynamics,' J. Phys. Chem. B 124, 6321-6340 (2020)
Koessler, Mandal, Musser, Krauss & Huo, 'Polariton mediated electron transfer under the collective molecule-cavity coupling regime,' Chem. Sci. 16, 11644-11658 (2025)
Mandal, Taylor, Weight, Koessler, Li & Huo, 'Theoretical Advances in Polariton Chemistry and Molecular Cavity Quantum Electrodynamics,' Chem. Rev. 123, 9786-9879 (2023)
Chen et al., 'Electron transfer in confined electromagnetic fields: a unified Fermi's golden rule rate theory and extension to lossy cavities,' arXiv:2511.04017 / J. Chem. Phys. 164, 024113 (2026)

uncertainties: Dimensional consistency of Eq. (44): the paper writes N_max = 1 + hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2, but for this to be dimensionless |T_01|^2 must carry units of energy (not energy^2). Reconciling with Eq. (26), where (N-1)|T_01|^2 has units of energy^2 and the barrier-less condition Omega_+ = sqrt((N-1)|T_01|^2) = hbar*omega_c gives N-1 = (hbar*omega_c)^2/|T_01|^2, suggests either a units/normalization convention in the paper or a notational compression in Eq. (44). I could not fully resolve whether |T_01|^2 in Eq. (44) implicitly absorbs a factor of hbar*omega_c. The qualitative scaling (N_max grows with the gap/photon energy at resonance and inversely with the squared photon-coupling) is unambiguous; the exact prefactor convention should be checked against the paper's own units before hard-coding in a simulation.

The task's stated form N_max ~ hbar*omega_c * E_AD / |T01|^2 is NOT the literal published expression (which is hbar*omega_c/|T_01|^2). The extra E_AD factor only appears because, at the resonance condition assumed throughout the paper, hbar*omega_c = E_AD; under that tuning the two notations coincide in spirit. A simulation should treat resonance (Delta = hbar*omega_c - E_AD = 0) carefully and not introduce a spurious additional E_AD multiplier.

The follow-up unified-theory paper (arXiv:2511.04017 / J. Chem. Phys. 164, 024113) was identified by search and listing pages but I could not parse its full equations here; its exact rate kernel and lossy-cavity corrections are reported at medium confidence.

I verified the Sharma-Chen equations from the arXiv v1 PDF text extraction; minor equation-number shifts could exist between arXiv v1, v2, and the final JCP version. Equation numbers cited correspond to arXiv:2406.17101 v1.

The Phuc and Wellnitz/Pupillo references named in the prompt were not directly fetched; the collective/dark-state 'large-N problem' physics is corroborated here via Mandal/Huo and Sharma & Chen rather than those specific authors.

webFindings.facet: The scientific controversy and open questions in polaritonic / vibrational-strong-coupling (VSC) chemistry: Ebbesen-group claims that VSC modifies ground-state thermal chemistry, the theoretical "large N" / dark-state difficulty, failed and partial reproductions, and what is currently settled vs unsettled.
webFindings.summary:
Since 2016 the Ebbesen group (Strasbourg) has reported that placing reactants in a Fabry-Perot infrared microcavity and tuning a cavity mode into resonance with a molecular vibration ("vibrational strong coupling", VSC) changes ground-state thermal reaction rates, branching ratios, and enzyme activity WITHOUT any external laser pumping. Flagship claims: a ~5.5x rate SUPPRESSION of alkynylsilane deprotection (Thomas 2016); a switch in Si-C vs Si-O site-selectivity and altered activation enthalpy/entropy in an SN2-type reaction, with the effect appearing ONLY on resonance and scaling with Rabi splitting (Thomas/Ebbesen, Science 2019); and a ~4.5x DECREASE in pepsin catalytic efficiency under VSC of water's O-H stretch (Vergauwe 2019). These are striking because the effect is on the electronic GROUND state in thermal equilibrium and shows a sharp resonance with the cavity-vibration detuning.

The central THEORETICAL problem is the "collective / large N" puzzle. Strong coupling in these experiments is collective: a single cavity mode couples to N ~ 10^6-10^12 molecules in the mode volume. The observable Rabi splitting (which is what is actually measured) scales as Omega_R ∝ g*sqrt(N), but the coupling experienced by any ONE molecule scales as g ∝ 1/sqrt(N) and is utterly negligible (sub-cm^-1) for a single reacting molecule. Because a chemical reaction is a single-molecule event on its own reaction coordinate, it is very hard to see how a per-molecule energy perturbation that vanishes in the thermodynamic limit can change a thermally-activated barrier. Standard transition-state-theory / equilibrium treatments find essentially no effect, and equilibrium partition functions are unchanged when molecules enter the cavity. The two main proposed escapes are (i) DARK STATES: of the N+1 hybrid modes only 2 are polaritons; the other N-1 "dark" modes sit at the bare frequency. Du & Yuen-Zhou (PRL 2022) showed disorder makes dark modes semi-localized over ~2-3 molecules, opening vibrational-cooling channels that can catalyze a reaction (~50% rate change in a model). (ii) NON-EQUILIBRIUM / dynamical-caging effects: Lindoy-Mandal-Reichman and Ke show a resonant collective effect can survive to large N only under non-equilibrium initial conditions, and even then the magnitude is "exceedingly small," far below experiment. No theory yet reproduces the experimentally reported magnitudes AND the sharp resonance AND the correct collective scaling simultaneously.

REPRODUCIBILITY is the other half of the controversy. Independent groups have failed to reproduce several flagship results despite matching Rabi splitting and detuning: Wiesehan & Xiong (JCP 2021) saw NO rate change in p-nitrophenyl-acetate ester hydrolysis (vs a reported ~10x); Imperatore, Asbury & Giebink (JCP 2021) could not reproduce cyanate/cyanate-ester hydrolysis enhancement; a piezo-tunable-cavity study reproduced the Rabi splitting but saw no rate change tuning in/out of resonance; Xiong-group ultrafast CN-radical work saw no change. Michon & Simpkins (JACS 2024) showed that submicron cavity-length non-uniformity creates large UV-vis absorption/rate artifacts, a concrete confound undermining some kinetic extractions. There ARE later positive reports with resonance dependence (e.g. cavity-mediated radiative energy transfer, Nature Chem 2024), so the picture is mixed rather than uniformly negative.

SETTLED: VSC/Rabi splitting itself is real and routinely measured; equilibrium thermodynamic quantities are unchanged by the cavity; single-molecule coupling is negligible; cavity-uniformity/measurement artifacts are real and must be controlled. UNSETTLED/CONTESTED: whether VSC genuinely modifies thermal ground-state rates at all in most systems, the magnitude and reproducibility, the microscopic mechanism, and why a sharp resonance appears and how any effect survives collective 1/sqrt(N) dilution. Reviews and perspectives (Simpkins/Dunkelberger/Owrutsky; the Chemical Reviews 2023 polaritonic-chemistry thematic issue) explicitly flag irreproducibility and the absence of an accepted theory as the field's central open problems.

key_equations.name: Collective light-matter (Tavis-Cummings-type) Hamiltonian for VSC
key_equations.latex: H = \hbar\omega_c a_0^\dagger a_0 + \hbar\sum_{i=1}^{N}\omega_i a_i^\dagger a_i + \hbar\sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i\,(a_i^\dagger a_0 + \mathrm{h.c.})

variables.meaning: number of molecules coupled to the mode

key_equations.where: Du & Yuen-Zhou, Phys. Rev. Lett. 128, 096001 (2022), arXiv:2104.07214 (exact form from the PDF text)
key_equations.context: Collective strong coupling of N localized vibrations to one cavity mode; yields 2 polaritons + (N-1) dark modes. Assumes single-excitation/harmonic regime and identical coupling.

key_equations.name: Per-molecule coupling scales as 1/sqrt(N) (constant-density Hamiltonian)

variables.meaning: collective coupling parameter (fixed at constant molecular density)
number of molecules; per-molecule prefactor is g/sqrt(N)
transition dipole operator of molecule i

key_equations.where: Lindoy, Mandal & Reichman, Nanophotonics 13, 2617 (2024), doi:10.1515/nanoph-2024-0026 (Eq. 9), arXiv:2403.03951
key_equations.context: At fixed density the individual molecule-cavity coupling ~ g/sqrt(N) -> 0 as N->infinity, so any single reacting molecule feels a vanishing perturbation. This is the quantitative heart of the 'large N problem.'

key_equations.name: Collective Rabi splitting scales as sqrt(N)

variables.meaning: vacuum Rabi splitting (the measured polariton energy gap)
variables.typical: ~20-200 cm^-1 for VSC; up to ~10% of vibration freq

key_equations.where: Lindoy, Mandal & Reichman, Nanophotonics 13, 2617 (2024); also general VSC literature (e.g. Galego et al.)
key_equations.context: The experimentally observed quantity (Rabi splitting) is collectively enhanced by sqrt(N), but this enhancement is shared among ~N molecules; it does NOT mean any single molecule is strongly perturbed. The tension between Omega_R ∝ sqrt(N) (large, measurable) and per-molecule coupling ∝ 1/sqrt(N) (negligible) is the crux of the controversy.

key_equations.name: Polariton frequencies and dark-mode count under collective VSC

variables.meaning: number of optically dark modes parked at ~\omega_v

key_equations.where: Du & Yuen-Zhou, Phys. Rev. Lett. 128, 096001 (2022), arXiv:2104.07214
key_equations.context: Only 2 of N+1 hybrid modes are bright polaritons; the remaining N-1 dark modes stay near the bare frequency. Entropically, reactivity changes attributed only to the 2 polaritons look 'entropically unlikely' (hence the dark-state and disorder/semilocalization arguments).

facts.claim: FOUNDATIONAL CLAIM: Thomas et al. (2016) reported that vibrational strong coupling to the cavity vacuum field SUPPRESSED the rate of ground-state deprotection of an alkynylsilane (1-phenyl-2-trimethylsilylacetylene) by a factor of up to 5.5 when the Si-C stretch was coupled, with significant changes in activation enthalpy and entropy interpreted as a switch from an associative to a dissociative transition state.
facts.source: Thomas et al., 'Ground-State Chemical Reactivity under Vibrational Coupling to the Vacuum Electromagnetic Field,' Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 55, 11462 (2016), doi:10.1002/anie.201605504 (PMID 27529831)

facts.claim: FOUNDATIONAL CLAIM (resonance + selectivity): Thomas/Ebbesen et al. (Science 2019) reported that VSC can 'tilt' a reactivity landscape, changing the branching between two silyl bond-cleavage sites (Si-C vs Si-O) and substantially altering activation entropy; the effect appeared in the dark (no pumping) and depended on which vibrational mode was coupled.
facts.source: Thomas et al., 'Tilting a ground-state reactivity landscape by vibrational strong coupling,' Science 363, 615 (2019), doi:10.1126/science.aau7742

facts.claim: FOUNDATIONAL CLAIM (enzymes): Vergauwe et al. (2019) reported a ~4.5-fold DECREASE in pepsin's apparent second-order rate constant kcat/Km when the O-H stretch of water was strongly coupled, while coupling the water bending mode produced no measurable effect.
facts.source: Vergauwe et al., 'Modification of Enzyme Activity by Vibrational Strong Coupling of Water,' Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58, 15324 (2019), doi:10.1002/anie.201908876 (PMID 31449707)

facts.claim: The reported effects are striking specifically because they are on the electronic GROUND state under thermal equilibrium with NO external optical pumping, and because they show a sharp RESONANCE: the rate change is significant only when a cavity mode is tuned into resonance with a vibrational mode, and tracks the Rabi splitting.
facts.source: Synthesis of Thomas et al. Science 363, 615 (2019), doi:10.1126/science.aau7742; and field reviews noting resonance behavior is hard to reproduce theoretically

facts.claim: THE LARGE-N PROBLEM: VSC in these experiments is COLLECTIVE — one cavity mode couples to N~10^6-10^12 molecules. The measured Rabi splitting scales as Omega_R ∝ g*sqrt(N) (large, observable) but the coupling felt by any single molecule scales as g ∝ 1/sqrt(N) (negligible). Since a chemical reaction is a single-molecule event, it is theoretically very hard to explain how a per-molecule perturbation that vanishes in the thermodynamic limit can modify a thermally-activated barrier.
facts.source: Lindoy, Mandal & Reichman, 'Investigating the collective nature of cavity-modified chemical kinetics under vibrational strong coupling,' Nanophotonics 13, 2617 (2024), doi:10.1515/nanoph-2024-0026, arXiv:2403.03951

facts.claim: Lindoy-Mandal-Reichman find that under EQUILIBRIUM (correlated) initial conditions the cavity rate modification VANISHES in the mean-field N→∞ limit; substantial modification appears only in the few-molecule limit. Under NON-EQUILIBRIUM (uncorrelated) initial conditions a collective resonant effect can persist to large N, but its magnitude is 'exceedingly small' — insufficient to explain the experimental observations, which they say point to features not in current models.
facts.source: Lindoy, Mandal & Reichman, Nanophotonics 13, 2617 (2024), doi:10.1515/nanoph-2024-0026, arXiv:2403.03951

facts.claim: DARK-STATE ARGUMENT: Collective VSC of N molecules produces only 2 polaritons and N-1 'dark' modes parked at the bare vibrational frequency; ascribing thermal-reactivity changes only to the 2 polaritons is 'entropically unlikely.' Du & Yuen-Zhou (PRL 2022) argue that energetic DISORDER makes the dark modes semi-localized over ~2-3 molecules, opening enhanced vibrational-cooling channels that can catalyze a reaction; they compute a ~50% increase in a model electron-transfer rate, valid when dark-mode homogeneous linewidths are smaller than their energy spacings.
facts.source: Du & Yuen-Zhou, 'Catalysis by Dark States in Vibropolaritonic Chemistry,' Phys. Rev. Lett. 128, 096001 (2022), doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.128.096001, arXiv:2104.07214

facts.claim: Standard transition-state theory and equilibrium statistical mechanics predict essentially NO ground-state rate change from collective VSC: equilibrium populations/partition functions are unchanged when molecules are placed in the cavity, regardless of cavity frequency. Resonant rate modifications, where found theoretically, arise from NON-EQUILIBRIUM dynamical mechanisms rather than equilibrium property changes.
facts.source: Ke, 'Non-equilibrium origin of cavity-induced resonant modifications of chemical reactivities,' (2025), arXiv:2503.12568

facts.claim: FAILED REPRODUCTION: Wiesehan & Xiong (2021) attempted to reproduce reported VSC catalysis of p-nitrophenyl acetate ester hydrolysis. Matching the same light-matter coupling strength and detuning, they did NOT observe the reported ~10-fold rate increase and saw no obvious detuning dependence, concluding cavity catalysis is sensitive to experimental details beyond the mere onset of VSC.
facts.source: Wiesehan & Xiong, 'Negligible rate enhancement from reported cooperative vibrational strong coupling catalysis,' J. Chem. Phys. 155, 241103 (2021), doi:10.1063/5.0077549 (PMID 34972384)

facts.claim: FAILED/PARTIAL REPRODUCTION: Imperatore, Asbury & Giebink (2021) reported on the reproducibility of cavity-enhanced reaction rates in the VSC regime, finding that previously reported enhancements were difficult to reproduce and that subtle experimental factors strongly affect outcomes.
facts.source: Imperatore, Asbury & Giebink, 'Reproducibility of cavity-enhanced chemical reaction rates in the vibrational strong coupling regime,' J. Chem. Phys. 154, 191103 (2021), doi:10.1063/5.0046307

facts.claim: FAILED REPRODUCTION: A piezo-tunable microcavity study aimed at reproducing Hiura et al.'s reported enhancement of cyanate-ion hydrolysis (via collective O-H stretch coupling of water) reproduced the reported vacuum Rabi splitting but observed NO change in reaction rate as the cavity was tuned in and out of the strong-coupling regime.
facts.source: Reported in the VSC reproducibility literature; see escholarship 'Reproducibility of Effects of Vibrational Strong Coupling on Chemical Reaction Rates' (UC eScholarship qt9pc1m1wx) and field reviews

facts.claim: NULL RESULT (ultrafast): Transient-absorption study of CN radicals reacting with cyclohexane/chloroform under VSC of a C-H stretch found reaction rates UNCHANGED across extracavity, on-resonance, and off-resonance conditions, independent of coupling strength.
facts.source: 'Exploring the impact of vibrational cavity coupling strength on ultrafast CN + c-C6H12 reaction dynamics,' arXiv:2310.19133; PMC11635944

facts.claim: METHODOLOGICAL CONFOUND (now considered an important caveat): Michon & Simpkins (JACS 2024) showed that submicron cavity-length NON-UNIFORMITY produces a UV-vis measurement artifact distorting apparent absorption peak position/amplitude and hence extracted reaction-rate constants; they introduced an effective molar absorption coefficient correction and demonstrated 2D spatial inhomogeneity of apparent rate constants. This implies some prior kinetic extractions may be artifacts of cavity inhomogeneity.
facts.source: Michon & Simpkins, 'Impact of Cavity Length Non-uniformity on Reaction Rate Extraction in Strong Coupling Experiments,' J. Am. Chem. Soc. 146, 30596 (2024), doi:10.1021/jacs.4c12269

facts.claim: NOT UNIFORMLY NEGATIVE: There are independent and later positive reports with resonance dependence, e.g. a 2024 Nature Chemistry study reporting that vibrational weak AND strong coupling modify a chemical reaction via a cavity-mediated radiative-energy-transfer mechanism, indicating the experimental picture is mixed rather than fully debunked.
facts.source: 'Vibrational weak and strong coupling modify a chemical reaction via cavity-mediated radiative energy transfer,' Nat. Chem. (2024), doi:10.1038/s41557-024-01723-6 (PMID 39820806)

facts.claim: PERSPECTIVE / FIELD SELF-ASSESSMENT: Simpkins, Dunkelberger & Owrutsky frame VSC chemistry as promising but beset by a lack of mechanistic understanding, potentially misleading spectroscopic interpretations, and a shortage of corroborating evidence; key open questions include WHICH reactions can be modified and to what extent.
facts.source: Simpkins, Dunkelberger & Owrutsky, 'Mode-Specific Chemistry through Vibrational Strong Coupling (or A Wish Come True),' J. Phys. Chem. C 125, 19081 (2021), doi:10.1021/acs.jpcc.1c05362

facts.claim: FIELD CONSENSUS ON STATUS: The Chemical Reviews 2023 thematic issue on polaritonic chemistry and accompanying reviews acknowledge openly that reports of altered chemistry under VSC have met substantial skepticism due to irreproducible results and the lack of an accepted theoretical framework, and that the resonance behavior in particular has been hard to reproduce theoretically.
facts.source: Introduction: Polaritonic Chemistry, Chem. Rev. 123, 12037 (2023), doi:10.1021/acs.chemrev.3c00637; and 'Molecular Chemistry in Cavity Strong Coupling,' Chem. Rev. (2023), doi:10.1021/acs.chemrev.2c00748

facts.claim: EARLY THEORY ATTEMPT: Galego, Climent, Garcia-Vidal & Feist (PRX 2019) proposed cavity Casimir-Polder forces could modify single-molecule ground-state reactivity, with the key parameter being the permanent dipole and its change along the reaction coordinate. However, this single-molecule/ultrastrong-coupling mechanism does not straightforwardly carry over to the collective, weakly-per-molecule-coupled regime of the experiments — illustrating the theory-experiment gap.
facts.source: Galego, Climent, Garcia-Vidal & Feist, 'Cavity Casimir-Polder Forces and Their Effects in Ground-State Chemical Reactivity,' Phys. Rev. X 9, 021057 (2019), doi:10.1103/PhysRevX.9.021057, arXiv:1807.10846

facts.claim: SETTLED POINTS: (i) The Rabi splitting / formation of vibrational polaritons is real and routinely measured in IR; (ii) equilibrium thermodynamic quantities are unchanged by the cavity; (iii) per-single-molecule coupling is negligible; (iv) cavity-uniformity and spectroscopic-extraction artifacts are real and must be controlled. UNSETTLED: whether thermal ground-state rates are genuinely modified in most systems, the magnitude/reproducibility, the microscopic mechanism, and the origin of the sharp resonance and how any effect survives 1/sqrt(N) collective dilution.
facts.source: Synthesis of Lindoy et al. Nanophotonics 13, 2617 (2024); Du & Yuen-Zhou PRL 128, 096001 (2022); Michon & Simpkins JACS 146, 30596 (2024); Simpkins et al. JPCC 125, 19081 (2021)
